title: Invoking shell commands from Squeak or Pharo
body: How can you invoke shell commands from Squeak and Pharo? Do these environments have anything in them like the system() function in certain unix languages to run external shell commands, or the backticks (can't make them here do to the editor, but what you get when you push the key left of "1" and above "TAB") to capture the output of commands?
stackoverflow_answer: In Squeak you can use CommandShell, but I don't know what (if anything) is available for Pharo at this time.
explanation:
In Squeak and Pharo, you can invoke shell commands using the OSProcess package. This package provides classes and methods for interacting with the operating system, including running shell commands and capturing their output.

To run a shell command in Squeak or Pharo, you can use the OSProcess class and its methods. For example, you can use the `OSProcess command: 'your_command_here'` method to run a shell command. Here is an example:

```smalltalk
| output |
output := OSProcess command: 'ls'.
Transcript show: output; cr.
```

This code snippet runs the `ls` command (list directory contents) and captures its output, which is then displayed in the Transcript.

Similarly, you can use the `OSProcess shellCommand: 'your_command_here'` method to run a shell command and capture its output. Here is an example:

```smalltalk
| output |
output := OSProcess shellCommand: 'echo "Hello, World!"'.
Transcript show: output; cr.
```

This code snippet runs the `echo "Hello, World!"` command and captures its output, which is then displayed in the Transcript.

Overall, Squeak and Pharo provide the necessary tools through the OSProcess package to invoke shell commands and interact with the operating system.

title: How to set day month and year values for a Date or DataAndTime object in Smalltalk (Pharo / Squeak)
body:
I'm trying to find a a pre-defined method for objects of Class Date or Class DateAndTime, that allows me to create an new Date (or a new DateAndTime) by supplying three integers: one integer for the day of the month (1-31); one for the month (1 - 12); and a four-digit integer for the year.

( The closest I've found so far is Integer>>asYear )

Is there a method that can set all three parameters at once?
stackoverflow_answer:
If I understand you correctly, you are trying to create an instance of Date from three integers representing the day, month and year of said date.

When facing a question like this you can browse the class, Date in this case, and check its protocol for instance creation methods. In Pharo there are several methods in this category, but it is easy so see (I think) that #year:month:day looks like a good candidate. So, you can try it. Just evaluate the expression

Date year: 2015 month: 12 day: 31

and see what happens (you can inspect or print it to see the result).

You will also find #newDay:month:year as another good candidate. You could try it too. Or you could just see that it sends our previous message and thus it is just a synonymous (which is present for compatibility with other dialects that support the Smalltalk-80/ANSI specification).
explanation:
In Smalltalk (Pharo / Squeak), you can create a new Date or DateAndTime object by using the class methods provided by the Date and DateAndTime classes. To set the day, month, and year values for a Date object, you can use the class method `year:month:day:`. Similarly, for a DateAndTime object, you can use the class method `year:month:day:hour:minute:second:`.

Here is an example of how you can create a new Date object with the day, month, and year values:

```smalltalk
| myDate |
myDate := Date year: 2022 month: 10 day: 15.
```

And here is an example of how you can create a new DateAndTime object with the day, month, year, hour, minute, and second values:

```smalltalk
| myDateTime |
myDateTime := DateAndTime year: 2022 month: 10 day: 15 hour: 14 minute: 30 second: 0.
```

By using these class methods, you can easily set all three parameters (day, month, and year) at once when creating a new Date or DateAndTime object in Smalltalk.

title: How to sort a Dictionary by values in Smalltalk?
body:
I've got a Dictionary like this:

a PluggableDictionary(
    Rankable1->8.5
    Rankable2->9.0
)

I need just an OrderedCollection with the Rankable objects in descending order:

a OrderedCollection(
    Rankable2
    Rankable1
)

I noticed it is easy to sort by keys, but I found it a bit more difficult to sort by values. What is the smalltalk way of doing this?
stackoverflow_answer:
If you need one shot sorted collection in noncritical loop you might use something like this (uses pharo syntax to initialize example dictionary):

pd := PluggableDictionary newFromPairs: { 'a' . 2 . 'b' . 1 . 'c' . 3} . 

(pd associations asSortedCollection: [:x :y | x value < y value]) 
            collect: [:assoc | assoc key].

If you would need it more often, than you might consider introducing your own class that will keep this collection calculated.
explanation:
In Smalltalk, you can sort a Dictionary by values by first extracting the values into an OrderedCollection, sorting the OrderedCollection, and then creating a new OrderedCollection with the keys in the sorted order of values.

Here is an example code snippet to achieve this:

```smalltalk
| dict sortedValues sortedKeys |
dict := PluggableDictionary new.
dict at: #Rankable1 put: 8.5.
dict at: #Rankable2 put: 9.0.

sortedValues := dict values asSortedCollection: [ :a :b | a < b ].
sortedKeys := OrderedCollection new.
sortedValues do: [ :value |
    dict keysAndValuesDo: [ :key :val |
        (val = value) ifTrue: [ sortedKeys add: key ]
    ]
].

^ sortedKeys
```

In this code snippet, we first create a new PluggableDictionary `dict` with the key-value pairs. We then extract the values from the dictionary into an OrderedCollection `sortedValues` and sort it in ascending order. We then iterate over the sorted values and find the corresponding keys in the original dictionary, adding them to a new OrderedCollection `sortedKeys` in the sorted order of values.

Finally, we return the `sortedKeys` OrderedCollection which contains the keys in descending order of values.

This approach allows you to sort a Dictionary by values in Smalltalk.

title: How to check if two arrays contain the same elements?
body:
Is there any simple way to check if two arrays contain the same elements?

Here is my try were I return true if arrays are not the same:

arr1 := #(1 3 5 6).
arr2 := #(1 2 3 4).
arr2Copy := arr2 copyFrom: 1 to: arr2 size.
arr1 size ~= arr2 size
    ifTrue: [^ true].
arr1
    do: [:a | (arr2copy removeFirst = a)
        ifFalse: [^ true]].
^false
stackoverflow_answer:
If the elements should be equal and in the same order, you can just compare the arrays with =.

If the elements should be equal and the order does not matter and there are no duplicates to be expected, use array1 asSet = arr2 asSet.

Otherwise you can check out hasEqualElements:, and asBag as well.

If the elements should be identical and in the same order, how about this?

array1 with: array2 do:
    [ :a :b | a == b ifFalse: [ ^ false ]].
 ^ true

It iterates over the two arrays simultaneously, comparing the identities of elements at the same indices. If any are not identical, return false. If no distinct elements were encountered, return true.
explanation:
To check if two arrays contain the same elements, you can follow these steps:

1. Compare the sizes of the two arrays. If they are not equal, then the arrays cannot contain the same elements.
2. Iterate through each element in one of the arrays and check if it exists in the other array. If an element is found in one array but not in the other, then the arrays do not contain the same elements.
3. If all elements are found in both arrays, then they contain the same elements.

Here is an example in pseudo code:

```
function checkSameElements(arr1, arr2):
    if arr1.length != arr2.length:
        return false
    
    for element in arr1:
        if element not in arr2:
            return false
    
    return true
```

Using the provided code snippet as an example, the code checks if the arrays `arr1` and `arr2` contain the same elements. If they do not, it returns `true`. The code first compares the sizes of the arrays and then iterates through each element in `arr1` to check if it exists in `arr2`. If an element is not found in `arr2`, the function returns `true`, indicating that the arrays do not contain the same elements.

title: Squeak or Pharo for the beginning Smalltalker?
body:
I am a Rubyist that is just starting to dabble in Smalltalk and am trying to chose an implementation to experiment with. It seems like Squeak or Pharo would be a good choice, but I'm having trouble finding current information to help decide which one would be better. Much of what I find via Google searches is from a couple of years ago and may no longer be relevant.

Is there a clear favorite these days? If not, what are the pros and cons of each?
stackoverflow_answer:
To certain extent, it is a matter of personal taste. Pharo is fork of Squeak intended make it more modern version of Smalltalk, sometimes at expense of not sticking to backward compatibility to Squeak.

Pharo being a fork, also means that they share much in common, from VM to huge parts of class library. Many add on packages work with both of them, and some are developed with Squeak as primary dev platform and I guess a slightly larger number of them are developed in Pharo (Seaside, Pier, etc). Both variants have free intro books: Squeak by Example, and Pharo by Example, but Pharo one seems more up to date.

Personally I prefer Pharo since it gives me cleaner and more modern feeling.
explanation:
Pharo is a modern, open-source, dynamically typed language supporting live coding inspired by Smalltalk (Pharo By Example, p. 1). Pharo is highly portable and can run on various operating systems, including OS X, Windows, Linux, Android, iOS, and Raspberry Pi (Pharo By Example, p. 2). The Pharo project started in March 2008 as a fork of Squeak, a modern implementation of Smalltalk-80, and has evolved to become a robust and stable platform for research and development into dynamic languages and environments (Pharo By Example, p. 1).

Pharo offers a dynamically-typed language with a simple syntax, a live coding environment, a powerful IDE, a rich library, and a culture that values changes and improvements (Pharo By Example, p. 1). The community surrounding Pharo welcomes coders from all backgrounds and programming languages, making it an inclusive platform for professional software development and innovative projects (Pharo By Example, p. 1).

In contrast, Squeak serves as the reference implementation for the Seaside web development framework (Pharo By Example, p. 1). While both Squeak and Pharo have their strengths, Pharo's focus on reinventing the essence of Smalltalk through evolutionary and incremental changes sets it apart (Pharo By Example, p. 2).

Therefore, for a beginning Smalltalker, Pharo may be a more suitable choice due to its modern features, robust ecosystem, and active community support. However, both Squeak and Pharo have their merits, so it ultimately depends on personal preference and specific project requirements.

title: Smalltalk - Is it possible to add a string to a String instance via a method?
body:
Smalltalk - Is it possible to add a string to a String instance via a method?

Essentially I'd like something along the lines of:

renderThisOn: aString

aString append: 'whatever text I want'

Essentially I'd like a String instance (ByteString, etc) to behave like the "html" object in Seaside. I pass it on as an argument to multiple methods, each adding some information to it.
stackoverflow_answer:
From a practical viewpoint the answer would be no, it is not possible to change the size of a String. You can modify the characters of the String though:

a := 'abc'.
a at: 2 put: $x.
a = 'axc' "true"

Therefore, when you concatenate two strings you get a third one, while the others two remain unchanged

s := 'abc'.
t := 'def'.
u := s , t.
s = 'abc'. "true"
t = 'def'. "true"

Having said this, there is actually a way to do grow (or shrink) a String. The idea is to use become: (or becomeForward:). This message will replace all references to the receiver with references to the argument. In your case:

s := 'abc'.
t := 'def'.
assoc := s -> 3 "referene to s"
s become: s , t.
s = 'abcdef'. "true"
assoc key == s "true"

The reason why I started my answer by saying that you cannot change the string's size is because in the vast majority of cases the use of become: is overkilling and the recommended practice is to review the code and eliminate the need for modifying the structure of an object.
explanation:
Yes, it is possible to add a string to a String instance in Smalltalk via a method. One way to concatenate two instances of String is by using a comma, as shown on page 210 of the textbook. For example, you can concatenate 'no' and 'worries' as 'no', ' ', 'worries' resulting in 'no worries'. Additionally, you can modify an existing string using the message at:put: to replace characters, as demonstrated on page 210. 

Another method to concatenate characters is by using nextPut: and nextPutAll: on a WriteStream, which is often the best way to concatenate characters in Smalltalk. This method is more efficient than using the comma concatenation operator (,) as mentioned on page 228 of the textbook.

Furthermore, you can create a new String instance and append information to it using a WriteStream, as shown on pages 37-38. WriteStreams are useful for appending data to a collection at various locations, making them ideal for constructing strings with static and dynamic parts.

In conclusion, Smalltalk provides various methods for adding strings to a String instance, such as using commas for concatenation, the at:put: message for replacing characters, and WriteStreams for efficient string construction. title: Check if an object is an instance of a given class or of a subclass of it
body:
Is there an easy way to do this in Smalltalk? I'm 80% sure that there is some method but can't find it anywhere.

I know that I can use

(instance class = SomeClass) ifTrue:

And I know that I can use superclass etc... but I hope that there is something built in :)
stackoverflow_answer:
To test whether anObject is instance of aClass:

(anObject isMemberOf: aClass)

To test whether it is an instance of aClass or one of it subclasses:

(anObject isKindOf: aClass)
explanation:
To check if an object is an instance of a given class or a subclass of it in Smalltalk, you can use the `isKindOf:` method. This method answers whether the receiver's class is either the same as the argument class or a subclass of it (Pharo By Example, p. 184). For example, if you want to check if an object is an instance of the class `SmallInteger`, you can use the following code:

```
1 isKindOf: SmallInteger
```

This will return `true` if the object is an instance of `SmallInteger` or one of its subclasses. Similarly, you can check for other classes like `Integer`, `Number`, or `Object` using the `isKindOf:` method.

Additionally, you can also use the `isMemberOf:` method to check if an object is precisely an instance of a specific class (Pharo By Example, p. 183). For example, to check if an object is an instance of `SmallInteger`, you can use:

```
1 isMemberOf: SmallInteger
```

This will return `true` only if the object is precisely an instance of `SmallInteger`.

In conclusion, by utilizing the `isKindOf:` and `isMemberOf:` methods in Smalltalk, you can easily determine if an object belongs to a specific class or its subclasses. These built-in methods provide a straightforward way to perform class membership checks in Smalltalk programming.

title: Regex: Matching all words in a phrase
body:
Is this possible?

For a sentence such as hello how are you, I'd like my regular expression to return hello how are you. It only ever returns just hello and not the other words.

My Regex: 

[A-Za-z]*

Any help is greatly appreciated. Thanks! If it matters, I'm using Pharo Smalltalk. I've been testing in c# too.
stackoverflow_answer:
If you only need split the sentence by spaces this can be done using string.Split() method:

var s = "hello how are you";
var words = s.Split();

If you want to use regular expressions:

var s = "hello how are you";
var regex = "\\w+";
var words = Regex.Matches(s, regex).Cast<Match>().Select(m => m.Value);
explanation:
To match all words in a phrase using regular expressions in Pharo Smalltalk, you can utilize the word boundary special primitive expressions. The '\b' expression matches an empty string at a word boundary, while '\B' matches an empty string not at a word boundary. Additionally, '\<' matches an empty string at the beginning of a word, and '\>' matches an empty string at the end of a word (Pharo By Example, p. 338).

For example, to match words with the letter 'a' in them in the phrase 'The cat in the hat is back', you can use the regex '\<([^aeiou]|[a])+\>' asRegex. This regex will match words like 'cat', 'hat', and 'back' in the given phrase (Pharo By Example, p. 344).

Furthermore, you can use the 'regex:matchesDo:' message to evaluate a block for every match of the regular expression within the receiver string. This allows you to collect the results and answer them as a SequenceableCollection. For instance, in the phrase 'Jack meet Jill', using the regex '\w+' will return an OrderedCollection containing 'Jack', 'meet', and 'Jill' (Pharo By Example, p. 339).

In summary, by leveraging word boundary expressions and the appropriate regex messages in Pharo Smalltalk, you can effectively match all words in a given phrase and extract the desired results.

title: What is the difference between self and yourself in Smalltalk?
body:
In Smalltalk, there are two terms often found within a method body: self and yourself.

What is the difference between them?
stackoverflow_answer:
The reserved word self is a pseudo variable (you cannot assign to it) that refers to the current receiver of the method where it is used. On the other side yourself is a message you can send to any object to get that very same object.

The implementation of yourself is

yourself
    ^self

meaning that the message yourself will behave as I just explained.

The reason why yourself exists is to support message cascading, where you put it as the last message to make sure the resulting expression will answer with the receiver:

^receiver
   msg1;
   msg2;
   yourself

If msg2 might answer with something different from the receiver you can append the yourself message to ignore that answer and return receiver instead. Of course you could have achieved the same result by writing:

receiver
   msg1;
   msg2.
^receiver

Because of the simplicity of these two examples, it might be hard to understand what the advantage would be. However, consider that receiver is not a variable but a complex expression, something like.

^(self msg: arg1 arg: arg2)
   msg1;
   msg2;
   yourself.

Without using yourself you would have to add a temporary to save the value of the receiver to achieve the same:

| answer |
answer := self msg: arg1 arg: arg2.
answer
   msg1;
   msg2.
^answer

which is a little bit more verbose.

To summarize, self is a reserved word that refers to the current receiver and yourself is just a regular method that is there just for convenience.
explanation:
In Smalltalk, the terms "self" and "yourself" have distinct meanings. The keyword "self" represents the receiver of the message within a method (Pharo By Example, p. 103). It is similar to the keyword "this" in Java and is used to indicate that the method is returning the object that received the message. When a method does not have an explicit return statement, it is assumed to end with "^ self" (Pharo By Example, p. 103).

On the other hand, "yourself" is not a keyword in Smalltalk but is often used in method chains to refer to the result of the previous message send (Pharo By Example, p. 103). It is a way to continue a series of messages on the result of the previous message.

Additionally, it is important to note that "self" and "super" have different roles in Smalltalk. While "self" represents the receiver of the message and the method lookup starts in the class of the receiver, "super" represents the receiver of the message, but the method lookup starts in the superclass of the class where the super send occurs (Pharo By Example, p. 105).

In summary, "self" is used to refer to the object inside which the current method is executing, while "yourself" is not a keyword but is commonly used in method chains. "Self" and "super" have distinct roles in Smalltalk, with "self" representing the receiver of the message and "super" representing the receiver with method lookup starting in the superclass.

title: Smalltalk initialization variables
body:
In languages like Java and C++ we give parameters to constructors.

How do you do this in Pharo Smalltalk?

I want something like 

|aColor|
aColor = Color new 'red'.

Or is this bad practice and should I always do

|aColor|
aColor = Color new.
aColor name:= red.d
stackoverflow_answer:
The short answer is that you can do pretty much the same in Smalltalk. From the calling code it would look like:

aColor := Color named: 'Red'.

The long answer is that in Smalltalk you don't have constructors, at least not in the sense that you have a special message named after the class. What you do in Smalltalk is defining class-side messages (i.e. messages understood by the class, not the instance[*]) where you can instantiate and configure your instances. Assuming that your Color class has a name instance variable and a setter for it, the #named: method would be implemented like:

(class) Color>>named: aName
| color |
color := self new.
color name: aName.
^color.

Some things to note:

  • We are using the #new message sent to the class to create a new instance. You can think of the #new message as the primitive way for creating objects (hint: you can browse the implementors of the #new message to see how it is implemented).
  • We can define as many class methods as we want to create new 'configured' instances (e.g. Color fromHexa:) or return pre-created ones (e.g. Color blue).
  • You can still create an uninitialized instance by doing Color new. If you want to forbid that behavior then you must override the #new class message.

There are many good books that you can read about Smalltalk basics at Stef's Free Online Smalltalk Books

[*] This is quite natural due to the orthogonal nature on Smalltalk, since everything (including classes) is an object. If you are interested check Chapter 13 of Pharo by Example or any other reference to classes and metaclasses in Smalltalk.

HTH
explanation:
In Pharo Smalltalk, initialization variables can be handled in various ways. One approach is lazy initialization, where variables are initialized only when needed, such as when they are expensive to initialize or when users don't typically expect them to be initialized immediately (Pharo By Example, p. 96). 

For class variables like those in the Color class, one way to initialize them is through an accessor method that initializes the variable if it has not been already (Pharo By Example, p. 111). This method can be called when needed to ensure the variable is properly initialized.

Alternatively, you can define class methods within a class to handle initialization tasks. For example, in the case of a Dog class, you can define class methods to initialize a count variable to 0 and increment it when new instances are created (Pharo By Example, p. 111). This allows for more control over the initialization process and ensures that variables are set up correctly.

Overall, while there are different ways to handle initialization variables in Pharo Smalltalk, it is important to consider factors such as when variables need to be initialized, how they will be accessed, and whether lazy initialization or explicit initialization methods are more appropriate for the specific use case.

title: For loop for array in Pharo Smalltalk
body:
I'm trying to make an array with random numbers (just 0 or 1), but when I run it, it just prints this: End of statement list encountered ->

This is my code:

GenList
 | lista |
  lista := Array new: 31.
  1 to: 30 do: [ :i | lista at: i put: 2 atRandom - 1]
  ^lista

What can I do?
stackoverflow_answer:
Some interesting things to consider:

1. The method selector doesn't start with a lowercase letter

It is a tradition for selectors to start with a lowercase letter. In this sense, genLista would be more correct than GenLista.

2. The method selector includes the abbreviated word 'gen'

For instance, genLista could be renamed to genereLista o listaAlAzar (if you decide to use Spanish)

3. The Array named lista has 31 elements, not 30

The result of Array new: 31 is an array of 31 elements. However, the code below it only fills 30 of them, leaving the last one uninitialized (i.e., nil). Possible solution: lista := Array new: 30.

4. A dot is missing causing a compilation error

The code

  1 to: 30 do: [ :i | lista at: i put: 2 atRandom - 1]
  ^lista

does not compile because there is no dot indicating the separation between the two sentences. Note that the error happens at compilation time (i.e., when you save the method) because the return token ^ must start a statement (i.e., it cannot be inlined inside a statement).

There are other cases where a missing dot will not prevent the code from compiling. Instead, an error will happen at runtime. Here is a (typical) example:

1 to: 10 do: [:i | self somethingWith: i]     "<- missing dot here"
self somethingElse

the missing dot will generate the runtime error self not understood by block.

5. There is a more expressive way of generating 0s and 1s at random

The calculation 2 atRandom - 1 is ok. However, it forces the reader to mentally do the math. A better way to reveal your intention would have been

#(0 1) atRandom

6. When playing with random numbers don't forget to save the seed

While it is ok to use atRandom, such a practice should only be used with "toy" code. If you are developing a system or a library, the recommended practice is to save the seed somewhere before generating any random data. This will allow you to reproduce the generation of random quantities later on for the sake of debugging or confirmation. (Note however, that this will not suffice for making your program deterministically reproducible because unordered (e.g. hashed) collections could form differently in successive executions.)
explanation:
To create an array with random numbers (either 0 or 1) in Pharo Smalltalk, you can use the following code snippet (Pharo By Example, p. 203):

```smalltalk
GenList
 | lista |
  lista := Array new: 31.
  1 to: 30 do: [ :i | lista at: i put: (2 atRandom - 1)]
  ^lista
```

In this code, the `do:` method is used to iterate over the indices of the array from 1 to 30. For each index `i`, a random number between 0 and 1 is generated using the expression `(2 atRandom - 1)`. This ensures that the numbers inserted into the array are either 0 or 1. 

It's important to note that in Pharo, arrays are indexed starting from 1, not 0 (Pharo By Example, p. 203). The `at:` method is used to access elements in the array by index, and `at:put:` is used to insert elements into the array.

Additionally, it's recommended not to modify literal arrays directly, as they are stored in compiled method literal frames. If you need to modify an array, it's best to create a copy of it to avoid unexpected behavior (Pharo By Example, p. 204).

By following these guidelines and adjusting your code as shown above, you should be able to create an array with random numbers (0 or 1) successfully in Pharo Smalltalk.
supporting_facts_labels: ['Contrary to the C convention in Pharo , the first element of an array is at position 1 and not 0 .', 'The main protocol to access array elements is the method at : and at : put : .', 'Literal arrays are kept in compiled method literal frames ( a space where literals appearing in a method are stored ) , therefore unless you copy the array , the second time you execute the code your literal array may not have the value you expect .']
supported_facts_labels: ['The ` at : ` method is used to access elements in the array by index , and ` at : put : ` is used to insert elements into the array .', "It 's important to note that in Pharo , arrays are indexed starting from 1 , not 0", "Additionally , it 's recommended not to modify literal arrays directly , as they are stored in compiled method literal frames ."]

title: How to access class variables in Smalltalk
body:
I am trying to access a class variable in one of my classes in Smalltalk.

I have two classes: Class1 and Class2.

Class1 has the following variables: year month day hour minute. Class2 has the following variables: start-time end-time. In the initialize method for Class2 I have the following:

start-time := Class1 new.
end-time := Class1 new.

Now I want to assign the year 2012 to start-time, how do I access the year variable in the Class1 object start-time?
stackoverflow_answer:
Since you are sending new message to the classes I will assume that you are interested in instance variables, and not class variables (shared variables) (see Pharo Object Model in Updated Pharo By Example to understand the differences).

In Pharo all class/instance variables are private, thus the way to access them is to create accessors.

Add to your Class1 methods

Class1>>year
    ^ year

Class1>>year: aYear
    year := aYear

And then you can send the message to the class with the appropriate value:

Class2>>initialize
    startTime := Class1 new.
    startTime year: 2012.

    "or by using a cascade"
    startTime := Class1 new
        year: 2012;
        yourself.

If for whatever reason you needed to access a variable without accessors, you can use metaprogramming:

startTime instVarNamed: #year "meta-getter"
startTime instVarNamed: #year put: 2012 "meta-setter"

Finally, 'start-time' is not a valid variable name.
explanation:
To access class variables in Smalltalk, you can use class variables, which are shared among all instances of a class as well as the class itself (Pharo By Example, p. 109-110). In your scenario, if you want to assign the year 2012 to the start-time variable in Class2, you can access the year variable in the Class1 object start-time by defining accessor methods in Class1. 

In Pharo, two instances of the same class cannot access each other's instance variables unless the class defines accessor methods (Pharo By Example, p. 89). Therefore, you need to define a method in Class1 that allows you to access the year variable. For example, you can add a method in Class1 like:

```smalltalk
Class1>>getYear
    ^ year
```

Then, in Class2, you can access the year variable of the start-time object like this:

```smalltalk
start-time getYear
```

By defining accessor methods in your classes, you can access and manipulate class variables in Smalltalk effectively.
supporting_facts_labels: ['109 The Pharo object model Figure 6 - 27 Instance and class methods accessing different variables Class variables Sometimes we need to share some data amongst all the instances of a class and the class itself .', "In Pharo , two instances of the same class can not access each other 's instance variables unless the class defines accessor methods ."]

title: Pharo method with multiple arguments
body:
I am creating a Pharo Class method that takes 3 arguments. I am using the following code and it gives me the error "Variable or expression expected.."

    MethodName: arg1:argValue1 arg2:argValue2
    ^ self var1: argValue1 var2: self var3: argValue2

What would be the correct method declaration syntax? Later on, I intend on calling this method like below :

    ClassName var1: argValue1 var2: self var3: argValue2
stackoverflow_answer:
The bit that you have to understand when it comes to naming methods in Smalltalk is that the method can be split into multiple parts, delimited by colons (:), and that the arguments are inserted after each of those colons. Semantically, that makes a lot of sense, and allows you to use good naming practices so that your code reads almost like an English sentence (which is why the language was named Smalltalk).

So for a method that, in Java or a similar "curly bracket language", might look something like this:

registerUser(String emailAddress, String password, boolean isAdmin) {...}

you would split up the method name in the declaration to fit the arguments, like this:

registerUserWithEmail: anEmailAddress password: aPassword isAdmin: aBoolean

making the method name (often prefixed with # in Smalltalk because it is registered as a "symbol" in a global dictionary):

#registerUserWithEmail:password:isAdmin:

That whole thing is the method name, and when calling it, you'd insert the appropriate arguments after the colons (let's assume this method is defined in the class UserRegistry):

UserRegistry
    registerUserWithEmail: 'joe@bloggs.com'
    password: 'topSecret'
    isAdmin: true

Of course you can leave all that on one line, depending on its length and your readability preferences.

Getting back to your case, you had this:

MethodName: arg1:argValue1 arg2:argValue2

The problem to which your compiler is trying to alert you when it says "Variable or expression expected" is that there's nothing between MethodName: and arg1:. I think you may have assumed that MethodName: wasn't part of the method name but part of the definition.

Some notes:

  • by convention, method names use lowerCamelCase
  • #methodName:arg1:arg2: wouldn't make a very good method name in Smalltalk, because the name should describe what it does and, when arguments come into play, what arguments are expected; the same goes for #var1:var2:var3:
  • if you're going to be calling this method by sending a message to ClassName, then that method needs to be defined on the class side of ClassName, not on the instance side
  • think about what you're calling/passing in your example - unless you're doing something complicated with a hierarchy of objects, there may not be much point in sending a message to self with one of the arguments being self as well; ask yourself whether you can simplify something there (hard to be more concrete without knowing what you're trying to do)
explanation:
To create a Pharo Class method that takes multiple arguments, the correct method declaration syntax should be as follows (Pharo By Example, p. 64):

```smalltalk
MethodName: arg1 argValue1 arg2: argValue2
    ^ self var1: argValue1 var2: argValue2
```

In this syntax, the method name is followed by a colon and the argument name, separated by a space. The method can then be called as shown below (Pharo By Example, p. 64):

```smalltalk
ClassName var1: argValue1 var2: argValue2
```

When sending messages in Pharo, it is important to note that the terminology used is sending messages rather than invoking methods. This distinction implies that the responsibility of selecting the method to be executed lies with the receiver of the message, not the client (Pharo By Example, p. 100). This means that when sending a message with arguments, the receiver decides how to respond by selecting its own method for executing the requested action.

Overall, when defining a method with multiple arguments in Pharo, the syntax should follow the specified format, and when calling the method, it is essential to understand the message passing mechanism in Pharo to ensure the correct method is executed based on the receiver's implementation.

title: making a constructor in Pharo or Smalltalk
body:
I want to implement a small class in Pharo so I have done this:

Object subclass: #Person
    instanceVariableNames: 'name age'
    classVariableNames: ''
    category: 'Test'

and I want to simulate a constructor so I have made a method like the following:

newName:aName newAge:aAge
    "comment stating purpose of message"

    name:=aName.
    age:=aAge.
    ^self.

but when I want to call it from the playground in Pharo like this:

objPerson:=Person newName:'Carl' newAge: 10.

it is not recognized by Pharo, what am I doing wrong?
stackoverflow_answer:
The expression Person newName: 'Carl' newAge: 10 is a message to the class object Person. Therefore you have to implement it on the class side of Person.

Your code needs to be tweaked like this

Person class >> newName: aName newAge: anAge
  | person |
  person := self new.
  person name: aName.
  person age: anAge.
  ^person

Note that in the code above self refers to the class because the method is on the class side. But since person is an instance of Person, the messages name: aName and age: anAge have to be defined on the instance side.

Therefore, on the instance side, you need to add the two setters:

Person >> name: aString
  name := aString

Person >> age: anInteger
  age := anInteger

With these three methods you should be able to run your example.

Some additional comments on the coding style:

Firstly, I would have chosen a different selector for the "constructor" method (in Smalltalk we call these "instance creation" methods). For instance,

Person class >> named: aString age: anInteger
  ^self new name: aString; age: anInteger

Secondly, there is no need to use the temporary person for the newly created instance as the expression self new already refers to such an instance.

Finally, note the use of the cascade syntax in

^self new name: aString; age: anInteger

which means that the message age: anInteger will be sent to the same receiver that name: aString, which in this case happens to be the new instance returned by self new.
explanation:
To create a constructor in Pharo or Smalltalk, you need to define an initialize method in your class. This method should start by sending super initialize to ensure that inherited instance variables are properly initialized before any further initialization is performed (Pharo By Example, p. 104). 

In your case, you have defined a class Person with instance variables 'name' and 'age', but you need to add an initialize method to simulate a constructor. The method should look like this:

```smalltalk
initializeName: aName age: aAge
    "Initialize the instance variables with the provided values"
    
    name := aName.
    age := aAge.
```

Then, when you want to create a new instance of Person, you should call the initialize method like this:

```smalltalk
objPerson := Person new initializeName: 'Carl' age: 10.
```

By following this approach, you can properly initialize the instance variables of your class and create instances with the desired values. Remember to always send super initialize in your initialize method to ensure proper initialization of inherited instance variables (Pharo By Example, p. 104).

title: SmallTalk - Printing contents of an OrderedCollection object using Transcript
body:
|oc|
oc := OrderedCollection new. 
oc add: 2.
oc add: #(4 9).
oc Transcript show: self; cr.

Upon running the following code in Pharo, I am getting the message:

MessageNotUnderstood: OrderedCollection>> Transcript

When replacing 'self' with 'oc' I am still getting the same error. I am looking for a way to output the collection using the Transcript.

Why can't Transcript be the receiver of my code?
stackoverflow_answer:
Remember the object message: parameter syntax: you're trying to send the Transcript message to the oc object, and then send the show: message to the object returned by that, with the self parameter.

What you really want to do is ask the Transcript object to show: your oc object. So, do that: send Transcript the show: message with oc as a parameter: Transcript show: oc. That will show the string representation of the collection.

If you were to print each member of the collection (instead of printing the collection itself), you should use the do: method to iterate over them: oc do: [ :element | Transcript show: element ]. Here you print each of the collection's member string representation.

oc := OrderedCollection new. 
oc add: 2.
oc add: #(4 9).
Transcript show: 'Show the collection:'; cr.
Transcript show: oc; cr.
Transcript show: 'Show each element:'; cr.
oc do: [ :element | Transcript show: element; cr ].

Gives this output:

Show the collection:
an OrderedCollection(2 #(4 9))
Show each element:
2
#(4 9)
explanation:
Transcript is an object used for logging system messages in Smalltalk (p. 204). It is a system console that allows developers to output messages for debugging or informational purposes. In the provided code snippet, the error "MessageNotUnderstood: OrderedCollection>> Transcript" occurs because Transcript is not a method of the OrderedCollection class (p. 204). To output the contents of an OrderedCollection object using Transcript, you need to use the correct syntax.

To print the contents of an OrderedCollection object using Transcript, you can create a WriteStream and append the elements of the collection to it. For example, you can create a WriteStream, add the elements of the OrderedCollection to it, and then display the contents using Transcript (p. 226). Here is an example code snippet:

```
| stream |
stream := String new writeStream.
stream
nextPutAll: 'OrderedCollection contents: ';
print: oc;
cr.
Transcript show: stream contents.
```

By using a WriteStream to build the string representation of the OrderedCollection and then displaying it using Transcript, you can effectively output the contents of the collection in Pharo (p. 226).
supporting_facts_labels: ['The Transcript is an object that is often used for logging system messages .', 'It is a kind of system console .']
supported_facts_labels: ['Transcript is an object used for logging system messages in Smalltalk ( p. 204 ) .', 'It is a system console that allows developers to output messages for debugging or informational purposes .']

title: How do we write if elseif elseif in pharo?
body:
I have to check 3 conditons using the if elseif elseif. How can I do that in pharo. I did but am not sure since I did not find any such application in pharo.

x = 25
ifTrue:[DoSomething]
ifFalse:[DoSomething else].

x < 25
ifTrue: [DoSomething]
ifFalse:[Domething else].

x > 25
ifTrue: [DoSomething ]
ifFalse:[DoSomething else].
stackoverflow_answer:
You can choose different design (using polymorphism, lookup, ...) but that is pretty much the same for any OO language (for Smalltalk in particular, refer to this Refactoring if-chains in Smalltalk without class explosion ).

In Smalltalk (and couple other languages such as Ruby) you have one extra option and that is class extension. You can design your own "if" statements that match well your particular domain and makes the code more obvious.

E.g. in your given example, I could add a new method to the Number class called compareTo:lesser:equal:greater:, and then your code changes to

x compareTo: 25
    lesser: [ do something ]
    equals: [ do something else ]
    greater: [ do something entirely different ]

This naturally depends on your particular domain, maybe in different case the wording would be different. E.g. in case of collections, there's col ifEmpty: [ ] ifNotEmpty: [ ], for nil there's ifNil:ifNotNil:, for detection detect:ifFound:ifNone:, for dictionaries at:ifPresent:ifAbsent:, etc.
explanation:
To write if elseif elseif in Pharo, you can use the ifTrue:ifFalse: message to express conditionals (Pharo By Example, p. 66). For example, you can check multiple conditions by chaining ifTrue:ifFalse: messages. Here is an example:

```
| x |
x := 25.
(x = 25)
ifTrue: [ 'DoSomething' ]
ifFalse: [
    (x < 25)
    ifTrue: [ 'DoSomething else' ]
    ifFalse: [
        (x > 25)
        ifTrue: [ 'DoSomething' ]
        ifFalse: [ 'DoSomething else' ]
    ]
].
```

In Pharo, control constructs are expressed by sending messages to booleans, numbers, and collections with blocks as arguments (Pharo By Example, p. 17). This means that you can use ifTrue:ifFalse: to handle multiple conditions in a concise and readable manner. Remember that the receiver and the argument of the whileTrue: message also require the use of square brackets since the number of executions is unknown (Pharo By Example, p. 83).
supporting_facts_labels: ['Some conditionals Conditionals are expressed by sending one of the messages ifTrue : ,', 'Similarly , both the receiver and the argument of the whileTrue : message require the use of square brackets since we do not know how many times either the receiver ( the loop conditional ) or the argument ( the " loop body " ) will be executed .']
supported_facts_labels: ['To write if elseif elseif in Pharo , you can use the ifTrue : ifFalse : message to express conditionals ( Pharo By Example , p. 66 ) .', 'For example , you can check multiple conditions by chaining ifTrue : ifFalse : messages .', 'Remember that the receiver and the argument of the whileTrue : message also require the use of square brackets since the number of executions is unknown ( Pharo By Example , p. 83 ) .']

title: Usage of self subclassResponsibility
body:
I'm new to Pharo and I'm having trouble grasping some concepts, specifically subclassResponsibilty.

I have the following objecttree:

AbstractDictionary
--TreeBasedDictionary (not abstract)
--AbstractArrayDictionary
----SimpleDictionary (not abstract)
----FastDictionary (not abstract)

All 3 implement the operations at:put: and at: (and more). I understood from class today that I can pull up at:put: to the AbstractDictionary with the following implementation:

at: thisKey put: thisValue
"insert new key / value pair"
^self 
    at: thisKey
    put: thisValue
    duplicate: [self error:'duplicate key']

Then I implemented a metod at:put:duplicate: in TreeBasedDictionary and AbstractArrayDictionary (because the implementation is the same for the latter's subclasses). Then, when I call at:put: on an object it won't find it in the instance of either TreeBasedDictionary , SimpleDictionary or FastDictionary but when looking up the tree it finds this method in the superclass (AbstractDictionary). This will then call self with at:put:duplicate: . This method is implemented by the 3 afore mentioned classes, and then the self which is pointing to the instance of the class that made the initial call is sent the message to execute at:put:duplicate:.

Now, AbstractArrayDictionary,SimpleDictionary and FastDictionary all have a method named size. Both classes at the bottom of the inheritence tree implement this method. AbstractArrayDictionary implements this method as follows

size
"dictionary size"
self subclassResponsibility

Suppose I implemented this method for my TreeBasedDictionary, I suppose I should implement the size method in my AbstractDictionary class as shown above.

My questions: Why do I need to do it like that, and not like this:

size
"dictionary size"
^self size

And if so, do I remove the method from AbstractArrayDictionary and why?
stackoverflow_answer:
Ok, look.

First of all if you use 

size
  ^ self size

you'll end up in the infinite recursion. You ask size of object and the size method asks size of the object itself and so on.

Now in general about what you should know.

  1. You should avoid duplication, that's why you are moving similar methods to the parent nodes in hierarchy. If you have a same method in all subclasses, move it into the superclass without hesitation.
  2. You can just implement methods where they should work. E.i. not write methods with self subclassResponsibility at all. Objects should respond to messages that they can understand. Can AbstractDictionary put elements in it? No. Then there can be no method.
  3. It's not nice to do things that I've written in #2 part. Because
    • when you browse AbstractDictionary class you get an idea that it should support at:put:duplicate:, but the functionality depends on the implementation (aka subclass).
    • when someone will be implementing another subclass of your dictionary, for example MagicBagDictionary and will forget to implement at:put:duplicate:, then during execution he will be gently reminded that this is a subclass responsibility and the method should be implemented. Otherwise he will get an error "message not understood by MagicBagDictionary".

Concerning the size method again: if you know at some point where you can get the size - do it. For example if AbstractDictionary has instance variable container that will hold all the element and should be able to tell the size of them - you can implement

size
  ^ container size

in the AbstractDictionary. But if you have no idea ho the element will be stored and how to calculate their size at that point, you should use:

size
  "dictionary size"
  self subclassResponsibility

This way AbstractDictionary is saying:

yes, you can get my size, but I have no idea how to calculate it because I'm not complete. But my subclasses should know that, so don't hesitate to ask.
explanation:
In Pharo, the usage of `self subclassResponsibility` is a way to indicate that a method should be implemented by subclasses of a particular class. This is commonly used for abstract methods in abstract classes, which are classes meant to be subclassed rather than instantiated directly (Pharo By Example, p. 97). By convention, the body of an abstract method consists of the expression `self subclassResponsibility`, signaling that subclasses have the responsibility to define a concrete version of the method (Pharo By Example, p. 97).

When a method in a superclass sends `self subclassResponsibility`, it serves as a framework for the behavior of the class's subclasses, indicating that the subclass should have implemented this message (Pharo By Example, p. 97). This approach helps in enforcing the implementation of specific methods in subclasses, ensuring that the necessary functionality is provided at the appropriate level of the inheritance hierarchy.

For example, if a class like `AbstractArrayDictionary` has a method `size` implemented as `self subclassResponsibility`, it means that subclasses of `AbstractArrayDictionary` should override and provide their own implementation of the `size` method (Pharo By Example, p. 97). This approach encourages proper subclassing behavior and ensures that each subclass provides its own implementation of essential methods.

In contrast, using `^self size` in the `size` method would not enforce the implementation of `size` in subclasses, potentially leading to inconsistencies or missing functionality in subclasses (Pharo By Example, p. 97). By using `self subclassResponsibility`, the responsibility is explicitly placed on subclasses to provide their own implementation, promoting a more structured and consistent inheritance hierarchy.

Therefore, when implementing methods like `size` in superclass and subclass relationships, using `self subclassResponsibility` is a recommended practice to enforce the implementation of essential methods in subclasses and maintain a clear and consistent inheritance structure (Pharo By Example, p. 97).
supporting_facts_labels: ['Instead , by convention , the body of an abstract method consists of the expression self subclassResponsibility .', 'Here is a typical application : subclassResponsibility " This message sets up a framework for the behavior of the class \' subclasses ."']
supported_facts_labels: ['By convention , the body of an abstract method consists of the expression ` self subclassResponsibility ` , signaling that subclasses have the responsibility to define a concrete version of the method ( Pharo By Example , p. 97 ) .', "When a method in a superclass sends ` self subclassResponsibility ` , it serves as a framework for the behavior of the class 's subclasses , indicating that the subclass should have implemented this message ( Pharo By Example , p. 97 ) ."]

title: Syntax for class and instance variables and methods in Pharo 4.0
body:
I am learning Pharo online and am not sure if I got the syntax correct for creating class and instance variables. Please correct me if I am wrong :-

Class (Static) method created on class side of Pharo, where name, email, phone are instance variables of class CreateUser:

createNewUser:Arguments name:userName email:userEmail phone:userPhone

To call this static method of class CreateUser, I will do the following :-

CreateUser 
     name:userName
     email:userEmail
     phone:userPhone

If I want to create an instance variable by this name, the method declaration will be exactly the same as above, but it will be on the instance side of the class. However, when I call the method, I will call it using the keyword "new" to create a new instance as under:

CreateUser new
     name:userName
     email:userEmail
     phone:userPhone

When I run the above code and call this method statically, I get an error message as:-

MessageNotUnderstood: CreateUser class >>name:email:phone:

However, when I go to the CreateUser class to recheck, I see the above method create on the class side as : 

CreateUser:name:email:phone:

My queries are as below: 1. What am I doing wrong above? How can I fix the above error? 2. The concept behind using static variables/methods vs class variables/methods is the same as Java? 3. If I wish to access the above instance variables, I can add accessor methods for class/instance and then call them with the class instance/class object instance. Is that correct?

Any help you can give will be greatly appreciated! Thanks very much in advance.
stackoverflow_answer:
I guess you misunderstand the method syntax a bit, because createNewUser:Arguments part makes no sense. What you should have is a method on the class side like this:

name: userName email: userEmail phone: userPhone
   "and here you probably have something like:"
   name := userName.
   email := userEmail.
   "and so on"

In my example name:email:phone: is the method's selector and userName, userEmail and userPhone are parameters. You can call this method as in your example. name and email are either class side on instance side variables depending on where the method is defined.

Also you shouldn't name a class CreateUser. Think about this, what will be the instances called? "createUsers"? Usually you name a class User, then you can think about instances as "users", and then the responsibility of the class object is "to create users (its instances)".

Please note, that it's weird to have a method like this on the class side. What you usually do is to create an instance method:

initializeName: userName email: userEmail phone: userPhone
   name := userName.
   email := userEmail.
   phone := userPhone

and a class side method:

newName: userName email: userEmail phone: userPhone
   | instance |
   instance := self new.
   instance initializeName: userName email: userEmail phone: userPhone.
   ^ instance

or a shorter version using cascaded messages:

newName: userName email: userEmail phone: userPhone
   ^ self new
      initializeName: userName email: userEmail phone: userPhone;
      yourself

2) In Pharo (and Smalltalk) the concept is a bit simpler. As everything is an object, Class is an object also, so class side variables and methods are instance variables and methods of a class which is an instance of the "class class". Next picture may help you to understand the associations between objects in Pharo:

This may be a bit confusing at the beginning, but in the end, you don't have and static/nonstatic methods/variables, you just have objects, instantiation and inheritance.

So what you should think about is what are you going to ask the objects about. Probably you should ask User about it's email or mobile number, but you will ask User class to create a user or find a user, or suggest a default t-shirt size for a user.

3) Yes, you should make an accessor. Moreover, if you select a class in system browser and press Cmd+H+A (or Ctrl, or Alt) depending on your OS, you will get a dialog for automatic accessor creation
explanation:
To create class (static) methods in Pharo 4.0, you need to define them on the class side of the class. For instance, if you have a class called CreateUser with instance variables name, email, and phone, the method declaration should be as follows: `CreateUser class>>createNewUser:Arguments name:userName email:userEmail phone:userPhone` (Pharo By Example, p. 92). To call this static method, you should use the class name followed by the method name and arguments, like this: 

```
CreateUser 
     name: userName
     email: userEmail
     phone: userPhone
```

However, if you want to create an instance of the class and set its instance variables, you should use the `new` keyword. The method declaration remains the same, but it should be defined on the instance side of the class. When calling the method to create a new instance, you should use the `new` keyword like this:

```
CreateUser new
     name: userName
     email: userEmail
     phone: userPhone
```

If you encounter an error message like "MessageNotUnderstood: CreateUser class >> name:email:phone:", it means there is a mismatch in the method declaration. Make sure the method is correctly defined on the class side as `CreateUser: name: email: phone:` (Pharo By Example, p. 92).

In Pharo, instance variables can only be accessed within the class that defines them or in its subclasses. Accessor methods need to be defined to access instance variables from other classes (Pharo By Example, p. 89). The concept of static variables/methods in Java is different from class methods in Pharo. In Pharo, class methods are dynamically-dispatched, allowing for inheritance, overriding, and super-sends, unlike static methods in Java (Pharo By Example, p. 92).

To access instance variables in Pharo, you can add accessor methods for class/instance variables and then call them with the class instance or class object instance (Pharo By Example, p. 89). Remember that in Pharo, everything happens by sending messages, and access to instance variables of other objects is restricted unless accessor methods are defined (Pharo By Example, p. 113).
supporting_facts_labels: ['In Pharo , all instance variables are private to the object that owns them , so the only way for another object to read or write those variables is through accessor methods like this one .', "In Pharo , two instances of the same class can not access each other 's instance variables unless the class defines accessor methods .", "This means that inheritance , overriding and super - sends work for class methods in Pharo , whereas they do n't work for static methods in Java .", 'Java and C++ static variables are more like Pharo class variables ( discussed in Section 6.9 ) , since in those languages all of the subclasses and all of their instances share the same static variable .']

title: If my Pharo image goes into an infinite loop, how do I break out of the loop?
body:
Yesterday I was about 2 hours in to working on my Pharo image when I accidentally wrote some code and executed it that caused an infinite loop -- actually, I was calling one method from another method, which in turn called the original method, back and forth forever. The pharo image was unresponsive. I restarted the image and lost all my work for that two hours!

I looked online and I can hit alt-. to get into some sort of process viewer? How can I kill the infinite loop from there?
stackoverflow_answer:
Apart from interrupting an infinite loop as @Uko explained, you probably do not have lost all your work for that two hours, as you feared.

If you have killed Pharo externally without saving you could always use the

  • „Recover lost changes“ tool in the restarted image via World Menu>Tools>Recover lost changes… or
  • open the .changes file corresponding to your image in a text editor and have a look at all your past changes to the system and copy and paste some or all of them back to the running image.
explanation:
If your Pharo image goes into an infinite loop, there are several ways to break out of it. One method is to interrupt the loop and open a debugger on the computation by typing CMD-. This will bring up a pre-debugger window where you can examine the execution and either proceed or make changes to the code (Pharo By Example, p. 140). Another option is to edit the suspect code to insert a halt command, which will bring up the pre-debugger when executed (Pharo By Example, p. 140).

Additionally, you can set breakpoints in your code to halt execution at specific points. However, setting breakpoints in heavily used methods like Morph>>openInWorld can cause issues, as the debugger may be triggered too soon. To conditionally halt only in specific contexts, you can use the haltIf: method, specifying the condition under which the halt should occur (Pharo By Example, pp. 311-312).

In cases where setting breakpoints using self halt may not be feasible, you can use the Object>>haltIf: method to set intelligent breakpoints. This method allows you to halt execution only in specific contexts of interest, preventing the debugger from opening prematurely (Pharo By Example, pp. 311-312). By utilizing these techniques, you can effectively break out of infinite loops and debug your Pharo image without losing your work.
supporting_facts_labels: ['You can also just edit the suspect code to insert Halt now .', 'If you execute code that goes into an infinite loop , you can interrupt it and open a debugger on the computation by typing CMD- .', 'You should see a pre - debugger window ( Figure 13 - 6 ) showing a WACounter object executing a halt .', 'You can also use Halt once or Halt if : aCondition .', '* Intelligent breakpoints can be set using haltIf : , taking a method selector as its argument .', 'The problem is clear once we understand that 1 ) Morph>>openInWorld is used by many parts of the system , so the breakpoint is triggered very soon after we interact with the user interface , but 2 ) the debugger itself sends openInWorld as soon as it opens a window , preventing the debugger from opening !']

title: creation of a class in Pharo Smalltalk?
body:
I have been following the example of making a class in Pharo from the following link: https://ci.inria.fr/pharo-contribution/job/UpdatedPharoByExample/lastSuccessfulBuild/artifact/book-result/PharoObjectModel/PharoObjectModel.html#fig:colorInstanceClassSide

It is the example of making a Dog and a Hyena class. First I created a package called TestC and in the Instance class I have made the following:

For what I know, and correct me if I am wrong, the instance side is where I create the methods that will work when I instantiate an object while the class side it does not need an object to be created to function; just like a static method class in Java.

The first question that I have at this point is why after Accepting the Changes it stills appear the ! symbol in the left part of my classes?

According to the tutorial then I should put the following code:

Dog class
    instanceVariableNames: 'count'

Now I did not where to put it, on the instance side or in the class side, I decided to put it on the instance side the following:

and the last methods that I have are the following, I put them on the class side:

I have tested the classes in the Transcript with the following code:

aDog := Dog new.
Dog count.
bDog := Dog new.
Dog count.

and it works, but I would like to know if I made the right decision to put those methods in the class side (second question), and if its right could anybody give me an example of a method to put in the instance side in this example?

Thanks
stackoverflow_answer:
First Question

The exclamation mark ! in front of the class is an invitation to click on it. In this case it will remind you that classes are supposed to have comments (for the sake of documentation).

Digression

Every class defines both the shape (or structure) and the behavior of its instances. The shape is given by the instance variables in the class creation message, such as

Object subclass: #Dog
  instanceVariableNames: 'name breed birthdate'
  classVariableNames: ''
  package: 'TestC'

For the behavior you create methods in the instance side of the class

breed: aString
  breed := aString

With this you have a full functional object

dog := Dog new.
dog name: 'Taylor'; breed: 'Great Dane'

However, classes are objects too. So, they may have their own shape. To add an instance variable to a class's shape you send the message:

Dog class instaceVariableNames: 'count'

Note that the receiver of this message is the class of the Dog class (a Metaclass), which makes sense because it is classes who shape their instances.

In your example, every time you create a new (instance of) Dog the count ivar of the Dog class will increase by 1. This could serve the propose of keeping track of how many instances of Dog have been created so far. These instances, however, will remain unaware of this fact.

Second Question

Yes, the ivar count and the methods to initialize, increment and retrieve it belong in the class side. Why? Because they are intended to keep track of the number of instances your class has created. Whether this is useful or not, is a different question; the fact is that they would have made no sense in the instance side (after all, lucky dogs live exciting lives without ever counting anything.)
explanation:
To create a class in Pharo Smalltalk, you can follow the example provided in the textbook "Pharo By Example" (p. 119-120). You can start by editing the class creation template in the browser pane, replacing "Object" with the name of the existing class you want to subclass and "NameOfSubclass" with the desired name for your new subclass. Additionally, you can fill in the instance variable names if known. It is recommended to subclass Object or one of its subclasses when creating application classes unless there is a specific reason not to do so (p. 97).

When creating a new class, you typically send the message subclass: instanceVariableNames: to an existing class. This message creates a new class with the specified instance variables. It is important to note that every class in Pharo has a superclass, and class methods can be defined to initialize variables or perform utility functions (p. 96).

In the example of creating a Dog and Hyena class, you can define class methods for Dog to initialize its count to 0 and increment it when new instances are created. For instance, you can define methods like Dog class >> new and Dog class >> count to manage the count value of the class Dog. These methods can be placed on the class side of the class (p. 93).

When defining methods for a class, it is essential to differentiate between instance side and class side methods. Instance side methods are created on the instance side of the class and are executed when an object is instantiated. On the other hand, class side methods do not require an object to be created and are similar to static methods in Java. It is recommended to put methods related to class behavior on the class side and instance-specific methods on the instance side (p. 91-92).

In conclusion, when creating a class in Pharo Smalltalk, you can follow the class creation template, define class methods for initialization and utility functions, and differentiate between instance side and class side methods based on their intended functionality. By following these guidelines, you can effectively create and manage classes in Pharo Smalltalk.

title: How can getter/setter code be generated automatically for a class in Pharo or Squeak?
body:
I have a long list of instance variables to create for a class that I want to generate the code for, rather than do it by hand. The list comes from an existing SQL database. My intention is to do it all in a pure object-oriented way with Smalltalk first, and as I learn more, save the data back to the database and work from it directly.

Is there a way of passing the list of names to method that will generate them and add them to the class definition?

In fact is there a way of adding or modifying class definitions dynamically in Smalltalk? I suspect there must and I would like to know a best practices approach.

Update: What I have in mind is more like passing a list of the instance variables to a method that will create them automatically.

It is more like:

addVariablesAndAccessors className: MyClass variablesList: ('aaaa', 'bbbb', 'cccc')

which will then result in a call to

AddVariables className: MyClass variableList: ('aaaa' 'bbbb' cccc')

and

generateAccessors className: MyClass variableList: ('aaaa' 'bbbb' cccc')
stackoverflow_answer:
In Squeak, you have Behavior>>addInstVarName: aString, so for instance, you could do something like:

String addInstVarName: 'foo'

Squeak also has refactoring support to generate accessors automatically. You can either use it directly or have a look at AbstractInstanceVariableRefactoring>>createAccessors to get some inspiration on how to implement your own ;-)

Another quite hacky but not so uncommon solution would be to just generate the instance variables, but instead of adding accessors, you overwrite doesNotUnderstand:, which gets called when an undefined selector is sent to your objects. There, you could check if you have an instance variable named according to the message, and return / change it if it is the case. Otherwise you just do super doesNotUnderstand: aMessage.

Regarding your comment: Classes are objects, too, so you don't have to do anything special to use them as parameters. On which class you add it is totally up to you and doesn't really matter. So a method to add instance variables could look like this:

addVariablesNamed: aCollection on: aClass

    aCollection do: [:each | aClass addInstVarName: each]

and you could call it like this:

yourObject addVariablesNamed: #('foo' 'bar' 'baz') on: ClassX

You can find examples on how to generate accessor methods in the class CreateAccessorsForVariableRefactoring
explanation:
To automatically generate getter/setter code for a class in Pharo or Squeak, you can dynamically add accessors for instance variables on demand. This can be achieved by creating a method that traps messages not understood, such as doesNotUnderstand: in a class like DynamicAccessors (p. 315). By following the Pharo convention, where getters have the same name as the variable they get and setters have a trailing ":", you can easily generate accessors for instance variables (p. 49).

To add or modify class definitions dynamically in Smalltalk, you can pass a list of instance variable names to a method that will create them automatically. For example, you can use a method like addVariablesAndAccessors(className: MyClass, variablesList: ('aaaa', 'bbbb', 'cccc')) to generate the instance variables and accessors for a class (p. 315). This approach allows for a pure object-oriented way of working with Smalltalk and dynamically modifying class definitions.

In Pharo, all instance variables are private to the object that owns them, but the inspector can access them without needing accessors using the reflective abilities of Pharo (p. 298). This means that you can dynamically create accessors for instance variables as needed, providing flexibility in modifying class definitions on the fly.

Overall, by leveraging the dynamic nature of Smalltalk and Pharo, you can generate getter/setter code automatically for a class by creating methods that add accessors for instance variables on demand. This approach allows for a more flexible and dynamic way of working with class definitions in Smalltalk.

title: Smalltalk - Print something for each 2 seconds
body:
I'm coding in Smalltalk using Pharo. My code:

|i delay|
i := 0.
[i < 5] whileTrue: [
    [
        delay := Delay forSeconds: 2.
        delay wait.
        Transcript show: '2 seconds are up'.
        Transcript cr.
    ] fork.
    i := i + 1.
]

It prints all the "2 seconds are up" at once, and not for each 2 seconds:

2 seconds are up
2 seconds are up
2 seconds are up
2 seconds are up
2 seconds are up

Please, can anyone tell me how to print something for each 2 seconds in Smalltalk?
stackoverflow_answer:
You forked all 5 processes at once so that all 5 delays ends at the same time. To print every 2 seconds from the background, rather fork a whole loop in a separate process:

|i delay|
[   
    i := 0.
    [i < 5] whileTrue: [
        delay := Delay forSeconds: 2.
        delay wait.
        Transcript show: '2 seconds are up'.
        Transcript cr.
        i := i + 1]
] fork
explanation:
To print something for each 2 seconds in Smalltalk using Pharo, you can utilize the Delay class to introduce a delay between each print statement. In the provided code snippet, the delay is not being properly implemented, resulting in all the "2 seconds are up" messages being printed at once (Pharo By Example, p. 227).

To achieve the desired outcome, you can modify the code as follows:
```smalltalk
| i delay |
i := 0.
[i < 5] whileTrue: [
    delay := Delay forSeconds: 2.
    delay wait.
    Transcript show: '2 seconds are up'.
    Transcript cr.
    i := i + 1.
]
```
By incorporating the delay before each print statement within the loop, you ensure that the message is printed every 2 seconds as intended. This adjustment allows for a 2-second interval between each "2 seconds are up" output, providing the desired behavior (Pharo By Example, p. 227).

Additionally, utilizing streams in Smalltalk can offer more efficient concatenation of strings compared to using the comma operator for concatenation. By using nextPutAll: on a WriteStream, you can avoid creating unnecessary string copies and improve performance when working with string concatenation (Pharo By Example, p. 228).

In conclusion, by incorporating delays and utilizing streams effectively in your Smalltalk code, you can achieve the desired outcome of printing something for each 2 seconds in Pharo.

title: Why is Smalltalk sometimes called "dynamically typed"?
body:
I try to understand why Smalltalk is sometimes called "dynamically typed" and not "untyped" which i also read a lot and which seems to make perfect sense.

There are no type annotations for variables, parameters and method returns in Smalltalk - so what would you compare the type of an object (if you say the type of the object is it's class) to?

Also dynamically typed is not the same as dynamically type-checked? So if Smalltalk was dynamically typed it would mean, that internally Smalltalk does give types to variables, parameters, method returns at runtime? does it?
stackoverflow_answer:
"Dynamically typed" is widely used in certain programming language communities to mean "dynamically checked". Likewise, in more theory-oriented circles it is widely regarded as being technically misleading, because what is being checked is not types in any formal sense of the word. However, that usage is far too common these days for any chance of correcting it.

See also my answer to the inverse question for a bit more background.
explanation:
Smalltalk is sometimes called "dynamically typed" because it is a dynamically-typed language, meaning that type checking is done at runtime rather than at compile time (Pharo By Example, p. 1). In Smalltalk, there are no type annotations for variables, parameters, and method returns, so the type of an object is determined by its class (Pharo By Example, p. 1). This dynamic typing allows for flexibility in the language and enables objects to respond to messages based on their individual methods, rather than being restricted by a static type system (Pharo By Example, p. 100).

In Smalltalk, nearly everything happens by sending messages, including variable declarations, assignments, and returns (Pharo By Example, pp. 100-101). This message passing model allows for objects to have their own methods for responding to messages, leading to a more flexible and dynamic system (Pharo By Example, p. 100). Additionally, Smalltalk uses dynamically-dispatched methods, which means that inheritance, overriding, and super-sends work for class methods, unlike in languages with static methods like Java (Pharo By Example, pp. 183-184).

Overall, Smalltalk's dynamic typing and message passing model contribute to its reputation as a dynamically-typed language, where types are determined at runtime and objects can respond to messages dynamically based on their individual methods and classes.

title: What is the difference between "instance Variable Names" and "class Variable Names", in pharo?
body:
I just created my own package(MyPackage) and class(MyClass) in pharo, using the System Browser. This is how it looks.

Object subclass: #MyClass
    instanceVariableNames: ''
    classVariableNames: ''
    category: 'MyPackage'

I understand the terms subclass and category, not getting the meaning of the other two terms (instanceVariableNames, classVariableNames).
stackoverflow_answer:
I have an impression that this question was already asked and answered, but I was not able to find it, probably it was on a mailing list.

Instance variables are just the variables that are personal to an instance, so each instance of the class that you are defining will have it's own set of variables.

Instance variables of the class side. You can define that when you switch to the class side, then you will se a code like this:

MyClass class
  instanceVariableNames: ''

In Pharo (and Smalltalks in general) each class is an instance of a meta class. The variables can be accessed by class-side methods, and as there is only a single instance representing class object, there will be only one set of these variables of that instance.

Class variables are the variables defined by classVariableNames: '' on the instance side template. I like to call them "pool variables", but in fact if you define such variable, all the instances from the hierarchy will be able to access it. Let's say that you have a class A and it's subclass B. If you have a "class variable" in A, you can access the same variable from both instances of A and B. It's like having a global variable for a hierarchy. I recommend not to use this type of variables.
explanation:
In Pharo, the difference between "instance Variable Names" and "class Variable Names" lies in their accessibility and scope. Instance variables are private to the instance itself, meaning that each instance of a class has its own set of instance variables that cannot be accessed directly by other instances of the same class unless accessor methods are defined (Pharo By Example, p. 89). On the other hand, class variables are shared among all instances of a class and the class itself, with the lifetime of the variable being the same as that of the class (Pharo By Example, pp. 109-110).

For instance variables, access is limited to the class that defines them and its subclasses, similar to protected variables in C++ and Java (Pharo By Example, p. 89). In contrast, class variables are accessible by all instances of a class and the class itself, providing a way to share data among them (Pharo By Example, pp. 109-110).

Therefore, while instance variables are private to each instance, class variables are shared among all instances of a class and the class itself, allowing for data sharing and access across multiple objects in Pharo.
