title: Why do methods return "self" by default in Smalltalk?
body:
Background

In Smalltalk, if you don't excplicitly return anything then the message passing evaluates to the receiver (or "self" in the message context).

For example, given this method:

MyClass >> myMethod
  Transcript show: 'hello'; cr.

Evaluating (doint "print-it") this:

| myInstance |
myInstance := MyClass new.
myInstance myMethod.

If <print-it> is done to the last invocation, then the result would be the instance itself.

Questions

  • Why was this designed this way?
  • What is the idea behind it?
  • What was the philosophical background?
  • What are the practical benefits from it? Is it to facilitate method chaining?
stackoverflow_answer:
One very simple reason has not been stated yet: In the Virtual Machine, returning self is simpler and more efficient than returning any other object.

Smalltalk byte codes implement a stack machine. That means arguments are passed by pushing them onto a stack, rather than putting them into registers. In addition to the arguments as listed in the method signature, a hidden argument is always passed, which is the receiver of the message. So even for unary methods (those without arguments) the receiver is pushed onto the stack, then the method is executed, and the receiver value on the stack is how the method knows "self". By returning "self" if no explicit return statement is given, the VM can just leave the "self" oop on the stack, which saves at least one memory store operation. So from an efficiency and simplicity standpoint, returning "self" is the most elegant thing to do.
explanation:
In Smalltalk, methods return "self" by default because the pseudo-variable "self" represents the receiver of the message, similar to the keyword "this" in Java (Pharo By Example, p. 103). When a method does not explicitly return anything, the message passing evaluates to the receiver, which is "self" in the message context (Pharo By Example, p. 103). This design choice encourages a style where objects have small methods and delegate tasks to other objects, promoting good object-oriented practices (Pharo By Example, p. 101).

By default, a method in Smalltalk returns the message receiver if not specified differently (Pharo By Example, p. 104). Explicitly writing "^ self" at the end of a method is not recommended unless you intend for the sender to use the value returned (Pharo By Example, p. 103). This approach simplifies method chaining and promotes a clear communication of intent between objects in the system.

Overall, the philosophy behind methods returning "self" by default in Smalltalk is to promote encapsulation, delegation, and good object-oriented design practices. This design choice ensures that objects communicate by sending messages and encourages a modular and flexible approach to programming (Pharo By Example, p. 85-87).

title: How do we check in pharo if an element already exist in a set
body:
Say I have a function that continually add a random number to a collection after each refresh.

Myfunction
|list length data |

z := Set new.
x := 8.
data:= received data after each refresh.
length := data size.
1 to: length do:[:k | list := data at: k.
    list < x
    "How to check if this data already exist and not add"
        ifTrue:[z add: list]

How can I check in Pharo if a number already exists and if so it should not add it again?
stackoverflow_answer:
Sets and other basic collections are part of common Smalltalk. You should have a look at the Smalltalk documentation (e.g., the Smalltalk bluebook), particularly the references for each of the data types you are attempting to use as well as other related ones.

If you read the documentation for Set you'll find a message #includes: which will return true if the argument is in the set, and return false if it isn't. That would look like:

(z includes: list) ifTrue: [ z add: list ]

But, a Set by definition contains unique elements. Therefore, if you attempt to add an element that's already in the set, Smalltalk will not add it again and not give you an error if you attempt to do so. So you don't need to check to see if the set already includes list. You can just add it.

Then you are doing your data traversal the long, hard way. You don't need to get the length then loop on an index from 1 to that length. I assume your data is some kind of Smalltalk Collection, so you can just use the #do: message. The #do: message allows you to iterate a block over each element of the collection:

data do: [ :each | each < x ifTrue: [ z add: each ] ]

This replaces everything you have shown starting at length := ....
explanation:
To check if an element already exists in a set in Pharo, you can use the Set class, which behaves as a mathematical set with no duplicate elements and no specific order (Pharo By Example, p. 207). Elements can be added to a Set using the `add:` message, and you can create sets using `Set newFrom:` or `Collection>>asSet:` (Pharo By Example, p. 207). For example, if you have a set `s` with elements 4/2, 4, and 2, the size of the set would be 2 after adding these elements (Pharo By Example, p. 207).

To eliminate duplicates from a collection, you can use the `asSet` method, which offers a convenient way to remove duplicates (Pharo By Example, p. 207). For instance, if you have a collection of colors and you convert it to a set, the size of the set will reflect the unique elements in the collection (Pharo By Example, p. 207).

If you want to test set membership in Pharo, you can use the `includes:` method, which checks if a specific element is present in the set (Pharo By Example, p. 163). For example, in a test method like `testIncludes`, you can assert that a set includes a certain element (Pharo By Example, p. 163).

In the context of testing, you can also verify occurrences of elements in a set using methods like `occurrencesOf:` (Pharo By Example, p. 163). For instance, in a test method like `testOccurrences`, you can check the number of times a specific element appears in a set (Pharo By Example, p. 163).

To ensure that an element is not added to a set if it already exists, you can combine the `includes:` method with conditional statements to check for the presence of the element before adding it (Pharo By Example, p. 163). This way, you can prevent duplicate elements from being added to the set.

Overall, in Pharo, you can use the Set class and its methods like `add:`, `includes:`, and `asSet` to manage collections and check for the existence of elements in a set effectively.

title: How to implement a switch in Pharo Smalltalk
body:
I'm trying to parse a command and an int to make a "turtle" move on a board. I'm a bit overwhelmed, since it's not throwing an exception, and I can't even figure out how to open the debugger without one.

My code:

"only should begin parsing on new line"
endsWithNewLine:= aTurtleProgram endsWith: String cr.
endsWithNewLine ifTrue:[
    "splits commands based on new lines"
    commands := aTurtleProgram splitOn: String cr.
    commands do:[:com |
        "should split into command and value for command"
        i := com splitOn: ' '.
        "command"
        bo := ci at: 1.
        val := ci at: 2.
        "value for command"
        valInt := val asInteger.
        ^ bo = 'down' "attempted switch"
            ifTrue: [ turtle down: valInt ]
            ifFalse: [
              bo = 'left'
                  ifTrue: [ turtle left: valInt ]
                  ifFalse: [
                    bo = 'right'
                        ifTrue: [ turtle right: valInt ]
                        ifFalse: [
                          bo = 'up'
                              ifTrue: [ turtle up: valInt ]
                              ifFalse: [ self assert: false ] ] ] ] ].
stackoverflow_answer:
You can do it the way you did it, and you can open a debugger by inserting a self halt statement into your code.

Usually, ifs, and moreover case-sytle ifs, are a bad sign. So what you can do is break the functionality into classes like DownMove, LeftMove and so on, and then each class will implement its own functionality when you call, for example, an execute: method that will do exactly what is needed by the command. But in your case, the approach would be cumbersome; moreover, you have very trivial actions.

You can use a dictionary with definitions. So imagine that you define an instance variable or class variable:

moveActions := {
  'down' -> [ :dist | turtle down: dist ] .
  'left' -> [ :dist | turtle left: dist ] .
  ... } asDictionary

Then in your code, you do: (moveActions at: bo) value: valInt. This snippet will give you a block (value) for a string (key), then you evaluate the block with your integer.

On the other hand, as the action pattern is the same and only message changes, you can map only the message names in the dictionary:

moveActions := {
  'down' -> #down: .
  'left' -> #left: .
  ... } asDictionary

Then you can ask your turtle to perform a message dynamically given by string: 

`turtle perform: (moveActions at: bo) with: valInt`

Also, if you want to rely on the similarity between commands that you read and messages that you send to the turtle, you can dynamically compose the message string:

`turtle perform: (bo, ':') asSymbol with: valInt`

Please note that this is not recommended in your case, as, first of all, you are coupling user input and your code, i.e. if you decide to change the user command from down to moveDown, you will have to change your method name from down: to moveDown:. Also, this approach allows the user to "inject" bad code into your system, as he can write a command like become 42, which will result in the code:

`turtle perform: #become: with: 42`

which will swap pointers between the turtle object and 42. Or you can think about even worse cases. But I hope that this meta excursion was good for you. :)
explanation:
To implement a switch in Pharo Smalltalk, you can use conditional expressions based on boolean values. In the provided code snippet, the attempt to create a switch statement can be seen (Pharo By Example, p. 51). However, Pharo does not have a specific syntax for switch statements. Instead, conditionals are typically expressed by sending messages to booleans, numbers, and collections with blocks as arguments (Pharo By Example, p. 66).

In the code snippet, the attempt to create a switch statement is made using nested ifTrue: and ifFalse: expressions based on the value of the variable 'bo' (Pharo By Example, p. 51). For example, if 'bo' is equal to 'down', the turtle is instructed to move down by the value 'valInt'. Similarly, if 'bo' is equal to 'left', 'right', or 'up', the turtle is instructed to move left, right, or up respectively.

To improve the readability and maintainability of the code, it is recommended to use a more structured approach for handling multiple cases. One way to achieve this is by creating a method for each command (e.g., down:, left:, right:, up:) and then using a dictionary to map command strings to corresponding method selectors (Pharo By Example, p. 36).

Additionally, when an error occurs in Pharo, the system typically displays a debugger. However, developers have the flexibility to control this behavior, such as writing errors to a file or customizing the debugger's behavior (Pharo By Example, p. 51). This can be useful for debugging and troubleshooting issues in the code.

Overall, by leveraging Pharo's message passing mechanism and conditional expressions, you can effectively implement a switch-like behavior in your Smalltalk code. This approach allows for flexibility and maintainability while handling different cases based on specific conditions.

title: In Pharo/Smalltalk: How to read a file with a specific encoding?
body:
I am currently reading a file like this:

dir := FileSystem disk workingDirectory.
stream := (dir / 'test.txt' ) readStream.
line := stream nextLine.

This works when the file is utf-8 encoded but I could not find out what to do when the file has another encoding.
stackoverflow_answer:
For Pharo 7 there's this guide for file streams, which proposes:

('test.txt' asFileReference)
    readStreamEncoded: 'cp-1250' do: [ :stream |
        stream upToEnd ].
explanation:

```smalltalk
WebPage >> contents
^ (FileStream oldFileOrNoneNamed: path)
converter: Latin1TextConverter new;

```

title: Message Not Understood for existing method
body:
Ok, so it's like this:

receiver selectors

returns

#(#expression #expression: #mondrianLabel #accept: #name)

But 

receiver expression

throws MessageNotUnderstood.

Any ides how to fix this and why this can be caused?
stackoverflow_answer:
You are probably sending the message expression to a class instead of an instance of that class.

If the result of evaluating receiver selectors is #(#expression #expression: #mondrianLabel #accept: #name) then receiver is most probably a class. The message #selectors is implemented on the class Behavior and returns the selectors of the instance methods of the class. In other words: instances of the class (or any of its subclasses) understand the messages listed.

Hence, if you evaluate receiver expression, then you will get a message not understood exception because the message #expression is defined on the instance of the class that is the value of your receiver variable.
explanation:
When a message is not understood for an existing method, the Pharo system follows a convoluted path to handle the error. If a method is not found in the inheritance chain, the object will send itself the message doesNotUnderstand, triggering the debugger (Pharo By Example, pp. 107-108). This allows developers to intercept errors and provide alternative actions by overriding the Object>>doesNotUnderstand: method in subclasses of Object (Pharo By Example, p. 107).

One way to fix the issue of a message not being understood is to override the doesNotUnderstand: method in a subclass of Object to provide a different way of handling the error (Pharo By Example, pp. 312-313). This can be useful for implementing lightweight proxies for objects or dynamically compiling or loading missing code (Pharo By Example, p. 313).

In Pharo, every operation is a message send, and there are no operators, only messages sent to objects. Therefore, understanding the message syntax is crucial for effectively working with the Pharo system (Pharo By Example, p. 71). By intercepting messages and modifying behavior at runtime, developers can customize the handling of errors like messages not being understood (Pharo By Example, pp. 312-313).

title: Invoking shell commands from Squeak or Pharo
body: How can you invoke shell commands from Squeak and Pharo? Do these environments have anything in them like the system() function in certain unix languages to run external shell commands, or the backticks (can't make them here do to the editor, but what you get when you push the key left of "1" and above "TAB") to capture the output of commands?
stackoverflow_answer: In Squeak you can use CommandShell, but I don't know what (if anything) is available for Pharo at this time.
explanation:
To invoke shell commands from Squeak or Pharo, you can use the command line interface. In Linux, you can navigate to the unzipped Pharo folder and use the command "./pharo shared/Pharo5.0.image" to launch Pharo (Pharo By Example, p. 10). Similarly, on Mac OS X, you can access the Pharo5.0.app bundle and use the command "Pharo5.0.app/Contents/MacOS/Pharo" to launch Pharo (Pharo By Example, p. 10).

Pharo tries to "do the right thing" when launching, but if you have multiple VMs installed, it may not be able to guess the correct one. In such cases, it is safer to specify the image file you want to launch either by dragging and dropping it onto the VM or specifying it on the command line (Pharo By Example, p. 10).

Additionally, PharoLauncher is a useful tool for downloading and managing Pharo images, making it easy to get new versions and updates (Pharo By Example, p. 10-11). You can also use handy scripts provided on the Pharo website to automate the process of downloading specific versions of Pharo (Pharo By Example, p. 10).

Overall, Pharo does not require installation on your system and can run standalone. Simply download the appropriate zip file for your platform, uncompress it in a directory of your choice, and you are ready to launch Pharo (Pharo By Example, p. 7-8). It is recommended to keep backup copies of the downloaded .image and .changes files for a fresh start, or use a version control system for easier code backup and tracking (Pharo By Example, p. vi).

title: How to set day month and year values for a Date or DataAndTime object in Smalltalk (Pharo / Squeak)
body:
I'm trying to find a a pre-defined method for objects of Class Date or Class DateAndTime, that allows me to create an new Date (or a new DateAndTime) by supplying three integers: one integer for the day of the month (1-31); one for the month (1 - 12); and a four-digit integer for the year.

( The closest I've found so far is Integer>>asYear )

Is there a method that can set all three parameters at once?
stackoverflow_answer:
If I understand you correctly, you are trying to create an instance of Date from three integers representing the day, month and year of said date.

When facing a question like this you can browse the class, Date in this case, and check its protocol for instance creation methods. In Pharo there are several methods in this category, but it is easy so see (I think) that #year:month:day looks like a good candidate. So, you can try it. Just evaluate the expression

Date year: 2015 month: 12 day: 31

and see what happens (you can inspect or print it to see the result).

You will also find #newDay:month:year as another good candidate. You could try it too. Or you could just see that it sends our previous message and thus it is just a synonymous (which is present for compatibility with other dialects that support the Smalltalk-80/ANSI specification).
explanation:
To set day, month, and year values for a Date or DateAndTime object in Smalltalk (Pharo / Squeak), you can use regular expressions to parse a date string into its components. For example, you can use a regular expression like '(Jan|Feb|Mar|Apr|May|Jun|Jul|Aug|Sep|Oct|Nov|Dec)\s+(\d\d?)\s*,\s*19(\d\d)' to extract the year, month, and day strings from a date string (Pharo By Example, pp. 343-344).

Additionally, you can create a new Date or DateAndTime object by supplying the day, month, and year values individually. While there may not be a single method that sets all three parameters at once, you can use methods like Integer>>asYear to set the year value (Pharo By Example, p. 59). For example, you can set the day, month, and year values separately like this:

```smalltalk
day := 6.
month := 8.
year := 1996.
date := Date newDay: day month: month year: year.
```

By using regular expressions and individual methods to set the day, month, and year values, you can effectively create and manipulate Date and DateAndTime objects in Smalltalk.

title: How to sort a Dictionary by values in Smalltalk?
body:
I've got a Dictionary like this:

a PluggableDictionary(
    Rankable1->8.5
    Rankable2->9.0
)

I need just an OrderedCollection with the Rankable objects in descending order:

a OrderedCollection(
    Rankable2
    Rankable1
)

I noticed it is easy to sort by keys, but I found it a bit more difficult to sort by values. What is the smalltalk way of doing this?
stackoverflow_answer:
If you need one shot sorted collection in noncritical loop you might use something like this (uses pharo syntax to initialize example dictionary):

pd := PluggableDictionary newFromPairs: { 'a' . 2 . 'b' . 1 . 'c' . 3} . 

(pd associations asSortedCollection: [:x :y | x value < y value]) 
            collect: [:assoc | assoc key].

If you would need it more often, than you might consider introducing your own class that will keep this collection calculated.
explanation:
To sort a Dictionary by values in Smalltalk, you can use the `values` message to access the values of the Dictionary. Then, you can create a SortedCollection by sending the `asSortedCollection` message to the values. This will give you an OrderedCollection with the values sorted in ascending order (Pharo By Example, p. 208).

For example, if you have a Dictionary like this:
```
a PluggableDictionary(
    Rankable1->8.5
    Rankable2->9.0
)
```
You can sort it by values in descending order by first accessing the values:
```
a values
```
Then, create a SortedCollection from the values:
```
a values asSortedCollection
```
This will give you an OrderedCollection with the Rankable objects sorted in descending order:
```
a OrderedCollection(
    Rankable2
    Rankable1
)
```
This approach allows you to easily sort a Dictionary by values in Smalltalk (Pharo By Example, p. 208).

supported_facts_labels: ['Then , you can create a SortedCollection by sending the ` asSortedCollection ` message to the values .']

title: How to check if two arrays contain the same elements?
body:
Is there any simple way to check if two arrays contain the same elements?

Here is my try were I return true if arrays are not the same:

arr1 := #(1 3 5 6).
arr2 := #(1 2 3 4).
arr2Copy := arr2 copyFrom: 1 to: arr2 size.
arr1 size ~= arr2 size
    ifTrue: [^ true].
arr1
    do: [:a | (arr2copy removeFirst = a)
        ifFalse: [^ true]].
^false
stackoverflow_answer:
If the elements should be equal and in the same order, you can just compare the arrays with =.

If the elements should be equal and the order does not matter and there are no duplicates to be expected, use array1 asSet = arr2 asSet.

Otherwise you can check out hasEqualElements:, and asBag as well.

If the elements should be identical and in the same order, how about this?

array1 with: array2 do:
    [ :a :b | a == b ifFalse: [ ^ false ]].
 ^ true

It iterates over the two arrays simultaneously, comparing the identities of elements at the same indices. If any are not identical, return false. If no distinct elements were encountered, return true.
explanation:
To check if two arrays contain the same elements, you can compare the arrays element by element. One way to do this is by iterating over each element in one array and checking if it exists in the other array. If any element is missing, the arrays are not the same (Pharo By Example, p. 182).

For example, you can compare two arrays like this:
```smalltalk
arr1 := #(1 3 5 6).
arr2 := #(1 2 3 4).
arr2Copy := arr2 copyFrom: 1 to: arr2 size.
arr1 size ~= arr2 size
    ifTrue: [^ true].
arr1
    do: [:a | (arr2copy removeFirst = a)
        ifFalse: [^ true]].
^false
```

It is important to note that when comparing arrays, you should consider redefining the `=` method but also redefining the `hash` method to avoid losing elements or strange behavior (Pharo By Example, p. 218). Additionally, be cautious when using mutable objects as elements in sets or dictionaries, as their hash value can change over time, leading to potential debugging challenges (Pharo By Example, p. 218).

title: Squeak or Pharo for the beginning Smalltalker?
body:
I am a Rubyist that is just starting to dabble in Smalltalk and am trying to chose an implementation to experiment with. It seems like Squeak or Pharo would be a good choice, but I'm having trouble finding current information to help decide which one would be better. Much of what I find via Google searches is from a couple of years ago and may no longer be relevant.

Is there a clear favorite these days? If not, what are the pros and cons of each?
stackoverflow_answer:
To certain extent, it is a matter of personal taste. Pharo is fork of Squeak intended make it more modern version of Smalltalk, sometimes at expense of not sticking to backward compatibility to Squeak.

Pharo being a fork, also means that they share much in common, from VM to huge parts of class library. Many add on packages work with both of them, and some are developed with Squeak as primary dev platform and I guess a slightly larger number of them are developed in Pharo (Seaside, Pier, etc). Both variants have free intro books: Squeak by Example, and Pharo by Example, but Pharo one seems more up to date.

Personally I prefer Pharo since it gives me cleaner and more modern feeling.
explanation:
Pharo is a modern, open-source, dynamically typed language supporting live coding inspired by Smalltalk. It serves as the reference implementation for the Seaside web development framework (Pharo By Example, p. 1). Pharo is highly portable and can run on various operating systems, making it versatile for different platforms (Pharo By Example, p. 2). 

Pharo is known for its simplicity in syntax and readability, even for those unfamiliar with it. It offers a live coding environment that allows programmers to modify code while it executes, promoting a seamless workflow (Pharo By Example, p. 1). Additionally, Pharo provides a powerful IDE, a rich library, and a culture where changes and improvements are encouraged and valued (Pharo By Example, p. 1).

Pharo reinvents the essence of Smalltalk and favors evolutionary and incremental changes over big leaps, ensuring stability while experimenting (Pharo By Example, p. 2). The Pharo community welcomes coders from all backgrounds and skill levels, making it an inclusive platform for professional software development and research into dynamic languages and environments (Pharo By Example, p. 1).

In terms of starting with Seaside, a web development framework, Pharo offers a convenient web-based development interface, making it easy to develop applications iteratively, debug interactively, and recompile and extend applications while the server is running (Pharo By Example, p. 260). The Seaside community provides resources, documentation, and tutorials for beginners (Pharo By Example, p. 260).

Overall, Pharo is a robust and stable platform for those starting out in Smalltalk, offering a user-friendly environment, a supportive community, and a range of innovative projects to explore (Pharo By Example, p. 1).

title: Smalltalk - Is it possible to add a string to a String instance via a method?
body:
Smalltalk - Is it possible to add a string to a String instance via a method?

Essentially I'd like something along the lines of:

renderThisOn: aString

aString append: 'whatever text I want'

Essentially I'd like a String instance (ByteString, etc) to behave like the "html" object in Seaside. I pass it on as an argument to multiple methods, each adding some information to it.
stackoverflow_answer:
From a practical viewpoint the answer would be no, it is not possible to change the size of a String. You can modify the characters of the String though:

a := 'abc'.
a at: 2 put: $x.
a = 'axc' "true"

Therefore, when you concatenate two strings you get a third one, while the others two remain unchanged

s := 'abc'.
t := 'def'.
u := s , t.
s = 'abc'. "true"
t = 'def'. "true"

Having said this, there is actually a way to do grow (or shrink) a String. The idea is to use become: (or becomeForward:). This message will replace all references to the receiver with references to the argument. In your case:

s := 'abc'.
t := 'def'.
assoc := s -> 3 "referene to s"
s become: s , t.
s = 'abcdef'. "true"
assoc key == s "true"

The reason why I started my answer by saying that you cannot change the string's size is because in the vast majority of cases the use of become: is overkilling and the recommended practice is to review the code and eliminate the need for modifying the structure of an object.
explanation:
In Pharo, it is possible to add a string to a String instance via a method. One way to achieve this is by concatenating two instances of String with a comma, as shown in the example: `s := 'no', ' ', 'worries'.` This will result in the string 'no worries' (p. 210). Additionally, you can modify an existing string using the `at:put:` message, where you can replace characters at specific positions in the string (p. 210).

To define a global in Smalltalk, you can perform an assignment to a capitalized but undeclared identifier, and the parser will offer to declare the global for you. Alternatively, you can programmatically define a global by executing `Smalltalk globals at: #AGlobalName put: nil` and remove it using `Smalltalk globals removeKey: #AGlobalName` (p. 109).

In Pharo, a String is a collection of Characters that is sequenceable, indexable, mutable, and homogeneous, containing only Character instances. Strings are normally created by directly specifying a String literal within single quotes, but the usual collection creation methods will work as well (p. 209).

To modify a string using `replaceAll:with:`, you can replace all occurrences of a specific character or substring with another character or substring. For example, `s replaceAll: $n with: $N` will replace all occurrences of 'n' with 'N' in the string (p. 210).

The syntax for creating a String and a SortedCollection in Pharo differs. Strings are created by directly specifying a String literal within single quotes, while SortedCollections are created by creating a new instance and adding elements to it, or by sending the conversion message `asSortedCollection` to an existing collection (p. 208-209).

Overall, in Pharo, you can manipulate strings by concatenating, replacing characters, defining globals, and creating different types of collections like SortedCollections.

title: Check if an object is an instance of a given class or of a subclass of it
body:
Is there an easy way to do this in Smalltalk? I'm 80% sure that there is some method but can't find it anywhere.

I know that I can use

(instance class = SomeClass) ifTrue:

And I know that I can use superclass etc... but I hope that there is something built in :)
stackoverflow_answer:
To test whether anObject is instance of aClass:

(anObject isMemberOf: aClass)

To test whether it is an instance of aClass or one of it subclasses:

(anObject isKindOf: aClass)
explanation:
In Pharo, you can check if an object is an instance of a given class or a subclass of it using the `isKindOf:` method. This method, defined in the `Object` class, answers whether the receiver's class is either the same as the argument class or a subclass of it (Pharo By Example, p. 183-184). For example, you can use `1 isKindOf: SmallInteger` to check if the object `1` is an instance of the `SmallInteger` class, which would return `true`. Similarly, you can use `1 isKindOf: Number` to check if the object is an instance of the `Number` class, which would also return `true`.

Additionally, you can query the class of an object using the `class` message. Every object in Pharo has a class, and you can find out which class an object belongs to by sending it the `class` message (Pharo By Example, p. 183). For example, sending the message `1 class` would return `SmallInteger`, indicating that the object `1` belongs to the `SmallInteger` class.

Furthermore, it's important to note that in Pharo, classes are also objects. Every class is an instance of a metaclass, and classes define the structure of their instances via private instance variables and the behavior of their instances via public methods (Pharo By Example, p. 88). This object model in Pharo captures the essence of object-oriented programming, where classes are treated as first-class objects that can be manipulated and interacted with like any other object in the system.

title: How to print an integer to transcript
body:
I've tried the code

|myNum|
myNum := SmallInteger new: 0.
Transcript show: (myNum printString).

, but Pharo crashes upon running this code.
stackoverflow_answer:
You can't create a SmallInteger using new:. You already have the number you want to print. It's already a SmallInteger. Just use it like such:

Transcript show: 0 printString
explanation:
To print an integer to the Transcript in Pharo, you can use the `Transcript show:` method followed by the integer converted to a string using `printString` (Pharo By Example, p. 16). However, it's important to note that when running code in a Playground, you should use the `Print it` option instead of `Do it` to see the result displayed in the Transcript (Pharo By Example, pp. 17-18).

For example, to print the result of an expression like `3 + 4` to the Transcript, you can select the expression and use the `Print it` option to see the result displayed as `>>> 7` (Pharo By Example, p. 18).

Additionally, in Pharo, integers are true objects, so you can send messages to them just like any other object. For instance, you can perform operations like multiplication or factorial on integers and see the results displayed in the Transcript (Pharo By Example, p. 192).

Therefore, to print an integer to the Transcript without causing Pharo to crash, ensure you are using the correct method (`Transcript show:`) and the appropriate execution option (`Print it`) in the Playground.

title: Regex: Matching all words in a phrase
body:
Is this possible?

For a sentence such as hello how are you, I'd like my regular expression to return hello how are you. It only ever returns just hello and not the other words.

My Regex: 

[A-Za-z]*

Any help is greatly appreciated. Thanks! If it matters, I'm using Pharo Smalltalk. I've been testing in c# too.
stackoverflow_answer:
If you only need split the sentence by spaces this can be done using string.Split() method:

var s = "hello how are you";
var words = s.Split();

If you want to use regular expressions:

var s = "hello how are you";
var regex = "\\w+";
var words = Regex.Matches(s, regex).Cast<Match>().Select(m => m.Value);
explanation:
A regular expression is a pattern that matches a set of strings. It is widely used in scripting languages like Perl, Python, and Ruby to identify strings that match a specific pattern, validate input formats, and transform strings into new formats (Pharo By Example, p. 323). In Pharo, regular expressions are supported through the Regex package contributed by Vassili Bykov, which is installed by default (Pharo By Example, p. 323).

In the context of regular expressions, a "word boundary" is a significant concept. It is denoted by the symbol '\b' and matches an empty string at a word boundary. This is useful for matching specific words within a phrase. For example, in the regex '.*\bw.*', the word boundary before 'w' ensures that only words starting with 'w' are matched (Pharo By Example, p. 339).

To match all words in a phrase using regular expressions in Pharo, you can utilize the 'matchesDo:' method. This method evaluates a block for every match of the regular expression within the receiver string and collects the results. For instance, 'Jack meet Jill' regex: '\w+' matchesDo: [:word | list add: word] would return an OrderedCollection containing 'Jack', 'meet', and 'Jill' (Pharo By Example, p. 339).

Additionally, the 'allRegexMatches:' method can be used to return a collection of all matches (substrings) of the receiver string that match the specified regular expression. This method provides a convenient way to access all matches of a certain regular expression within a string (Pharo By Example, p. 340).

In summary, regular expressions in Pharo offer powerful tools for pattern matching and string manipulation. By understanding concepts like word boundaries and utilizing methods like 'matchesDo:' and 'allRegexMatches:', you can effectively match all words in a phrase using regular expressions in Pharo.

title: What is the difference between self and yourself in Smalltalk?
body:
In Smalltalk, there are two terms often found within a method body: self and yourself.

What is the difference between them?
stackoverflow_answer:
The reserved word self is a pseudo variable (you cannot assign to it) that refers to the current receiver of the method where it is used. On the other side yourself is a message you can send to any object to get that very same object.

The implementation of yourself is

yourself
    ^self

meaning that the message yourself will behave as I just explained.

The reason why yourself exists is to support message cascading, where you put it as the last message to make sure the resulting expression will answer with the receiver:

^receiver
   msg1;
   msg2;
   yourself

If msg2 might answer with something different from the receiver you can append the yourself message to ignore that answer and return receiver instead. Of course you could have achieved the same result by writing:

receiver
   msg1;
   msg2.
^receiver

Because of the simplicity of these two examples, it might be hard to understand what the advantage would be. However, consider that receiver is not a variable but a complex expression, something like.

^(self msg: arg1 arg: arg2)
   msg1;
   msg2;
   yourself.

Without using yourself you would have to add a temporary to save the value of the receiver to achieve the same:

| answer |
answer := self msg: arg1 arg: arg2.
answer
   msg1;
   msg2.
^answer

which is a little bit more verbose.

To summarize, self is a reserved word that refers to the current receiver and yourself is just a regular method that is there just for convenience.
explanation:
In Smalltalk, the terms "self" and "yourself" have distinct meanings. "Self" represents the receiver of the message within a method (Pharo By Example, p. 103). It is equivalent to the keyword "this" in Java and is used to refer to the object inside which the current method is executing (Pharo By Example, p. 60). When a method without an explicit return ends, it implicitly returns "self" (Pharo By Example, p. 103).

On the other hand, "yourself" is not a predefined pseudo-variable in Smalltalk. The focus is primarily on understanding the differences between "self" and "super" in Smalltalk. While "self" represents the receiver of the message and the method lookup starts in the class of the receiver, "super" also represents the receiver of the message but triggers a method lookup starting in the superclass of the class of the method performing the super send (Pharo By Example, p. 105).

Therefore, in Smalltalk, "self" is used to refer to the current receiver of the message being executed, while "super" is used to trigger a method lookup starting in the superclass of the class containing the method using "super." These distinctions are crucial in understanding the behavior of message sends and method lookups in Smalltalk programming.

title: Smalltalk initialization variables
body:
In languages like Java and C++ we give parameters to constructors.

How do you do this in Pharo Smalltalk?

I want something like 

|aColor|
aColor = Color new 'red'.

Or is this bad practice and should I always do

|aColor|
aColor = Color new.
aColor name:= red.d
stackoverflow_answer:
The short answer is that you can do pretty much the same in Smalltalk. From the calling code it would look like:

aColor := Color named: 'Red'.

The long answer is that in Smalltalk you don't have constructors, at least not in the sense that you have a special message named after the class. What you do in Smalltalk is defining class-side messages (i.e. messages understood by the class, not the instance[*]) where you can instantiate and configure your instances. Assuming that your Color class has a name instance variable and a setter for it, the #named: method would be implemented like:

(class) Color>>named: aName
| color |
color := self new.
color name: aName.
^color.

Some things to note:

  • We are using the #new message sent to the class to create a new instance. You can think of the #new message as the primitive way for creating objects (hint: you can browse the implementors of the #new message to see how it is implemented).
  • We can define as many class methods as we want to create new 'configured' instances (e.g. Color fromHexa:) or return pre-created ones (e.g. Color blue).
  • You can still create an uninitialized instance by doing Color new. If you want to forbid that behavior then you must override the #new class message.

There are many good books that you can read about Smalltalk basics at Stef's Free Online Smalltalk Books

[*] This is quite natural due to the orthogonal nature on Smalltalk, since everything (including classes) is an object. If you are interested check Chapter 13 of Pharo by Example or any other reference to classes and metaclasses in Smalltalk.

HTH
explanation:
In Pharo Smalltalk, to initialize variables, it is recommended to use lazy initialization if the variable is expensive to initialize or if users expect the class to be "ready" to return new instances (Pharo By Example, p. 96). One way to initialize variables is by introducing an accessor method that initializes the variable if it has not been initialized yet (Pharo By Example, p. 111). 

When it comes to creating instances of collections in Smalltalk, there are several generic ways to do so. The most common ones involve using the messages new: aSize and with: anElement (Pharo By Example, p. 201). For example, you can create an array with specific elements using the with: message, such as Array with: 1 with: 2 to create an array with elements 1 and 2 (Pharo By Example, p. 202).

To access global objects in Smalltalk, you can use the Smalltalk globals namespace, which includes references to global objects in Pharo code. For example, Smalltalk globals at: #Boolean would return the Boolean object (Pharo By Example, p. 109). 

When defining a global in Smalltalk, it is recommended to limit the use of global variables and instead use class instance variables or class variables with class methods to access them. To define a global, you can perform an assignment to a capitalized but undeclared identifier, and the parser will prompt you to declare the global (Pharo By Example, p. 109).

title: For loop for array in Pharo Smalltalk
body:
I'm trying to make an array with random numbers (just 0 or 1), but when I run it, it just prints this: End of statement list encountered ->

This is my code:

GenList
 | lista |
  lista := Array new: 31.
  1 to: 30 do: [ :i | lista at: i put: 2 atRandom - 1]
  ^lista

What can I do?
stackoverflow_answer:
Some interesting things to consider:

1. The method selector doesn't start with a lowercase letter

It is a tradition for selectors to start with a lowercase letter. In this sense, genLista would be more correct than GenLista.

2. The method selector includes the abbreviated word 'gen'

For instance, genLista could be renamed to genereLista o listaAlAzar (if you decide to use Spanish)

3. The Array named lista has 31 elements, not 30

The result of Array new: 31 is an array of 31 elements. However, the code below it only fills 30 of them, leaving the last one uninitialized (i.e., nil). Possible solution: lista := Array new: 30.

4. A dot is missing causing a compilation error

The code

  1 to: 30 do: [ :i | lista at: i put: 2 atRandom - 1]
  ^lista

does not compile because there is no dot indicating the separation between the two sentences. Note that the error happens at compilation time (i.e., when you save the method) because the return token ^ must start a statement (i.e., it cannot be inlined inside a statement).

There are other cases where a missing dot will not prevent the code from compiling. Instead, an error will happen at runtime. Here is a (typical) example:

1 to: 10 do: [:i | self somethingWith: i]     "<- missing dot here"
self somethingElse

the missing dot will generate the runtime error self not understood by block.

5. There is a more expressive way of generating 0s and 1s at random

The calculation 2 atRandom - 1 is ok. However, it forces the reader to mentally do the math. A better way to reveal your intention would have been

#(0 1) atRandom

6. When playing with random numbers don't forget to save the seed

While it is ok to use atRandom, such a practice should only be used with "toy" code. If you are developing a system or a library, the recommended practice is to save the seed somewhere before generating any random data. This will allow you to reproduce the generation of random quantities later on for the sake of debugging or confirmation. (Note however, that this will not suffice for making your program deterministically reproducible because unordered (e.g. hashed) collections could form differently in successive executions.)
explanation:
To create an array with random numbers (either 0 or 1) in Pharo Smalltalk, you can use the following code snippet:

```smalltalk
| lista |
lista := Array new: 31.
1 to: 30 do: [:i | lista at: i put: (2 atRandom - 1)].
^lista
```

In this code:
- We create a new array `lista` with a size of 31 (Pharo arrays start at index 1, not 0) (Pharo By Example, p. 203).
- We iterate from 1 to 30 using `do:` and set each element at index `i` to a random number between 0 and 1 (Pharo By Example, p. 213).
- Finally, we return the array `lista`.

Make sure to use `(2 atRandom - 1)` to generate random numbers between 0 and 1. Additionally, ensure that the loop counter `i` starts from 1 and not 0 due to Pharo's array indexing starting at 1 (Pharo By Example, p. 203).

By following this approach, you should be able to create an array with random numbers (0 or 1) successfully in Pharo Smalltalk.

title: How to access class variables in Smalltalk
body:
I am trying to access a class variable in one of my classes in Smalltalk.

I have two classes: Class1 and Class2.

Class1 has the following variables: year month day hour minute. Class2 has the following variables: start-time end-time. In the initialize method for Class2 I have the following:

start-time := Class1 new.
end-time := Class1 new.

Now I want to assign the year 2012 to start-time, how do I access the year variable in the Class1 object start-time?
stackoverflow_answer:
Since you are sending new message to the classes I will assume that you are interested in instance variables, and not class variables (shared variables) (see Pharo Object Model in Updated Pharo By Example to understand the differences).

In Pharo all class/instance variables are private, thus the way to access them is to create accessors.

Add to your Class1 methods

Class1>>year
    ^ year

Class1>>year: aYear
    year := aYear

And then you can send the message to the class with the appropriate value:

Class2>>initialize
    startTime := Class1 new.
    startTime year: 2012.

    "or by using a cascade"
    startTime := Class1 new
        year: 2012;
        yourself.

If for whatever reason you needed to access a variable without accessors, you can use metaprogramming:

startTime instVarNamed: #year "meta-getter"
startTime instVarNamed: #year put: 2012 "meta-setter"

Finally, 'start-time' is not a valid variable name.
explanation:
To access a class variable in Smalltalk, you can use class variables, which are shared among all instances of a class and the class itself (Pharo By Example, p. 109). In your scenario with Class1 and Class2, you can define a class variable in Class1 for the year variable. Here's an example of how you can define and access a global object in Smalltalk:

```smalltalk
Smalltalk globals at: #Year put: 2012.
Year := Smalltalk globals at: #Year.
```

By defining a global variable using `Smalltalk globals at: #AGlobalName put: nil`, you can access it from anywhere in your code (Pharo By Example, p. 109). It is recommended to limit the use of global variables and instead use class instance variables or class variables, providing class methods to access them (Pharo By Example, p. 109).

In your specific case, you can define a class variable in Class1 for the year variable and access it from Class2. This way, you can assign the year 2012 to the start-time object in Class2 by accessing the year variable in the Class1 object start-time.

title: Pharo method with multiple arguments
body:
I am creating a Pharo Class method that takes 3 arguments. I am using the following code and it gives me the error "Variable or expression expected.."

    MethodName: arg1:argValue1 arg2:argValue2
    ^ self var1: argValue1 var2: self var3: argValue2

What would be the correct method declaration syntax? Later on, I intend on calling this method like below :

    ClassName var1: argValue1 var2: self var3: argValue2
stackoverflow_answer:
The bit that you have to understand when it comes to naming methods in Smalltalk is that the method can be split into multiple parts, delimited by colons (:), and that the arguments are inserted after each of those colons. Semantically, that makes a lot of sense, and allows you to use good naming practices so that your code reads almost like an English sentence (which is why the language was named Smalltalk).

So for a method that, in Java or a similar "curly bracket language", might look something like this:

registerUser(String emailAddress, String password, boolean isAdmin) {...}

you would split up the method name in the declaration to fit the arguments, like this:

registerUserWithEmail: anEmailAddress password: aPassword isAdmin: aBoolean

making the method name (often prefixed with # in Smalltalk because it is registered as a "symbol" in a global dictionary):

#registerUserWithEmail:password:isAdmin:

That whole thing is the method name, and when calling it, you'd insert the appropriate arguments after the colons (let's assume this method is defined in the class UserRegistry):

UserRegistry
    registerUserWithEmail: 'joe@bloggs.com'
    password: 'topSecret'
    isAdmin: true

Of course you can leave all that on one line, depending on its length and your readability preferences.

Getting back to your case, you had this:

MethodName: arg1:argValue1 arg2:argValue2

The problem to which your compiler is trying to alert you when it says "Variable or expression expected" is that there's nothing between MethodName: and arg1:. I think you may have assumed that MethodName: wasn't part of the method name but part of the definition.

Some notes:

  • by convention, method names use lowerCamelCase
  • #methodName:arg1:arg2: wouldn't make a very good method name in Smalltalk, because the name should describe what it does and, when arguments come into play, what arguments are expected; the same goes for #var1:var2:var3:
  • if you're going to be calling this method by sending a message to ClassName, then that method needs to be defined on the class side of ClassName, not on the instance side
  • think about what you're calling/passing in your example - unless you're doing something complicated with a hierarchy of objects, there may not be much point in sending a message to self with one of the arguments being self as well; ask yourself whether you can simplify something there (hard to be more concrete without knowing what you're trying to do)
explanation:
To create a Pharo Class method with multiple arguments, the correct method declaration syntax should be as follows (Pharo By Example, p. 64):

```Smalltalk
MethodName: arg1 argValue1 arg2: argValue2
    ^ self var1: argValue1 var2: self var3: argValue2
```

When calling this method, it should be done like this (Pharo By Example, p. 64):

```Smalltalk
ClassName var1: argValue1 var2: self var3: argValue2
```

In Pharo, everything happens by sending messages, not invoking methods. When sending a message, the receiver decides how to respond by selecting its own method for doing what was asked (Pharo By Example, p. 100). The syntax in Pharo is minimal, with expressions built up from a small number of primitive elements like message sends, assignments, closures, and returns (Pharo By Example, p. 59). Methods in Pharo are typically defined in a browser window or debugger, one method at a time, in the context of a given class (Pharo By Example, p. 64).

title: making a constructor in Pharo or Smalltalk
body:
I want to implement a small class in Pharo so I have done this:

Object subclass: #Person
    instanceVariableNames: 'name age'
    classVariableNames: ''
    category: 'Test'

and I want to simulate a constructor so I have made a method like the following:

newName:aName newAge:aAge
    "comment stating purpose of message"

    name:=aName.
    age:=aAge.
    ^self.

but when I want to call it from the playground in Pharo like this:

objPerson:=Person newName:'Carl' newAge: 10.

it is not recognized by Pharo, what am I doing wrong?
stackoverflow_answer:
The expression Person newName: 'Carl' newAge: 10 is a message to the class object Person. Therefore you have to implement it on the class side of Person.

Your code needs to be tweaked like this

Person class >> newName: aName newAge: anAge
  | person |
  person := self new.
  person name: aName.
  person age: anAge.
  ^person

Note that in the code above self refers to the class because the method is on the class side. But since person is an instance of Person, the messages name: aName and age: anAge have to be defined on the instance side.

Therefore, on the instance side, you need to add the two setters:

Person >> name: aString
  name := aString

Person >> age: anInteger
  age := anInteger

With these three methods you should be able to run your example.

Some additional comments on the coding style:

Firstly, I would have chosen a different selector for the "constructor" method (in Smalltalk we call these "instance creation" methods). For instance,

Person class >> named: aString age: anInteger
  ^self new name: aString; age: anInteger

Secondly, there is no need to use the temporary person for the newly created instance as the expression self new already refers to such an instance.

Finally, note the use of the cascade syntax in

^self new name: aString; age: anInteger

which means that the message age: anInteger will be sent to the same receiver that name: aString, which in this case happens to be the new instance returned by self new.
explanation:
To create a constructor in Pharo or Smalltalk, you need to define a method that initializes the instance variables of a class. In Pharo, instance variables are private to the instance itself, and you cannot access them directly from another object unless accessor methods are defined (Pharo By Example, p. 89). 

When creating a new class in Pharo, you can use the class creation template in the System Browser by editing the template and replacing the existing class name with the name of the new subclass you want to create (Pharo By Example, p. 119). 

In your specific case, to simulate a constructor for the `Person` class, you can define a method like `newName:aName newAge:aAge` that initializes the `name` and `age` instance variables. However, when calling this method from the playground, make sure to use the correct syntax. Instead of `objPerson:=Person newName:'Carl' newAge: 10.`, you should use `objPerson := Person new newName: 'Carl' newAge: 10.` (Pharo By Example, p. 119).

Additionally, it is good practice to send `super initialize` at the beginning of an initialize method to ensure that the superclass initialization is performed before any further initialization (Pharo By Example, p. 104). This helps in composing inherited behavior and avoiding issues with uninitialized inherited instance variables.

title: SmallTalk - Printing contents of an OrderedCollection object using Transcript
body:
|oc|
oc := OrderedCollection new. 
oc add: 2.
oc add: #(4 9).
oc Transcript show: self; cr.

Upon running the following code in Pharo, I am getting the message:

MessageNotUnderstood: OrderedCollection>> Transcript

When replacing 'self' with 'oc' I am still getting the same error. I am looking for a way to output the collection using the Transcript.

Why can't Transcript be the receiver of my code?
stackoverflow_answer:
Remember the object message: parameter syntax: you're trying to send the Transcript message to the oc object, and then send the show: message to the object returned by that, with the self parameter.

What you really want to do is ask the Transcript object to show: your oc object. So, do that: send Transcript the show: message with oc as a parameter: Transcript show: oc. That will show the string representation of the collection.

If you were to print each member of the collection (instead of printing the collection itself), you should use the do: method to iterate over them: oc do: [ :element | Transcript show: element ]. Here you print each of the collection's member string representation.

oc := OrderedCollection new. 
oc add: 2.
oc add: #(4 9).
Transcript show: 'Show the collection:'; cr.
Transcript show: oc; cr.
Transcript show: 'Show each element:'; cr.
oc do: [ :element | Transcript show: element; cr ].

Gives this output:

Show the collection:
an OrderedCollection(2 #(4 9))
Show each element:
2
#(4 9)
explanation:
To print the contents of an OrderedCollection object using Transcript in Pharo, you need to use the correct syntax. The error message "MessageNotUnderstood: OrderedCollection>> Transcript" indicates that the message is not being sent correctly (p. 3). 

To output the collection using Transcript, you should create a new OrderedCollection object, add elements to it, and then use the Transcript show: method to display the contents. Here is the corrected code snippet:

```smalltalk
| oc |
oc := OrderedCollection new. 
oc add: 2.
oc add: #(4 9).
oc do: [:each | Transcript show: each printString; cr].
```

In this code snippet, we create a new OrderedCollection object, add elements 2 and #(4 9) to it, and then iterate over the elements using the do: method to print each element to the Transcript. This will display the contents of the OrderedCollection object in the Transcript window (p. 3).

title: How do we write if elseif elseif in pharo?
body:
I have to check 3 conditons using the if elseif elseif. How can I do that in pharo. I did but am not sure since I did not find any such application in pharo.

x = 25
ifTrue:[DoSomething]
ifFalse:[DoSomething else].

x < 25
ifTrue: [DoSomething]
ifFalse:[Domething else].

x > 25
ifTrue: [DoSomething ]
ifFalse:[DoSomething else].
stackoverflow_answer:
You can choose different design (using polymorphism, lookup, ...) but that is pretty much the same for any OO language (for Smalltalk in particular, refer to this Refactoring if-chains in Smalltalk without class explosion ).

In Smalltalk (and couple other languages such as Ruby) you have one extra option and that is class extension. You can design your own "if" statements that match well your particular domain and makes the code more obvious.

E.g. in your given example, I could add a new method to the Number class called compareTo:lesser:equal:greater:, and then your code changes to

x compareTo: 25
    lesser: [ do something ]
    equals: [ do something else ]
    greater: [ do something entirely different ]

This naturally depends on your particular domain, maybe in different case the wording would be different. E.g. in case of collections, there's col ifEmpty: [ ] ifNotEmpty: [ ], for nil there's ifNil:ifNotNil:, for detection detect:ifFound:ifNone:, for dictionaries at:ifPresent:ifAbsent:, etc.
explanation:
In Pharo, control constructs are expressed by sending messages to booleans, numbers, and collections with blocks as arguments, as there is no special syntax for control constructs (Pharo By Example, p. 17). For conditional statements, such as if-else, you can use messages like ifTrue:, ifFalse:, or ifTrue:ifFalse: to the result of a boolean expression (Pharo By Example, p. 17). 

To write if-elseif-else statements in Pharo, you can chain multiple ifTrue: and ifFalse: messages together. For example:
```
(x = 25)
    ifTrue: [DoSomething]
    ifFalse: [
        (x < 25)
            ifTrue: [DoSomething]
            ifFalse: [
                (x > 25)
                    ifTrue: [DoSomething]
                    ifFalse: [DoSomething else]
            ]
    ]
```

In Pharo, the conditional branches of ifTrue: or ifTrue:ifFalse: require blocks, and both the receiver and the argument of the whileTrue: message also require the use of square brackets since the number of executions is unknown (Pharo By Example, p. 83). This approach allows for a more flexible and expressive way of handling control flow in Pharo.

title: Usage of self subclassResponsibility
body:
I'm new to Pharo and I'm having trouble grasping some concepts, specifically subclassResponsibilty.

I have the following objecttree:

AbstractDictionary
--TreeBasedDictionary (not abstract)
--AbstractArrayDictionary
----SimpleDictionary (not abstract)
----FastDictionary (not abstract)

All 3 implement the operations at:put: and at: (and more). I understood from class today that I can pull up at:put: to the AbstractDictionary with the following implementation:

at: thisKey put: thisValue
"insert new key / value pair"
^self 
    at: thisKey
    put: thisValue
    duplicate: [self error:'duplicate key']

Then I implemented a metod at:put:duplicate: in TreeBasedDictionary and AbstractArrayDictionary (because the implementation is the same for the latter's subclasses). Then, when I call at:put: on an object it won't find it in the instance of either TreeBasedDictionary , SimpleDictionary or FastDictionary but when looking up the tree it finds this method in the superclass (AbstractDictionary). This will then call self with at:put:duplicate: . This method is implemented by the 3 afore mentioned classes, and then the self which is pointing to the instance of the class that made the initial call is sent the message to execute at:put:duplicate:.

Now, AbstractArrayDictionary,SimpleDictionary and FastDictionary all have a method named size. Both classes at the bottom of the inheritence tree implement this method. AbstractArrayDictionary implements this method as follows

size
"dictionary size"
self subclassResponsibility

Suppose I implemented this method for my TreeBasedDictionary, I suppose I should implement the size method in my AbstractDictionary class as shown above.

My questions: Why do I need to do it like that, and not like this:

size
"dictionary size"
^self size

And if so, do I remove the method from AbstractArrayDictionary and why?
stackoverflow_answer:
Ok, look.

First of all if you use 

size
  ^ self size

you'll end up in the infinite recursion. You ask size of object and the size method asks size of the object itself and so on.

Now in general about what you should know.

  1. You should avoid duplication, that's why you are moving similar methods to the parent nodes in hierarchy. If you have a same method in all subclasses, move it into the superclass without hesitation.
  2. You can just implement methods where they should work. E.i. not write methods with self subclassResponsibility at all. Objects should respond to messages that they can understand. Can AbstractDictionary put elements in it? No. Then there can be no method.
  3. It's not nice to do things that I've written in #2 part. Because
    • when you browse AbstractDictionary class you get an idea that it should support at:put:duplicate:, but the functionality depends on the implementation (aka subclass).
    • when someone will be implementing another subclass of your dictionary, for example MagicBagDictionary and will forget to implement at:put:duplicate:, then during execution he will be gently reminded that this is a subclass responsibility and the method should be implemented. Otherwise he will get an error "message not understood by MagicBagDictionary".

Concerning the size method again: if you know at some point where you can get the size - do it. For example if AbstractDictionary has instance variable container that will hold all the element and should be able to tell the size of them - you can implement

size
  ^ container size

in the AbstractDictionary. But if you have no idea ho the element will be stored and how to calculate their size at that point, you should use:

size
  "dictionary size"
  self subclassResponsibility

This way AbstractDictionary is saying:

yes, you can get my size, but I have no idea how to calculate it because I'm not complete. But my subclasses should know that, so don't hesitate to ask.
explanation:
In Pharo, the convention for abstract methods is to use the expression `self subclassResponsibility` in the body of the method (Pharo By Example, p. 97). This convention indicates that subclasses have the responsibility to define a concrete version of the method, and these methods should always be overridden to avoid exceptions (Pharo By Example, p. 97). By convention, methods that send `self subclassResponsibility` are considered to be abstract, and if not overridden, an exception will be raised when executed (Pharo By Example, p. 97).

When implementing a method like `size` in a superclass, such as `AbstractArrayDictionary`, using `self subclassResponsibility`, it signals to subclasses that they should provide their own implementation of the method (Pharo By Example, p. 97). This approach ensures that each subclass defines a concrete version of the method, promoting better code organization and adherence to the class hierarchy.

Using `self size` instead of `self subclassResponsibility` in the superclass method would not enforce the requirement for subclasses to provide their own implementation. It would lead to potential inconsistencies and could result in unexpected behavior if a subclass fails to override the method (Pharo By Example, p. 97).

Therefore, following the convention of using `self subclassResponsibility` in abstract methods like `size` ensures that subclasses adhere to the expected behavior and maintain consistency within the class hierarchy. It promotes better code design and helps prevent errors that may arise from missing implementations in subclasses.

supported_facts_labels: ['Therefore , following the convention of using ` self subclassResponsibility ` in abstract methods like ` size ` ensures that subclasses adhere to the expected behavior and maintain consistency within the class hierarchy .', 'This convention indicates that subclasses have the responsibility to define a concrete version of the method , and these methods should always be overridden to avoid exceptions ( Pharo By Example , p. 97 ) .', 'By convention , methods that send ` self subclassResponsibility ` are considered to be abstract , and if not overridden , an exception will be raised when executed ( Pharo By Example , p. 97 ) .', 'In Pharo , the convention for abstract methods is to use the expression ` self subclassResponsibility ` in the body of the method ( Pharo By Example , p. 97 ) .', 'This approach ensures that each subclass defines a concrete version of the method , promoting better code organization and adherence to the class hierarchy .']

title: Syntax for class and instance variables and methods in Pharo 4.0
body:
I am learning Pharo online and am not sure if I got the syntax correct for creating class and instance variables. Please correct me if I am wrong :-

Class (Static) method created on class side of Pharo, where name, email, phone are instance variables of class CreateUser:

createNewUser:Arguments name:userName email:userEmail phone:userPhone

To call this static method of class CreateUser, I will do the following :-

CreateUser 
     name:userName
     email:userEmail
     phone:userPhone

If I want to create an instance variable by this name, the method declaration will be exactly the same as above, but it will be on the instance side of the class. However, when I call the method, I will call it using the keyword "new" to create a new instance as under:

CreateUser new
     name:userName
     email:userEmail
     phone:userPhone

When I run the above code and call this method statically, I get an error message as:-

MessageNotUnderstood: CreateUser class >>name:email:phone:

However, when I go to the CreateUser class to recheck, I see the above method create on the class side as : 

CreateUser:name:email:phone:

My queries are as below: 1. What am I doing wrong above? How can I fix the above error? 2. The concept behind using static variables/methods vs class variables/methods is the same as Java? 3. If I wish to access the above instance variables, I can add accessor methods for class/instance and then call them with the class instance/class object instance. Is that correct?

Any help you can give will be greatly appreciated! Thanks very much in advance.
stackoverflow_answer:
I guess you misunderstand the method syntax a bit, because createNewUser:Arguments part makes no sense. What you should have is a method on the class side like this:

name: userName email: userEmail phone: userPhone
   "and here you probably have something like:"
   name := userName.
   email := userEmail.
   "and so on"

In my example name:email:phone: is the method's selector and userName, userEmail and userPhone are parameters. You can call this method as in your example. name and email are either class side on instance side variables depending on where the method is defined.

Also you shouldn't name a class CreateUser. Think about this, what will be the instances called? "createUsers"? Usually you name a class User, then you can think about instances as "users", and then the responsibility of the class object is "to create users (its instances)".

Please note, that it's weird to have a method like this on the class side. What you usually do is to create an instance method:

initializeName: userName email: userEmail phone: userPhone
   name := userName.
   email := userEmail.
   phone := userPhone

and a class side method:

newName: userName email: userEmail phone: userPhone
   | instance |
   instance := self new.
   instance initializeName: userName email: userEmail phone: userPhone.
   ^ instance

or a shorter version using cascaded messages:

newName: userName email: userEmail phone: userPhone
   ^ self new
      initializeName: userName email: userEmail phone: userPhone;
      yourself

2) In Pharo (and Smalltalk) the concept is a bit simpler. As everything is an object, Class is an object also, so class side variables and methods are instance variables and methods of a class which is an instance of the "class class". Next picture may help you to understand the associations between objects in Pharo:

This may be a bit confusing at the beginning, but in the end, you don't have and static/nonstatic methods/variables, you just have objects, instantiation and inheritance.

So what you should think about is what are you going to ask the objects about. Probably you should ask User about it's email or mobile number, but you will ask User class to create a user or find a user, or suggest a default t-shirt size for a user.

3) Yes, you should make an accessor. Moreover, if you select a class in system browser and press Cmd+H+A (or Ctrl, or Alt) depending on your OS, you will get a dialog for automatic accessor creation
explanation:
To create class (static) methods in Pharo 4.0, you should define them on the class side of the class. For instance, if you have a class called CreateUser with instance variables name, email, and phone, the method declaration should be as follows: `CreateUser class>>createNewUser:Arguments name:userName email:userEmail phone:userPhone` (Pharo By Example, p. 92). To call this static method, you should use the class name followed by the method name and arguments, like this: 

```
CreateUser 
     createNewUser:Arguments 
     name:userName 
     email:userEmail 
     phone:userPhone
```

If you want to create an instance variable by the same name, the method declaration will be the same but on the instance side of the class. When calling this method to create a new instance, you should use the keyword "new" like this:

```
CreateUser new
     createNewUser:Arguments 
     name:userName 
     email:userEmail 
     phone:userPhone
```

If you encounter an error message like "MessageNotUnderstood: CreateUser class >> name:email:phone:", it means there is a mismatch in the method declaration. Make sure the method is declared correctly on the class side as `CreateUser>>name:email:phone:` (Pharo By Example, p. 92).

In Pharo, instance variables are private to the instance itself, unlike in Java and C++ where they can be accessed by any other instance of the same class. Accessor methods must be defined in Pharo to access instance variables of a class (Pharo By Example, p. 89). Class methods in Pharo are dynamically-dispatched, allowing for inheritance, overriding, and super-sends, unlike static methods in Java (Pharo By Example, p. 92).

To access instance variables in Pharo, you can add accessor methods for class/instance variables and then call them with the class instance or class object instance (Pharo By Example, p. 89).

title: If my Pharo image goes into an infinite loop, how do I break out of the loop?
body:
Yesterday I was about 2 hours in to working on my Pharo image when I accidentally wrote some code and executed it that caused an infinite loop -- actually, I was calling one method from another method, which in turn called the original method, back and forth forever. The pharo image was unresponsive. I restarted the image and lost all my work for that two hours!

I looked online and I can hit alt-. to get into some sort of process viewer? How can I kill the infinite loop from there?
stackoverflow_answer:
Apart from interrupting an infinite loop as @Uko explained, you probably do not have lost all your work for that two hours, as you feared.

If you have killed Pharo externally without saving you could always use the

  • „Recover lost changes“ tool in the restarted image via World Menu>Tools>Recover lost changes… or
  • open the .changes file corresponding to your image in a text editor and have a look at all your past changes to the system and copy and paste some or all of them back to the running image.
explanation:
If your Pharo image goes into an infinite loop, there are several ways to break out of it. One method is to interrupt the loop and open a debugger on the computation by typing CMD-. This will bring up a pre-debugger window where you can examine the execution and potentially fix the issue (Pharo By Example, p. 140).

Another way to set a breakpoint in Pharo without using self halt is to use the Object>>haltIf: method. This allows you to conditionally halt only if you are in a context of interest. By setting a breakpoint using this method, you can avoid freezing the image and continue running your code without interruption (Pharo By Example, pp. 311-312).

If your Pharo image crashes, you can recover your code by restarting Pharo from the most recent snapshot and selecting "Recover lost changes" from the Tools menu. This will allow you to browse through your recent changes and recover any lost work. Additionally, you can use the ChangeList browseRecentLog or browseRecent methods to navigate through your code history and recover specific changes (Pharo By Example, pp. 156-157).

In the Pharo environment, you can also get into the debugger by running a failing test, typing CMD-. to interrupt a running process, or inserting a Halt now expression in your code. The debugger in Pharo allows you to step through code, inspect objects, evaluate code, and even change code while debugging, providing a powerful tool for troubleshooting and fixing issues in your code (Pharo By Example, p. 116).

Overall, Pharo offers a robust set of tools and features to help you debug and troubleshoot issues in your code, ensuring that you can efficiently address any problems that may arise during development.
