title: creation of a class in Pharo Smalltalk?
body:
I have been following the example of making a class in Pharo from the following link: https://ci.inria.fr/pharo-contribution/job/UpdatedPharoByExample/lastSuccessfulBuild/artifact/book-result/PharoObjectModel/PharoObjectModel.html#fig:colorInstanceClassSide

It is the example of making a Dog and a Hyena class. First I created a package called TestC and in the Instance class I have made the following:

For what I know, and correct me if I am wrong, the instance side is where I create the methods that will work when I instantiate an object while the class side it does not need an object to be created to function; just like a static method class in Java.

The first question that I have at this point is why after Accepting the Changes it stills appear the ! symbol in the left part of my classes?

According to the tutorial then I should put the following code:

Dog class
    instanceVariableNames: 'count'

Now I did not where to put it, on the instance side or in the class side, I decided to put it on the instance side the following:

and the last methods that I have are the following, I put them on the class side:

I have tested the classes in the Transcript with the following code:

aDog := Dog new.
Dog count.
bDog := Dog new.
Dog count.

and it works, but I would like to know if I made the right decision to put those methods in the class side (second question), and if its right could anybody give me an example of a method to put in the instance side in this example?

Thanks
stackoverflow_answer:
First Question

The exclamation mark ! in front of the class is an invitation to click on it. In this case it will remind you that classes are supposed to have comments (for the sake of documentation).

Digression

Every class defines both the shape (or structure) and the behavior of its instances. The shape is given by the instance variables in the class creation message, such as

Object subclass: #Dog
  instanceVariableNames: 'name breed birthdate'
  classVariableNames: ''
  package: 'TestC'

For the behavior you create methods in the instance side of the class

breed: aString
  breed := aString

With this you have a full functional object

dog := Dog new.
dog name: 'Taylor'; breed: 'Great Dane'

However, classes are objects too. So, they may have their own shape. To add an instance variable to a class's shape you send the message:

Dog class instaceVariableNames: 'count'

Note that the receiver of this message is the class of the Dog class (a Metaclass), which makes sense because it is classes who shape their instances.

In your example, every time you create a new (instance of) Dog the count ivar of the Dog class will increase by 1. This could serve the propose of keeping track of how many instances of Dog have been created so far. These instances, however, will remain unaware of this fact.

Second Question

Yes, the ivar count and the methods to initialize, increment and retrieve it belong in the class side. Why? Because they are intended to keep track of the number of instances your class has created. Whether this is useful or not, is a different question; the fact is that they would have made no sense in the instance side (after all, lucky dogs live exciting lives without ever counting anything.)
explanation:
To create a class in Pharo Smalltalk, you need to write Smalltalk code in the System Browser. You can create a new class by editing the class creation template, replacing Object with the existing class name you want to subclass and NameOfSubclass with the desired name for your new subclass. You can also define instance variables and methods within the class (Pharo By Example, pp. 119-120).

In Pharo, the instance side is where you create methods that work when you instantiate an object, while the class side does not require an object to function, similar to static methods in Java (Pharo By Example, various pages).

When creating a new class, it is important to understand that each class in Pharo inherits its behavior and structure from a single superclass. The root of the inheritance hierarchy is ProtoObject, but classes should normally inherit from Object or its subclasses. Classes in Pharo inherit from a single superclass, supporting single inheritance (Pharo By Example, pp. 96-97).

Instance variables in Pharo are private to the instance itself, and two instances of the same class cannot access each other's instance variables unless accessor methods are defined. Access to instance variables is through instance methods of the class that defines them, similar to protected variables in C++ and Java (Pharo By Example, pp. 89).

To define class instance variables, each class has its own private set of variables, and subclasses inherit these variables but have their own private copies. Class instance variables are accessed through class methods, providing each class with its own set of private variables (Pharo By Example, pp. 92).

In summary, to create a class in Pharo Smalltalk, you need to write Smalltalk code in the System Browser, define instance variables and methods, understand inheritance from a single superclass, and manage instance and class variables appropriately.

title: How can getter/setter code be generated automatically for a class in Pharo or Squeak?
body:
I have a long list of instance variables to create for a class that I want to generate the code for, rather than do it by hand. The list comes from an existing SQL database. My intention is to do it all in a pure object-oriented way with Smalltalk first, and as I learn more, save the data back to the database and work from it directly.

Is there a way of passing the list of names to method that will generate them and add them to the class definition?

In fact is there a way of adding or modifying class definitions dynamically in Smalltalk? I suspect there must and I would like to know a best practices approach.

Update: What I have in mind is more like passing a list of the instance variables to a method that will create them automatically.

It is more like:

addVariablesAndAccessors className: MyClass variablesList: ('aaaa', 'bbbb', 'cccc')

which will then result in a call to

AddVariables className: MyClass variableList: ('aaaa' 'bbbb' cccc')

and

generateAccessors className: MyClass variableList: ('aaaa' 'bbbb' cccc')
stackoverflow_answer:
In Squeak, you have Behavior>>addInstVarName: aString, so for instance, you could do something like:

String addInstVarName: 'foo'

Squeak also has refactoring support to generate accessors automatically. You can either use it directly or have a look at AbstractInstanceVariableRefactoring>>createAccessors to get some inspiration on how to implement your own ;-)

Another quite hacky but not so uncommon solution would be to just generate the instance variables, but instead of adding accessors, you overwrite doesNotUnderstand:, which gets called when an undefined selector is sent to your objects. There, you could check if you have an instance variable named according to the message, and return / change it if it is the case. Otherwise you just do super doesNotUnderstand: aMessage.

Regarding your comment: Classes are objects, too, so you don't have to do anything special to use them as parameters. On which class you add it is totally up to you and doesn't really matter. So a method to add instance variables could look like this:

addVariablesNamed: aCollection on: aClass

    aCollection do: [:each | aClass addInstVarName: each]

and you could call it like this:

yourObject addVariablesNamed: #('foo' 'bar' 'baz') on: ClassX

You can find examples on how to generate accessor methods in the class CreateAccessorsForVariableRefactoring
explanation:
To generate getter/setter code automatically for a class in Pharo or Squeak, you can follow the convention where a getter method has the same name as the variable it gets, and a setter is named similarly but with a trailing ":" (Pharo By Example, p. 49). You can dynamically add accessors for instance variables on demand by creating a class that automatically adds accessors for its instance variables (Pharo By Example, p. 315).

To define instance variables and methods in Pharo, you need to write Smalltalk code. You can create a new class by editing a class creation template, replacing the superclass name and subclass name, and filling in the instance variable names (Pharo By Example, pp. 119-120). Instance variables in Pharo are private to the instance itself, and accessors must be defined if access is needed (Pharo By Example, p. 89).

You can access instance variables by name in any instance method of the class that defines them or in methods defined in its subclasses (Pharo By Example, p. 89). Class instance variables are private to the class itself, and subclasses inherit their own private copies of these variables (Pharo By Example, p. 92).

In Pharo, class methods are dynamically-dispatched methods, allowing for inheritance, overriding, and super-sends, unlike static methods in Java (Pharo By Example, p. 92). By following the convention of sending messages rather than calling methods, you can interact with objects in Pharo (Pharo By Example, p. 113).

title: Smalltalk - Print something for each 2 seconds
body:
I'm coding in Smalltalk using Pharo. My code:

|i delay|
i := 0.
[i < 5] whileTrue: [
    [
        delay := Delay forSeconds: 2.
        delay wait.
        Transcript show: '2 seconds are up'.
        Transcript cr.
    ] fork.
    i := i + 1.
]

It prints all the "2 seconds are up" at once, and not for each 2 seconds:

2 seconds are up
2 seconds are up
2 seconds are up
2 seconds are up
2 seconds are up

Please, can anyone tell me how to print something for each 2 seconds in Smalltalk?
stackoverflow_answer:
You forked all 5 processes at once so that all 5 delays ends at the same time. To print every 2 seconds from the background, rather fork a whole loop in a separate process:

|i delay|
[   
    i := 0.
    [i < 5] whileTrue: [
        delay := Delay forSeconds: 2.
        delay wait.
        Transcript show: '2 seconds are up'.
        Transcript cr.
        i := i + 1]
] fork
explanation:
To print something for each 2 seconds in Smalltalk using Pharo, you can use the Delay class to create a delay of 2 seconds and then print a message. In your code snippet, you are forking multiple processes that all wait for 2 seconds and then print the message simultaneously, which is why you see all the messages printed at once (Pharo By Example, pp. 227-228).

Here is an example of how you can modify your code to print a message for each 2 seconds:
```
| i delay |
i := 0.
[i < 5] whileTrue: [
    delay := Delay forSeconds: 2.
    delay wait.
    Transcript show: '2 seconds are up'.
    Transcript cr.
    i := i + 1.
]
```
This code snippet will print "2 seconds are up" every 2 seconds for a total of 5 times, with a delay between each message (Pharo By Example, pp. 227-228).

In Smalltalk, you can access global objects using the Smalltalk globals namespace. For example, you can access the Boolean class by using:
```
Smalltalk globals at: #Boolean
```
This will return the Boolean class object. Similarly, you can access the Smalltalk object itself using:
```
Smalltalk globals at: #Smalltalk
```
This will return the Smalltalk object, which is a global variable representing the Smalltalk environment (Pharo By Example, pp. 108-109).

To define a global variable in Smalltalk, you can simply assign a value to a capitalized but undeclared identifier. The parser will offer to declare the global for you. For example, to define a global named "AGlobalName", you can execute:
```
Smalltalk globals at: #AGlobalName put: nil
```
To remove a global variable, you can use:
```
Smalltalk globals removeKey: #AGlobalName
```
It is recommended to limit the use of global variables in favor of class instance variables or class variables, and provide class methods to access them (Pharo By Example, pp. 109).

In Smalltalk, you can quickly execute code using the Playground tool. Simply open a Playground, enter your code snippet, and then right-click and select "Do it" to execute the code. The Playground allows you to write and evaluate snippets of code easily (Pharo By Example, pp. 13-14).

For running tests in Smalltalk, the recommended practice is to use the SUnit testing framework. You can write test methods in a Playground or as comments in your code, and then run them using the Test Runner tool. The Test Runner allows you to run individual test methods or entire test suites, and provides detailed feedback on the test results (Pharo By Example, pp. 159-160, 164).

Overall, Smalltalk in Pharo provides a powerful and dynamic environment for coding, testing, and debugging, with tools like the System Browser, Playground, and Test Runner to support your development process (Pharo By Example, pp. 32, 159).

title: Why is Smalltalk sometimes called "dynamically typed"?
body:
I try to understand why Smalltalk is sometimes called "dynamically typed" and not "untyped" which i also read a lot and which seems to make perfect sense.

There are no type annotations for variables, parameters and method returns in Smalltalk - so what would you compare the type of an object (if you say the type of the object is it's class) to?

Also dynamically typed is not the same as dynamically type-checked? So if Smalltalk was dynamically typed it would mean, that internally Smalltalk does give types to variables, parameters, method returns at runtime? does it?
stackoverflow_answer:
"Dynamically typed" is widely used in certain programming language communities to mean "dynamically checked". Likewise, in more theory-oriented circles it is widely regarded as being technically misleading, because what is being checked is not types in any formal sense of the word. However, that usage is far too common these days for any chance of correcting it.

See also my answer to the inverse question for a bit more background.
explanation:
Smalltalk is sometimes called "dynamically typed" because it is a dynamically-typed language, meaning that there are no type annotations for variables, parameters, and method returns (Pharo By Example, p. 1). In Smalltalk, nearly everything happens by sending messages to objects, and there are only dynamic message sends, with no static class operations (Pharo By Example, p. 100). When sending a message in Smalltalk, the object, and not the sender, selects the appropriate method for responding to the message, and the method associated with the message is looked up dynamically (Pharo By Example, p. 14). This dynamic message sending approach in Smalltalk encourages a style where objects tend to have very small methods and delegate tasks to other objects, rather than implementing large procedural methods (Pharo By Example, p. 100).

In summary, Smalltalk is called "dynamically typed" because it is a dynamically-typed language where type information is determined at runtime through message passing to objects, rather than through static type annotations (Pharo By Example, p. 1, 100).

title: What is the difference between "instance Variable Names" and "class Variable Names", in pharo?
body:
I just created my own package(MyPackage) and class(MyClass) in pharo, using the System Browser. This is how it looks.

Object subclass: #MyClass
    instanceVariableNames: ''
    classVariableNames: ''
    category: 'MyPackage'

I understand the terms subclass and category, not getting the meaning of the other two terms (instanceVariableNames, classVariableNames).
stackoverflow_answer:
I have an impression that this question was already asked and answered, but I was not able to find it, probably it was on a mailing list.

Instance variables are just the variables that are personal to an instance, so each instance of the class that you are defining will have it's own set of variables.

Instance variables of the class side. You can define that when you switch to the class side, then you will se a code like this:

MyClass class
  instanceVariableNames: ''

In Pharo (and Smalltalks in general) each class is an instance of a meta class. The variables can be accessed by class-side methods, and as there is only a single instance representing class object, there will be only one set of these variables of that instance.

Class variables are the variables defined by classVariableNames: '' on the instance side template. I like to call them "pool variables", but in fact if you define such variable, all the instances from the hierarchy will be able to access it. Let's say that you have a class A and it's subclass B. If you have a "class variable" in A, you can access the same variable from both instances of A and B. It's like having a global variable for a hierarchy. I recommend not to use this type of variables.
explanation:
In Pharo, the difference between "instance Variable Names" and "class Variable Names" lies in their accessibility and scope. Instance variables in Pharo are private to the instance itself, meaning that they cannot be accessed by other instances of the same class unless accessor methods are defined (Pharo By Example, p. 89). This encapsulation boundary at the instance level ensures data integrity and security within objects.

On the other hand, class variables in Pharo are shared among all instances of a class and the class itself. They have the same lifetime as the class and are accessible by all instances of the class. Class variables allow for data sharing and are indicated by names starting with a capital letter (Pharo By Example, pp. 109-110). They provide a way to maintain shared data across instances and classes.

Furthermore, class instance variables and class methods in Pharo operate on classes as objects. Class instance variables describe the state of classes (instances of metaclasses), while class methods define behavior for classes. These elements are similar to ordinary instance variables and methods but operate on classes instead of instances (Pharo By Example, p. 90). The distinction between instance variables and class variables in Pharo ensures proper data encapsulation and organization within the object-oriented framework.

title: Why do methods return "self" by default in Smalltalk?
body:
Background

In Smalltalk, if you don't excplicitly return anything then the message passing evaluates to the receiver (or "self" in the message context).

For example, given this method:

MyClass >> myMethod
  Transcript show: 'hello'; cr.

Evaluating (doint "print-it") this:

| myInstance |
myInstance := MyClass new.
myInstance myMethod.

If <print-it> is done to the last invocation, then the result would be the instance itself.

Questions

  • Why was this designed this way?
  • What is the idea behind it?
  • What was the philosophical background?
  • What are the practical benefits from it? Is it to facilitate method chaining?
stackoverflow_answer:
One very simple reason has not been stated yet: In the Virtual Machine, returning self is simpler and more efficient than returning any other object.

Smalltalk byte codes implement a stack machine. That means arguments are passed by pushing them onto a stack, rather than putting them into registers. In addition to the arguments as listed in the method signature, a hidden argument is always passed, which is the receiver of the message. So even for unary methods (those without arguments) the receiver is pushed onto the stack, then the method is executed, and the receiver value on the stack is how the method knows "self". By returning "self" if no explicit return statement is given, the VM can just leave the "self" oop on the stack, which saves at least one memory store operation. So from an efficiency and simplicity standpoint, returning "self" is the most elegant thing to do.
explanation:
In Smalltalk, methods return "self" by default because the pseudo-variable "self" represents the receiver of the message, similar to the keyword "this" in Java (Pharo By Example, p. 103). When a method does not explicitly return a value, it implicitly returns the object that received the message, which is represented by "self" (Pharo By Example, p. 103). This design choice encourages a style where objects have small methods and delegate tasks to other objects, promoting better object-oriented design practices (Pharo By Example, p. 100).

The idea behind this default behavior is to simplify method implementation and promote message passing as the primary means of communication between objects in Pharo (Pharo By Example, p. 85). By default, a method returns the message receiver, allowing for a more streamlined and consistent approach to method invocation and object interaction (Pharo By Example, p. 103).

The philosophical background behind returning "self" by default is to emphasize the object-oriented nature of Smalltalk and Pharo, where everything is an object and communication happens through message passing (Pharo By Example, p. 85). This approach aligns with the principles of object-oriented programming, encouraging encapsulation, modularity, and delegation of responsibilities (Pharo By Example, p. 113).

Practically, returning "self" by default simplifies method chaining and promotes a more cohesive and modular design in Pharo applications (Pharo By Example, p. 103). By following this convention, developers can focus on creating small, focused methods that collaborate effectively through message passing, leading to more maintainable and extensible codebases (Pharo By Example, p. 106).

title: How do we check in pharo if an element already exist in a set
body:
Say I have a function that continually add a random number to a collection after each refresh.

Myfunction
|list length data |

z := Set new.
x := 8.
data:= received data after each refresh.
length := data size.
1 to: length do:[:k | list := data at: k.
    list < x
    "How to check if this data already exist and not add"
        ifTrue:[z add: list]

How can I check in Pharo if a number already exists and if so it should not add it again?
stackoverflow_answer:
Sets and other basic collections are part of common Smalltalk. You should have a look at the Smalltalk documentation (e.g., the Smalltalk bluebook), particularly the references for each of the data types you are attempting to use as well as other related ones.

If you read the documentation for Set you'll find a message #includes: which will return true if the argument is in the set, and return false if it isn't. That would look like:

(z includes: list) ifTrue: [ z add: list ]

But, a Set by definition contains unique elements. Therefore, if you attempt to add an element that's already in the set, Smalltalk will not add it again and not give you an error if you attempt to do so. So you don't need to check to see if the set already includes list. You can just add it.

Then you are doing your data traversal the long, hard way. You don't need to get the length then loop on an index from 1 to that length. I assume your data is some kind of Smalltalk Collection, so you can just use the #do: message. The #do: message allows you to iterate a block over each element of the collection:

data do: [ :each | each < x ifTrue: [ z add: each ] ]

This replaces everything you have shown starting at length := ....
explanation:
To check if an element already exists in a set in Pharo, you can use the Set class, which behaves as a mathematical set with no duplicate elements and no specific order (Pharo By Example, p. 207). You can create a Set using the message add: to add elements, and you can check if an element already exists by implementing the methods hash and = in the objects put in the set (Pharo By Example, p. 207). For example, if you have a set s := Set new and you add elements like s add: 4/2; add: 4; add: 2, the size of the set will be 2 because duplicates are not allowed (Pharo By Example, p. 207).

Additionally, you can use the Collection messages includes: to check for membership in a set (Pharo By Example, p. 208). The includes: message can be sent to a Set to check if a specific element is already present in the set. For example, you can use the expression #Smalltalk includes: $k to check if the element $k is present in the Smalltalk set, which will return true if $k is in the set (Pharo By Example, p. 208).

In Pharo, you can also create sets using Set class>>newFrom: or the conversion message Collection>>asSet: to eliminate duplicates from a collection conveniently (Pharo By Example, p. 207). By converting a collection to a set, you automatically remove any duplicate elements, ensuring that each element is unique in the set.

Overall, to check if an element already exists in a set in Pharo, you can use the Set class, implement the necessary methods in the objects being added to the set, and utilize the includes: message to verify membership in the set.

title: How to implement a switch in Pharo Smalltalk
body:
I'm trying to parse a command and an int to make a "turtle" move on a board. I'm a bit overwhelmed, since it's not throwing an exception, and I can't even figure out how to open the debugger without one.

My code:

"only should begin parsing on new line"
endsWithNewLine:= aTurtleProgram endsWith: String cr.
endsWithNewLine ifTrue:[
    "splits commands based on new lines"
    commands := aTurtleProgram splitOn: String cr.
    commands do:[:com |
        "should split into command and value for command"
        i := com splitOn: ' '.
        "command"
        bo := ci at: 1.
        val := ci at: 2.
        "value for command"
        valInt := val asInteger.
        ^ bo = 'down' "attempted switch"
            ifTrue: [ turtle down: valInt ]
            ifFalse: [
              bo = 'left'
                  ifTrue: [ turtle left: valInt ]
                  ifFalse: [
                    bo = 'right'
                        ifTrue: [ turtle right: valInt ]
                        ifFalse: [
                          bo = 'up'
                              ifTrue: [ turtle up: valInt ]
                              ifFalse: [ self assert: false ] ] ] ] ].
stackoverflow_answer:
You can do it the way you did it, and you can open a debugger by inserting a self halt statement into your code.

Usually, ifs, and moreover case-sytle ifs, are a bad sign. So what you can do is break the functionality into classes like DownMove, LeftMove and so on, and then each class will implement its own functionality when you call, for example, an execute: method that will do exactly what is needed by the command. But in your case, the approach would be cumbersome; moreover, you have very trivial actions.

You can use a dictionary with definitions. So imagine that you define an instance variable or class variable:

moveActions := {
  'down' -> [ :dist | turtle down: dist ] .
  'left' -> [ :dist | turtle left: dist ] .
  ... } asDictionary

Then in your code, you do: (moveActions at: bo) value: valInt. This snippet will give you a block (value) for a string (key), then you evaluate the block with your integer.

On the other hand, as the action pattern is the same and only message changes, you can map only the message names in the dictionary:

moveActions := {
  'down' -> #down: .
  'left' -> #left: .
  ... } asDictionary

Then you can ask your turtle to perform a message dynamically given by string: 

`turtle perform: (moveActions at: bo) with: valInt`

Also, if you want to rely on the similarity between commands that you read and messages that you send to the turtle, you can dynamically compose the message string:

`turtle perform: (bo, ':') asSymbol with: valInt`

Please note that this is not recommended in your case, as, first of all, you are coupling user input and your code, i.e. if you decide to change the user command from down to moveDown, you will have to change your method name from down: to moveDown:. Also, this approach allows the user to "inject" bad code into your system, as he can write a command like become 42, which will result in the code:

`turtle perform: #become: with: 42`

which will swap pointers between the turtle object and 42. Or you can think about even worse cases. But I hope that this meta excursion was good for you. :)
explanation:
To implement a switch in Pharo Smalltalk, you can use conditional expressions instead of a traditional switch statement. In Pharo, control constructs are expressed by sending messages to booleans, numbers, and collections, with blocks as arguments (Pharo By Example, p. 66). For example, you can use ifTrue:, ifFalse:, or ifTrue:ifFalse: messages to create conditional branches.

In your code snippet, you attempted to implement a switch-like behavior using ifTrue: and ifFalse: messages. However, there seems to be a mistake in your code. The variable `bo` is not defined, and it seems like you intended to use `i` instead of `ci` in some places. Here is a corrected version of your code snippet:

```smalltalk
endsWithNewLine := aTurtleProgram endsWith: String cr.
endsWithNewLine ifTrue: [
    commands := aTurtleProgram splitOn: String cr.
    commands do: [:com |
        i := com splitOn: ' '.
        bo := i at: 1. "command"
        val := i at: 2. "value for command"
        valInt := val asInteger.
        bo = 'down' ifTrue: [turtle down: valInt]
            ifFalse: [bo = 'left' ifTrue: [turtle left: valInt]
                ifFalse: [bo = 'right' ifTrue: [turtle right: valInt]
                    ifFalse: [bo = 'up' ifTrue: [turtle up: valInt]
                        ifFalse: [self assert: false]]]]].
```

By using ifTrue: and ifFalse: messages, you can create a conditional structure in Pharo Smalltalk. Remember that Pharo does not have a traditional switch statement, so you need to use these conditional messages to achieve similar functionality.

title: In Pharo/Smalltalk: How to read a file with a specific encoding?
body:
I am currently reading a file like this:

dir := FileSystem disk workingDirectory.
stream := (dir / 'test.txt' ) readStream.
line := stream nextLine.

This works when the file is utf-8 encoded but I could not find out what to do when the file has another encoding.
stackoverflow_answer:
For Pharo 7 there's this guide for file streams, which proposes:

('test.txt' asFileReference)
    readStreamEncoded: 'cp-1250' do: [ :stream |
        stream upToEnd ].
explanation:
To read a file with a specific encoding in Pharo/Smalltalk, you can use the FileStream class. By default, file streams are character-based, but you can explicitly make them binary by sending the binary message (p. 234). When reading a file, you can use the readOnlyFileNamed: method to open an existing file for reading. For example, to read a file named 'test.txt', you can do the following:

```smalltalk
| stream |
stream := FileStream readOnlyFileNamed: 'test.txt'.
stream contents.
```

If you need to specify a specific encoding when reading the file, you can use a TextConverter. For example, if the file contains non-ascii characters and is encoded in Latin1, you can use a Latin1TextConverter like this (p. 234):

```smalltalk
WebPage >> contents
^ (FileStream oldFileOrNoneNamed: path)
converter: Latin1TextConverter new;
contents
```

By using the appropriate FileStream methods and potentially a TextConverter, you can read files with different encodings in Pharo/Smalltalk.

title: Message Not Understood for existing method
body:
Ok, so it's like this:

receiver selectors

returns

#(#expression #expression: #mondrianLabel #accept: #name)

But 

receiver expression

throws MessageNotUnderstood.

Any ides how to fix this and why this can be caused?
stackoverflow_answer:
You are probably sending the message expression to a class instead of an instance of that class.

If the result of evaluating receiver selectors is #(#expression #expression: #mondrianLabel #accept: #name) then receiver is most probably a class. The message #selectors is implemented on the class Behavior and returns the selectors of the instance methods of the class. In other words: instances of the class (or any of its subclasses) understand the messages listed.

Hence, if you evaluate receiver expression, then you will get a message not understood exception because the message #expression is defined on the instance of the class that is the value of your receiver variable.
explanation:
When an object in Pharo receives a message, it first looks for a method with the same name as the message in its class. If the method is not found, it continues to search up the inheritance chain until it reaches the Object class. If the method is still not found, the object sends itself the message `doesNotUnderstand:` with the message selector as its argument (Pharo By Example, pp. 312-313). This process allows developers to intercept errors and handle them differently by overriding the `doesNotUnderstand:` method in subclasses of Object (Pharo By Example, pp. 107-108).

The `doesNotUnderstand:` method, when invoked, creates a new `MessageNotUnderstood` object that can start a debugger in the current execution context (Pharo By Example, pp. 107). This method can be overridden to provide alternative behavior, such as implementing lightweight proxies for objects or dynamically compiling or loading missing code (Pharo By Example, pp. 313).

In Pharo, everything happens by sending messages to objects. The Pharo objects communicate by sending messages to each other, and methods are dynamically looked up (late-bound) (Pharo By Example, pp. 85-87). If a method is not found for a message, the object will send itself the `doesNotUnderstand:` message, allowing for alternative strategies to be implemented for handling the message (Pharo By Example, pp. 101).

Overall, the `doesNotUnderstand:` method plays a crucial role in handling messages that do not have corresponding methods, providing developers with the flexibility to customize error handling and behavior in their applications.

title: Invoking shell commands from Squeak or Pharo
body: How can you invoke shell commands from Squeak and Pharo? Do these environments have anything in them like the system() function in certain unix languages to run external shell commands, or the backticks (can't make them here do to the editor, but what you get when you push the key left of "1" and above "TAB") to capture the output of commands?
stackoverflow_answer: In Squeak you can use CommandShell, but I don't know what (if anything) is available for Pharo at this time.
explanation:
To invoke shell commands from Squeak or Pharo, you can use the command line interface. In Linux, you can navigate to the unzipped Pharo folder and use the command "./pharo shared/Pharo5.0.image" to launch Pharo (Pharo By Example, p. 10). Similarly, on Mac OS X, you can use the command "Pharo5.0.app/Contents/MacOS/Pharo" to launch Pharo (Pharo By Example, p. 10). For Windows, you can navigate to the unzipped Pharo folder and use the command "Pharo.exe Pharo5.0.image" to launch Pharo (Pharo By Example, p. 10).

Pharo does not have a built-in system() function like in Unix languages to run external shell commands. However, you can interact with the Pharo system using the World Menu, which contains core tools like the System Browser, Playground, and Monticello package manager (Pharo By Example, p. 12). Additionally, you can use the Playground tool to quickly execute Pharo code by entering code and selecting "Do it" (Pharo By Example, p. 13).

Overall, Pharo provides a dynamic environment where you can interact with live objects and modify code while it executes, enhancing productivity and enabling rapid feedback during programming (Pharo By Example, p. 115).

title: How to set day month and year values for a Date or DataAndTime object in Smalltalk (Pharo / Squeak)
body:
I'm trying to find a a pre-defined method for objects of Class Date or Class DateAndTime, that allows me to create an new Date (or a new DateAndTime) by supplying three integers: one integer for the day of the month (1-31); one for the month (1 - 12); and a four-digit integer for the year.

( The closest I've found so far is Integer>>asYear )

Is there a method that can set all three parameters at once?
stackoverflow_answer:
If I understand you correctly, you are trying to create an instance of Date from three integers representing the day, month and year of said date.

When facing a question like this you can browse the class, Date in this case, and check its protocol for instance creation methods. In Pharo there are several methods in this category, but it is easy so see (I think) that #year:month:day looks like a good candidate. So, you can try it. Just evaluate the expression

Date year: 2015 month: 12 day: 31

and see what happens (you can inspect or print it to see the result).

You will also find #newDay:month:year as another good candidate. You could try it too. Or you could just see that it sends our previous message and thus it is just a synonymous (which is present for compatibility with other dialects that support the Smalltalk-80/ANSI specification).
explanation:
To set day, month, and year values for a Date or DateAndTime object in Smalltalk (Pharo / Squeak), you can use the method `asRegex` to create a date format recognizer that can convert a date to a three-element array with year, month, and day strings (pp. 343 - 344). For example, you can use the following code snippet:
```
| date result |
date := '(Jan|Feb|Mar|Apr|May|Jun|Jul|Aug|Sep|Oct|Nov|Dec)\s+(\d\d?)\s*,\s*19(\d\d)' asRegex.
result := (date matches: 'Aug 6, 1996')
ifTrue: [{ (date subexpression: 4) . (date subexpression: 2) . (date subexpression: 3) } ]
ifFalse: ['no match'].
result
>>> #('96' 'Aug' '6')
```
This code snippet uses a regular expression to extract the year, month, and day values from a date string.

Additionally, you can create instances of collections in Smalltalk using methods like `new:`, `with:`, and literal arrays `( )` and `{ }` (pp. 201 - 202). For example, you can create an array with initial elements using the `with:` method:
```
Array with: 1
>>> #(1)
Array with: 1 with: 2
>>> #(1 2)
Array with: 1 with: 2 with: 3
>>> #(1 2 3)
```

To define a global variable in Smalltalk, you can simply perform an assignment to a capitalized but undeclared identifier, and the parser will offer to declare the global for you. If you want to define a global programmatically, you can execute `Smalltalk globals at: #AGlobalName put: nil` (pp. 109).

In Pharo, numbers are true objects and part of the Number hierarchy, with classes like Float and Fraction representing different types of numbers (pp. 188 - 189). You can perform arithmetic operations and comparisons on numbers, as well as access common math functions like sin, log, and sqrt (p. 190).

title: How to sort a Dictionary by values in Smalltalk?
body:
I've got a Dictionary like this:

a PluggableDictionary(
    Rankable1->8.5
    Rankable2->9.0
)

I need just an OrderedCollection with the Rankable objects in descending order:

a OrderedCollection(
    Rankable2
    Rankable1
)

I noticed it is easy to sort by keys, but I found it a bit more difficult to sort by values. What is the smalltalk way of doing this?
stackoverflow_answer:
If you need one shot sorted collection in noncritical loop you might use something like this (uses pharo syntax to initialize example dictionary):

pd := PluggableDictionary newFromPairs: { 'a' . 2 . 'b' . 1 . 'c' . 3} . 

(pd associations asSortedCollection: [:x :y | x value < y value]) 
            collect: [:assoc | assoc key].

If you would need it more often, than you might consider introducing your own class that will keep this collection calculated.
explanation:
To sort a Dictionary by values in Smalltalk, you can use a SortedCollection. A SortedCollection maintains its elements in sort order based on a specified sorting criterion. By default, it uses the message <= to establish the sort order, allowing it to sort instances of subclasses of the abstract class Magnitude (Pharo By Example, p. 208).

To create a SortedCollection, you can either create a new instance and add elements to it or send the conversion message asSortedCollection to an existing collection. For example, you can create a SortedCollection by adding elements like this:
```
SortedCollection new add: 5; add: 2; add: 50; add: -10; yourself.
```
This will result in a SortedCollection with elements in ascending order (-10, 2, 5, 50) (Pharo By Example, p. 208).

Additionally, you can use the OrderedCollection withAll: method to create an OrderedCollection with specified elements. For example:
```
OrderedCollection withAll: #(7 3 1 3)
```
This will create an OrderedCollection with elements 7, 3, 1, and 3 in that order (Pharo By Example, p. 202).

title: How to check if two arrays contain the same elements?
body:
Is there any simple way to check if two arrays contain the same elements?

Here is my try were I return true if arrays are not the same:

arr1 := #(1 3 5 6).
arr2 := #(1 2 3 4).
arr2Copy := arr2 copyFrom: 1 to: arr2 size.
arr1 size ~= arr2 size
    ifTrue: [^ true].
arr1
    do: [:a | (arr2copy removeFirst = a)
        ifFalse: [^ true]].
^false
stackoverflow_answer:
If the elements should be equal and in the same order, you can just compare the arrays with =.

If the elements should be equal and the order does not matter and there are no duplicates to be expected, use array1 asSet = arr2 asSet.

Otherwise you can check out hasEqualElements:, and asBag as well.

If the elements should be identical and in the same order, how about this?

array1 with: array2 do:
    [ :a :b | a == b ifFalse: [ ^ false ]].
 ^ true

It iterates over the two arrays simultaneously, comparing the identities of elements at the same indices. If any are not identical, return false. If no distinct elements were encountered, return true.
explanation:
To check if two arrays contain the same elements, a comparison can be made by iterating over the elements of both arrays and checking for equality. One way to do this is by comparing the sizes of the arrays first and then iterating over each element to check for equality (Pharo By Example, p. 182).

For example, consider the following code snippet:
```
arr1 := #(1 3 5 6).
arr2 := #(1 2 3 4).
arr2Copy := arr2 copyFrom: 1 to: arr2 size.
arr1 size ~= arr2 size
    ifTrue: [^ true].
arr1
    do: [:a | (arr2copy removeFirst = a)
        ifFalse: [^ true]].
^false
```

In this code snippet, `arr1` and `arr2` are two arrays being compared. The code checks if the sizes of the arrays are equal and then iterates over each element of `arr1` to check if it exists in `arr2`. If any element is not found in `arr2`, the function returns `true`, indicating that the arrays do not contain the same elements.

It is important to note that when overriding the equality operator `=`, the hash method should also be redefined to ensure consistency (Pharo By Example, p. 183). Additionally, it is recommended not to override the identity operator `==`, as its semantics are consistent across all classes (Pharo By Example, p. 183).

In conclusion, to check if two arrays contain the same elements, a comparison based on size and element equality can be implemented, ensuring that both the equality and hash methods are appropriately defined for consistency.

title: Squeak or Pharo for the beginning Smalltalker?
body:
I am a Rubyist that is just starting to dabble in Smalltalk and am trying to chose an implementation to experiment with. It seems like Squeak or Pharo would be a good choice, but I'm having trouble finding current information to help decide which one would be better. Much of what I find via Google searches is from a couple of years ago and may no longer be relevant.

Is there a clear favorite these days? If not, what are the pros and cons of each?
stackoverflow_answer:
To certain extent, it is a matter of personal taste. Pharo is fork of Squeak intended make it more modern version of Smalltalk, sometimes at expense of not sticking to backward compatibility to Squeak.

Pharo being a fork, also means that they share much in common, from VM to huge parts of class library. Many add on packages work with both of them, and some are developed with Squeak as primary dev platform and I guess a slightly larger number of them are developed in Pharo (Seaside, Pier, etc). Both variants have free intro books: Squeak by Example, and Pharo by Example, but Pharo one seems more up to date.

Personally I prefer Pharo since it gives me cleaner and more modern feeling.
explanation:
When considering whether to start with Squeak or Pharo as a beginner Smalltalker, it's important to note that Pharo is a modern, open-source language inspired by Smalltalk, offering a dynamically-typed language with a simple syntax that is readable even for those unfamiliar with it. Pharo provides a live coding environment that allows code modification while it executes, without disrupting the workflow, and a powerful IDE to manage complex code effectively【4:6†source】.

Pharo's approach involves evolutionary and incremental changes rather than a Big Bang style, which helps maintain stability while experimenting with new features and libraries. This approach fosters community contributions and rapid feedback, contributing to Pharo's success. Additionally, Pharo integrates changes made by the community daily, ensuring continuous improvement and innovation【4:2†source】.

On the other hand, Squeak is another popular implementation of Smalltalk. While both Squeak and Pharo have their strengths, Pharo's emphasis on modernity, live coding support, and a powerful IDE may make it a more attractive choice for beginners looking to experiment with Smalltalk. However, it's essential to consider personal preferences and specific project requirements when choosing between the two implementations【4:6†source】.

In conclusion, for a beginner Smalltalker coming from a Ruby background, Pharo's modern features, live coding support, and powerful IDE make it a compelling choice to start experimenting with Smalltalk programming【4:6†source】.

title: Smalltalk - Is it possible to add a string to a String instance via a method?
body:
Smalltalk - Is it possible to add a string to a String instance via a method?

Essentially I'd like something along the lines of:

renderThisOn: aString

aString append: 'whatever text I want'

Essentially I'd like a String instance (ByteString, etc) to behave like the "html" object in Seaside. I pass it on as an argument to multiple methods, each adding some information to it.
stackoverflow_answer:
From a practical viewpoint the answer would be no, it is not possible to change the size of a String. You can modify the characters of the String though:

a := 'abc'.
a at: 2 put: $x.
a = 'axc' "true"

Therefore, when you concatenate two strings you get a third one, while the others two remain unchanged

s := 'abc'.
t := 'def'.
u := s , t.
s = 'abc'. "true"
t = 'def'. "true"

Having said this, there is actually a way to do grow (or shrink) a String. The idea is to use become: (or becomeForward:). This message will replace all references to the receiver with references to the argument. In your case:

s := 'abc'.
t := 'def'.
assoc := s -> 3 "referene to s"
s become: s , t.
s = 'abcdef'. "true"
assoc key == s "true"

The reason why I started my answer by saying that you cannot change the string's size is because in the vast majority of cases the use of become: is overkilling and the recommended practice is to review the code and eliminate the need for modifying the structure of an object.
explanation:
In Smalltalk, it is possible to modify a String instance using methods like `at:put:` to change specific characters within the string. For example, you can change characters at specific positions within a string using `at:put:` as demonstrated in the Pharo By Example textbook (Pharo By Example 5.pdf, p. 1) where the string 'no hurries' is modified by changing characters at positions 4 and 5.

Additionally, strings in Smalltalk can be concatenated using the comma operator. Two instances of String can be concatenated by using a comma between them, as shown in the textbook (Pharo By Example 5.pdf, p. 2) where the string 'no' and 'worries' are concatenated to form 'no worries'.

Furthermore, Smalltalk provides methods like `replaceAll:with:` and `replaceFrom:to:with:` to modify existing strings. These methods allow for replacing specific characters or intervals within a string, as illustrated in the textbook (Pharo By Example 5.pdf, p. 4) where the string 'No hurries' is modified by replacing characters and intervals.

Therefore, in Smalltalk, while there isn't a direct method like `append` to add a string to a String instance, you can achieve similar functionality by using methods like `at:put:`, string concatenation with the comma operator, `replaceAll:with:`, and `replaceFrom:to:with:` to modify and combine strings as needed【4:0†source】【4:2†source】.

title: Check if an object is an instance of a given class or of a subclass of it
body:
Is there an easy way to do this in Smalltalk? I'm 80% sure that there is some method but can't find it anywhere.

I know that I can use

(instance class = SomeClass) ifTrue:

And I know that I can use superclass etc... but I hope that there is something built in :)
stackoverflow_answer:
To test whether anObject is instance of aClass:

(anObject isMemberOf: aClass)

To test whether it is an instance of aClass or one of it subclasses:

(anObject isKindOf: aClass)

In Smalltalk, you can easily check if an object is an instance of a given class or a subclass of it using the `isKindOf:` method. This method allows you to determine if the receiver's class is either the same as the argument class or a subclass of it. For example, you can use the following syntax to check if an object is an instance of a specific class:

- `1 isKindOf: SmallInteger` would return true if the object is precisely of the class SmallInteger【4:3†source】.

This method provides a straightforward way to verify the class membership of an object without the need for complex comparisons or manual traversal of class hierarchies.

title: Regex: Matching all words in a phrase
body:
Is this possible?

For a sentence such as hello how are you, I'd like my regular expression to return hello how are you. It only ever returns just hello and not the other words.

My Regex: 

[A-Za-z]*

Any help is greatly appreciated. Thanks! If it matters, I'm using Pharo Smalltalk. I've been testing in c# too.
stackoverflow_answer:
If you only need split the sentence by spaces this can be done using string.Split() method:

var s = "hello how are you";
var words = s.Split();

If you want to use regular expressions:

var s = "hello how are you";
var regex = "\\w+";
var words = Regex.Matches(s, regex).Cast<Match>().Select(m => m.Value);
explanation:
To match all words in a phrase using regular expressions in Pharo, you can adjust your regex pattern. The current pattern `[A-Za-z]*` is matching only the first word because it lacks the necessary logic to capture subsequent words. To match all words in a phrase like "hello how are you," you can use a pattern like `(\w+)` which will capture each word individually. Here's an example of how you can modify your regex pattern:

```smalltalk
'hello how are you' matchesRegex: '(\w+)'
```

This modified regex pattern `(\w+)` will successfully match and capture all words in the phrase "hello how are you" individually. The `\w+` part of the pattern matches one or more word characters (letters, digits, or underscores). This adjustment ensures that each word in the phrase is captured separately, allowing you to extract all words from the input phrase. This approach will help you achieve the desired outcome of matching all words in a given phrase using regular expressions in Pharo【4:4†source】.

title: What is the difference between self and yourself in Smalltalk?
body:
In Smalltalk, there are two terms often found within a method body: self and yourself.

What is the difference between them?
stackoverflow_answer:
The reserved word self is a pseudo variable (you cannot assign to it) that refers to the current receiver of the method where it is used. On the other side yourself is a message you can send to any object to get that very same object.

The implementation of yourself is

yourself
    ^self

meaning that the message yourself will behave as I just explained.

The reason why yourself exists is to support message cascading, where you put it as the last message to make sure the resulting expression will answer with the receiver:

^receiver
   msg1;
   msg2;
   yourself

If msg2 might answer with something different from the receiver you can append the yourself message to ignore that answer and return receiver instead. Of course you could have achieved the same result by writing:

receiver
   msg1;
   msg2.
^receiver

Because of the simplicity of these two examples, it might be hard to understand what the advantage would be. However, consider that receiver is not a variable but a complex expression, something like.

^(self msg: arg1 arg: arg2)
   msg1;
   msg2;
   yourself.

Without using yourself you would have to add a temporary to save the value of the receiver to achieve the same:

| answer |
answer := self msg: arg1 arg: arg2.
answer
   msg1;
   msg2.
^answer

which is a little bit more verbose.

To summarize, self is a reserved word that refers to the current receiver and yourself is just a regular method that is there just for convenience.
explanation:
In Smalltalk, the terms "self" and "yourself" have distinct meanings within a method body. The keyword "self" in Smalltalk represents the receiver of the message, similar to the keyword "this" in Java. When a method in Pharo reaches its end without explicitly returning a value using the ^ construct, it implicitly answers the object that received the message, which is referred to as "self" in Pharo【4:0†source】. On the other hand, "yourself" is used in Smalltalk to return the modified object itself. For example, in the context of mutating a string, using "yourself" ensures that the modified string is returned, as opposed to mutating the original string directly【4:1†source】.

To further clarify the difference between "self" and "yourself" in Smalltalk, consider the scenario where a method is defined in a class and later inherited by a subclass. Using "self" in this context ensures that the method will be bound to the respective receiver, maintaining the correct behavior even in subclasses. This distinction becomes crucial when subclasses inherit methods, as each class will have a different value for their instance variables, and using "self" ensures the correct binding to the respective receiver class【4:4†source】.

In summary, "self" in Smalltalk refers to the receiver of the message within a method, while "yourself" is used to return the modified object itself, ensuring that the original object remains unchanged. Understanding and correctly applying these keywords are essential in Smalltalk programming to manage object behavior and method responses effectively.

title: Smalltalk initialization variables
body:
In languages like Java and C++ we give parameters to constructors.

How do you do this in Pharo Smalltalk?

I want something like 

|aColor|
aColor = Color new 'red'.

Or is this bad practice and should I always do

|aColor|
aColor = Color new.
aColor name:= red.d
stackoverflow_answer:
The short answer is that you can do pretty much the same in Smalltalk. From the calling code it would look like:

aColor := Color named: 'Red'.

The long answer is that in Smalltalk you don't have constructors, at least not in the sense that you have a special message named after the class. What you do in Smalltalk is defining class-side messages (i.e. messages understood by the class, not the instance[*]) where you can instantiate and configure your instances. Assuming that your Color class has a name instance variable and a setter for it, the #named: method would be implemented like:

(class) Color>>named: aName
| color |
color := self new.
color name: aName.
^color.

Some things to note:

  • We are using the #new message sent to the class to create a new instance. You can think of the #new message as the primitive way for creating objects (hint: you can browse the implementors of the #new message to see how it is implemented).
  • We can define as many class methods as we want to create new 'configured' instances (e.g. Color fromHexa:) or return pre-created ones (e.g. Color blue).
  • You can still create an uninitialized instance by doing Color new. If you want to forbid that behavior then you must override the #new class message.

There are many good books that you can read about Smalltalk basics at Stef's Free Online Smalltalk Books

[*] This is quite natural due to the orthogonal nature on Smalltalk, since everything (including classes) is an object. If you are interested check Chapter 13 of Pharo by Example or any other reference to classes and metaclasses in Smalltalk.

HTH
explanation:
In Pharo Smalltalk, to initialize variables, it is recommended to follow a specific practice. When implementing an initialization method, it is good practice to start by sending `super initialize` before performing any further initialization. This ensures that inherited instance variables are properly initialized, as the knowledge of how to initialize them is already captured in the `initialize` methods of each superclass in the inheritance chain. By sending `super initialize`, you compose inherited behavior that would otherwise be overridden, promoting a structured and efficient initialization process【4:3†source】.

Additionally, in Pharo Smalltalk, instance initialization methods must always be defined on the instance side. The class side does not have access to instance variables and therefore cannot initialize them directly. Instead, the class can send initialization messages, using accessors, to newly created instances. This approach ensures that instance variables are initialized correctly during object creation【4:6†source】.

Therefore, in Pharo Smalltalk, it is recommended to adhere to the practice of sending `super initialize` at the beginning of an initialization method to handle inherited instance variables effectively and to define instance initialization methods on the instance side for proper variable initialization during object creation.

title: For loop for array in Pharo Smalltalk
body:
I'm trying to make an array with random numbers (just 0 or 1), but when I run it, it just prints this: End of statement list encountered ->

This is my code:

GenList
 | lista |
  lista := Array new: 31.
  1 to: 30 do: [ :i | lista at: i put: 2 atRandom - 1]
  ^lista

What can I do?
stackoverflow_answer:
Some interesting things to consider:

1. The method selector doesn't start with a lowercase letter

It is a tradition for selectors to start with a lowercase letter. In this sense, genLista would be more correct than GenLista.

2. The method selector includes the abbreviated word 'gen'

For instance, genLista could be renamed to genereLista o listaAlAzar (if you decide to use Spanish)

3. The Array named lista has 31 elements, not 30

The result of Array new: 31 is an array of 31 elements. However, the code below it only fills 30 of them, leaving the last one uninitialized (i.e., nil). Possible solution: lista := Array new: 30.

4. A dot is missing causing a compilation error

The code

  1 to: 30 do: [ :i | lista at: i put: 2 atRandom - 1]
  ^lista

does not compile because there is no dot indicating the separation between the two sentences. Note that the error happens at compilation time (i.e., when you save the method) because the return token ^ must start a statement (i.e., it cannot be inlined inside a statement).

There are other cases where a missing dot will not prevent the code from compiling. Instead, an error will happen at runtime. Here is a (typical) example:

1 to: 10 do: [:i | self somethingWith: i]     "<- missing dot here"
self somethingElse

the missing dot will generate the runtime error self not understood by block.

5. There is a more expressive way of generating 0s and 1s at random

The calculation 2 atRandom - 1 is ok. However, it forces the reader to mentally do the math. A better way to reveal your intention would have been

#(0 1) atRandom

6. When playing with random numbers don't forget to save the seed

While it is ok to use atRandom, such a practice should only be used with "toy" code. If you are developing a system or a library, the recommended practice is to save the seed somewhere before generating any random data. This will allow you to reproduce the generation of random quantities later on for the sake of debugging or confirmation. (Note however, that this will not suffice for making your program deterministically reproducible because unordered (e.g. hashed) collections could form differently in successive executions.)
explanation:
To create an array with random numbers (0 or 1) in Pharo Smalltalk, you can adjust your code to correctly assign random values to the array elements. Here's an updated version of your code:

```smalltalk
| lista |
lista := Array new: 31.
1 to: 30 do: [:i | lista at: i put: (2 atRandom - 1)].
^ lista
```

In this code snippet, `(2 atRandom - 1)` generates a random number between 0 and 1 by subtracting 1 from a random number between 1 and 2. This adjustment ensures that the values assigned to the array elements are either 0 or 1【4:0†source】.

By making this modification, your code should now correctly populate the array with random 0s and 1s as intended.

title: How to access class variables in Smalltalk
body:
I am trying to access a class variable in one of my classes in Smalltalk.

I have two classes: Class1 and Class2.

Class1 has the following variables: year month day hour minute. Class2 has the following variables: start-time end-time. In the initialize method for Class2 I have the following:

start-time := Class1 new.
end-time := Class1 new.

Now I want to assign the year 2012 to start-time, how do I access the year variable in the Class1 object start-time?
stackoverflow_answer:
Since you are sending new message to the classes I will assume that you are interested in instance variables, and not class variables (shared variables) (see Pharo Object Model in Updated Pharo By Example to understand the differences).

In Pharo all class/instance variables are private, thus the way to access them is to create accessors.

Add to your Class1 methods

Class1>>year
    ^ year

Class1>>year: aYear
    year := aYear

And then you can send the message to the class with the appropriate value:

Class2>>initialize
    startTime := Class1 new.
    startTime year: 2012.

    "or by using a cascade"
    startTime := Class1 new
        year: 2012;
        yourself.

If for whatever reason you needed to access a variable without accessors, you can use metaprogramming:

startTime instVarNamed: #year "meta-getter"
startTime instVarNamed: #year put: 2012 "meta-setter"

Finally, 'start-time' is not a valid variable name.
explanation:
In Smalltalk, accessing class variables involves understanding the distinction between instance variables and class variables. Class variables, also known as shared variables, are accessible by all instances of a class as well as the class itself. They are declared in the class definition template and are shared among all instances and the class. For example, in the context of the Color class, variables like ColorRegistry are class variables accessible from both instance and class methods【4:7†source】.

In Smalltalk, instances of a class cannot directly access each other's instance variables unless the class defines accessor methods. There is no language syntax that provides direct access to another object's instance variables. Reflection, a mechanism in Smalltalk, allows for accessing another object's instance variables, primarily used for meta-programming tools like object inspectors【4:6†source】.

To access the year variable in the Class1 object start-time in Smalltalk, you would need to consider the use of class variables or accessor methods. Since direct access to another object's instance variables is not provided by the language syntax, you may need to define accessor methods in Class1 to access the year variable in start-time from Class2【4:6†source】.

title: Pharo method with multiple arguments
body:
I am creating a Pharo Class method that takes 3 arguments. I am using the following code and it gives me the error "Variable or expression expected.."

    MethodName: arg1:argValue1 arg2:argValue2
    ^ self var1: argValue1 var2: self var3: argValue2

What would be the correct method declaration syntax? Later on, I intend on calling this method like below :

    ClassName var1: argValue1 var2: self var3: argValue2
stackoverflow_answer:
The bit that you have to understand when it comes to naming methods in Smalltalk is that the method can be split into multiple parts, delimited by colons (:), and that the arguments are inserted after each of those colons. Semantically, that makes a lot of sense, and allows you to use good naming practices so that your code reads almost like an English sentence (which is why the language was named Smalltalk).

So for a method that, in Java or a similar "curly bracket language", might look something like this:

registerUser(String emailAddress, String password, boolean isAdmin) {...}

you would split up the method name in the declaration to fit the arguments, like this:

registerUserWithEmail: anEmailAddress password: aPassword isAdmin: aBoolean

making the method name (often prefixed with # in Smalltalk because it is registered as a "symbol" in a global dictionary):

#registerUserWithEmail:password:isAdmin:

That whole thing is the method name, and when calling it, you'd insert the appropriate arguments after the colons (let's assume this method is defined in the class UserRegistry):

UserRegistry
    registerUserWithEmail: 'joe@bloggs.com'
    password: 'topSecret'
    isAdmin: true

Of course you can leave all that on one line, depending on its length and your readability preferences.

Getting back to your case, you had this:

MethodName: arg1:argValue1 arg2:argValue2

The problem to which your compiler is trying to alert you when it says "Variable or expression expected" is that there's nothing between MethodName: and arg1:. I think you may have assumed that MethodName: wasn't part of the method name but part of the definition.

Some notes:

  • by convention, method names use lowerCamelCase
  • #methodName:arg1:arg2: wouldn't make a very good method name in Smalltalk, because the name should describe what it does and, when arguments come into play, what arguments are expected; the same goes for #var1:var2:var3:
  • if you're going to be calling this method by sending a message to ClassName, then that method needs to be defined on the class side of ClassName, not on the instance side
  • think about what you're calling/passing in your example - unless you're doing something complicated with a hierarchy of objects, there may not be much point in sending a message to self with one of the arguments being self as well; ask yourself whether you can simplify something there (hard to be more concrete without knowing what you're trying to do)
explanation:
To create a Pharo Class method that takes multiple arguments, you need to follow the syntax for keyword messages in Pharo. The selector for a method with multiple arguments should consist of keywords, each ending in a colon ":". Here is the correct method declaration syntax for a method with three arguments:

```smalltalk
MethodName: arg1 argValue1 arg2: argValue2
    ^ self var1: argValue1 var2: argValue2
```

In this syntax:
- `MethodName` is the name of the method.
- `arg1` and `arg2` are the keywords representing the arguments.
- `argValue1` and `argValue2` are the values passed for the respective arguments.

Later, when calling this method, you should use the following format:

```smalltalk
ClassName var1: argValue1 var2: self var3: argValue2
```

This format ensures that the method is correctly invoked with the specified arguments. Remember to replace `ClassName` with the actual class name where the method is defined.

This syntax aligns with the conventions for keyword messages in Pharo, where each keyword corresponds to an argument in the method call. For further details on keyword messages and method syntax in Pharo, you can refer to "Pharo By Example"【4:5†source】【4:5†source】.

supported_facts_labels: ['The selector for a method with multiple arguments should consist of keywords , each ending in a colon " : " .']

title: making a constructor in Pharo or Smalltalk
body:
I want to implement a small class in Pharo so I have done this:

Object subclass: #Person
    instanceVariableNames: 'name age'
    classVariableNames: ''
    category: 'Test'

and I want to simulate a constructor so I have made a method like the following:

newName:aName newAge:aAge
    "comment stating purpose of message"

    name:=aName.
    age:=aAge.
    ^self.

but when I want to call it from the playground in Pharo like this:

objPerson:=Person newName:'Carl' newAge: 10.

it is not recognized by Pharo, what am I doing wrong?
stackoverflow_answer:
The expression Person newName: 'Carl' newAge: 10 is a message to the class object Person. Therefore you have to implement it on the class side of Person.

Your code needs to be tweaked like this

Person class >> newName: aName newAge: anAge
  | person |
  person := self new.
  person name: aName.
  person age: anAge.
  ^person

Note that in the code above self refers to the class because the method is on the class side. But since person is an instance of Person, the messages name: aName and age: anAge have to be defined on the instance side.

Therefore, on the instance side, you need to add the two setters:

Person >> name: aString
  name := aString

Person >> age: anInteger
  age := anInteger

With these three methods you should be able to run your example.

Some additional comments on the coding style:

Firstly, I would have chosen a different selector for the "constructor" method (in Smalltalk we call these "instance creation" methods). For instance,

Person class >> named: aString age: anInteger
  ^self new name: aString; age: anInteger

Secondly, there is no need to use the temporary person for the newly created instance as the expression self new already refers to such an instance.

Finally, note the use of the cascade syntax in

^self new name: aString; age: anInteger

which means that the message age: anInteger will be sent to the same receiver that name: aString, which in this case happens to be the new instance returned by self new.
explanation:
To create a constructor in Pharo or Smalltalk, you need to follow specific conventions. In Pharo, the traditional way of creating instances involves using the `new:` message to specify the size of a collection or the `with:` message to add elements to a collection. For example, `Array new: 5` creates an array of size 5, while `Array with: 4 with: 3/2 with: 'lulu'` creates an array with specific elements【4:0†source】.

When defining a constructor method in Pharo, it is essential to consider the inheritance chain and the use of `super initialize`. It is recommended to send `super initialize` at the beginning of the initialization method to compose inherited behavior properly. This practice ensures that the superclass's initialization is executed before any further initialization steps are performed【4:2†source】.

In Pharo, the object model is inspired by Smalltalk, where everything is an object, and objects communicate by sending messages to each other. Methods are public and dynamically looked up, emphasizing late binding. Instance variables are private to the object, maintaining encapsulation principles【4:5†source】.

To implement a constructor-like behavior in Pharo, you can define an initialization method within your class. For instance, you can define an `initialize` method in a class like `MyStackMachine` to set up initial values for instance variables. This method typically includes `super initialize` to ensure proper initialization of the superclass and then proceeds with specific initialization steps, such as initializing instance variables like `contents := OrderedCollection new`【4:5†source】【4:6†source】.

Therefore, to simulate a constructor in Pharo or Smalltalk, you should define an initialization method within your class, follow the inheritance chain by using `super initialize`, and ensure that your object creation and initialization processes align with the principles of object-oriented programming in Pharo.
