title: SmallTalk - Printing contents of an OrderedCollection object using Transcript
body:
|oc|
oc := OrderedCollection new. 
oc add: 2.
oc add: #(4 9).
oc Transcript show: self; cr.

Upon running the following code in Pharo, I am getting the message:

MessageNotUnderstood: OrderedCollection>> Transcript

When replacing 'self' with 'oc' I am still getting the same error. I am looking for a way to output the collection using the Transcript.

Why can't Transcript be the receiver of my code?
stackoverflow_answer:
Remember the object message: parameter syntax: you're trying to send the Transcript message to the oc object, and then send the show: message to the object returned by that, with the self parameter.

What you really want to do is ask the Transcript object to show: your oc object. So, do that: send Transcript the show: message with oc as a parameter: Transcript show: oc. That will show the string representation of the collection.

If you were to print each member of the collection (instead of printing the collection itself), you should use the do: method to iterate over them: oc do: [ :element | Transcript show: element ]. Here you print each of the collection's member string representation.

oc := OrderedCollection new. 
oc add: 2.
oc add: #(4 9).
Transcript show: 'Show the collection:'; cr.
Transcript show: oc; cr.
Transcript show: 'Show each element:'; cr.
oc do: [ :element | Transcript show: element; cr ].

Gives this output:

Show the collection:
an OrderedCollection(2 #(4 9))
Show each element:
2
#(4 9)
explanation:
When attempting to use `Transcript` as the receiver in Smalltalk code to output the contents of an `OrderedCollection`, you encountered the error "MessageNotUnderstood: OrderedCollection>>Transcript"【4:0†source】. This error occurs because the `Transcript` class does not understand the message `show:` when sent directly to an `OrderedCollection` object. To output the contents of the collection using `Transcript`, you need to iterate over the elements of the collection and send the `show:` message to each element individually. Here is an example of how you can modify your code to achieve this:

```smalltalk
| oc |
oc := OrderedCollection new. 
oc add: 2.
oc add: #(4 9).
oc do: [:each | Transcript show: each printString; cr].
```

By iterating over the elements of the `OrderedCollection` and sending the `show:` message to each element, you can successfully output the contents of the collection using `Transcript`【4:0†source】【4:1†source】.

title: How do we write if elseif elseif in pharo?
body:
I have to check 3 conditons using the if elseif elseif. How can I do that in pharo. I did but am not sure since I did not find any such application in pharo.

x = 25
ifTrue:[DoSomething]
ifFalse:[DoSomething else].

x < 25
ifTrue: [DoSomething]
ifFalse:[Domething else].

x > 25
ifTrue: [DoSomething ]
ifFalse:[DoSomething else].
stackoverflow_answer:
You can choose different design (using polymorphism, lookup, ...) but that is pretty much the same for any OO language (for Smalltalk in particular, refer to this Refactoring if-chains in Smalltalk without class explosion ).

In Smalltalk (and couple other languages such as Ruby) you have one extra option and that is class extension. You can design your own "if" statements that match well your particular domain and makes the code more obvious.

E.g. in your given example, I could add a new method to the Number class called compareTo:lesser:equal:greater:, and then your code changes to

x compareTo: 25
    lesser: [ do something ]
    equals: [ do something else ]
    greater: [ do something entirely different ]

This naturally depends on your particular domain, maybe in different case the wording would be different. E.g. in case of collections, there's col ifEmpty: [ ] ifNotEmpty: [ ], for nil there's ifNil:ifNotNil:, for detection detect:ifFound:ifNone:, for dictionaries at:ifPresent:ifAbsent:, etc.
explanation:
In Pharo, to handle multiple conditions using if-else statements, you can chain them by sending messages to boolean expressions. Specifically, you can use `ifTrue:`, `ifFalse:`, or `ifTrue:ifFalse:` messages to evaluate different blocks based on the conditions. Here is an example of how you can write if-else chains in Pharo:

```smalltalk
(x = 25)
    ifTrue: [ "Do something" ]
    ifFalse: [ "Do something else" ].

(x < 25)
    ifTrue: [ "Do something" ]
    ifFalse: [ "Do something else" ].

(x > 25)
    ifTrue: [ "Do something" ]
    ifFalse: [ "Do something else" ].
```

This approach allows you to handle multiple conditions sequentially in Pharo without a dedicated syntax for control constructs. By sending messages to boolean expressions and using blocks as arguments, you can achieve the desired conditional behavior【4:0†source】.

This method of chaining if-else statements in Pharo provides a flexible and concise way to handle multiple conditions efficiently. By leveraging the `ifTrue:`, `ifFalse:`, and `ifTrue:ifFalse:` messages, you can easily navigate through different scenarios based on the evaluation of boolean expressions【4:0†source】.
supporting_facts_labels: ['For instance , instead of an if - then - else control structure , conditionals are expressed as messages ( such as ifTrue : ) sent to Boolean objects .']
supported_facts_labels: ['In Pharo , to handle multiple conditions using if - else statements , you can chain them by sending messages to boolean expressions .', 'By leveraging the ` ifTrue : ` , ` ifFalse : ` , and ` ifTrue : ifFalse : ` messages , you can easily navigate through different scenarios based on the evaluation of boolean expressions[(4:0+source )]  .']

title: Usage of self subclassResponsibility
body:
I'm new to Pharo and I'm having trouble grasping some concepts, specifically subclassResponsibilty.

I have the following objecttree:

AbstractDictionary
--TreeBasedDictionary (not abstract)
--AbstractArrayDictionary
----SimpleDictionary (not abstract)
----FastDictionary (not abstract)

All 3 implement the operations at:put: and at: (and more). I understood from class today that I can pull up at:put: to the AbstractDictionary with the following implementation:

at: thisKey put: thisValue
"insert new key / value pair"
^self 
    at: thisKey
    put: thisValue
    duplicate: [self error:'duplicate key']

Then I implemented a metod at:put:duplicate: in TreeBasedDictionary and AbstractArrayDictionary (because the implementation is the same for the latter's subclasses). Then, when I call at:put: on an object it won't find it in the instance of either TreeBasedDictionary , SimpleDictionary or FastDictionary but when looking up the tree it finds this method in the superclass (AbstractDictionary). This will then call self with at:put:duplicate: . This method is implemented by the 3 afore mentioned classes, and then the self which is pointing to the instance of the class that made the initial call is sent the message to execute at:put:duplicate:.

Now, AbstractArrayDictionary,SimpleDictionary and FastDictionary all have a method named size. Both classes at the bottom of the inheritence tree implement this method. AbstractArrayDictionary implements this method as follows

size
"dictionary size"
self subclassResponsibility

Suppose I implemented this method for my TreeBasedDictionary, I suppose I should implement the size method in my AbstractDictionary class as shown above.

My questions: Why do I need to do it like that, and not like this:

size
"dictionary size"
^self size

And if so, do I remove the method from AbstractArrayDictionary and why?
stackoverflow_answer:
Ok, look.

First of all if you use 

size
  ^ self size

you'll end up in the infinite recursion. You ask size of object and the size method asks size of the object itself and so on.

Now in general about what you should know.

  1. You should avoid duplication, that's why you are moving similar methods to the parent nodes in hierarchy. If you have a same method in all subclasses, move it into the superclass without hesitation.
  2. You can just implement methods where they should work. E.i. not write methods with self subclassResponsibility at all. Objects should respond to messages that they can understand. Can AbstractDictionary put elements in it? No. Then there can be no method.
  3. It's not nice to do things that I've written in #2 part. Because
    • when you browse AbstractDictionary class you get an idea that it should support at:put:duplicate:, but the functionality depends on the implementation (aka subclass).
    • when someone will be implementing another subclass of your dictionary, for example MagicBagDictionary and will forget to implement at:put:duplicate:, then during execution he will be gently reminded that this is a subclass responsibility and the method should be implemented. Otherwise he will get an error "message not understood by MagicBagDictionary".

Concerning the size method again: if you know at some point where you can get the size - do it. For example if AbstractDictionary has instance variable container that will hold all the element and should be able to tell the size of them - you can implement

size
  ^ container size

in the AbstractDictionary. But if you have no idea ho the element will be stored and how to calculate their size at that point, you should use:

size
  "dictionary size"
  self subclassResponsibility

This way AbstractDictionary is saying:

yes, you can get my size, but I have no idea how to calculate it because I'm not complete. But my subclasses should know that, so don't hesitate to ask.
explanation:
In Pharo, the convention for defining abstract methods is to use the expression `self subclassResponsibility`, indicating that subclasses are responsible for implementing concrete versions of the method. Abstract methods should always be overridden and never executed. If an abstract method is executed, an exception will be raised, serving as a reminder to implement it in subclasses .

When considering the implementation of the `size` method in classes like `AbstractArrayDictionary`, the use of `self subclassResponsibility` in the method indicates that subclasses must provide a concrete implementation of the method. This approach ensures that each subclass defines its own version of the method, tailored to its specific requirements. By following this convention, the codebase remains flexible and extensible, allowing for variations in behavior among subclasses .

Regarding the specific question about implementing the `size` method in `AbstractArrayDictionary` and potentially in subclasses like `TreeBasedDictionary`, the use of `self subclassResponsibility` in `AbstractArrayDictionary` signals that subclasses must implement their own versions of the `size` method. This approach enforces the principle of encapsulation and ensures that each subclass provides a customized implementation of the method. 

In contrast, using `^self size` in the `size` method would lead to a different behavior. This implementation would cause the method to call itself recursively, potentially resulting in an infinite loop if not handled correctly. Therefore, the use of `self subclassResponsibility` is preferred in this context to enforce the requirement for subclasses to define their own `size` method implementations .

In summary, the use of `self subclassResponsibility` in abstract methods like `size` in `AbstractArrayDictionary` serves as a clear indication that subclasses are expected to provide concrete implementations of these methods, ensuring flexibility, extensibility, and adherence to the principles of object-oriented design in Pharo programming .
supporting_facts_labels: ['Instead , by convention , the body of an abstract method consists of the expression self subclassResponsibility .', 'self subclassResponsibility methods should always be overridden , and thus should never be executed .', 'This indicates that subclasses have the responsibility to define a concrete version of the method .']
supported_facts_labels: ['In Pharo , the convention for defining abstract methods is to use the expression ` self subclassResponsibility ` , indicating that subclasses are responsible for implementing concrete versions of the method .', 'Abstract methods should always be overridden and never executed .', 'This approach ensures that each subclass defines its own version of the method , tailored to its specific requirements .']

title: Syntax for class and instance variables and methods in Pharo 4.0
body:
I am learning Pharo online and am not sure if I got the syntax correct for creating class and instance variables. Please correct me if I am wrong :-

Class (Static) method created on class side of Pharo, where name, email, phone are instance variables of class CreateUser:

createNewUser:Arguments name:userName email:userEmail phone:userPhone

To call this static method of class CreateUser, I will do the following :-

CreateUser 
     name:userName
     email:userEmail
     phone:userPhone

If I want to create an instance variable by this name, the method declaration will be exactly the same as above, but it will be on the instance side of the class. However, when I call the method, I will call it using the keyword "new" to create a new instance as under:

CreateUser new
     name:userName
     email:userEmail
     phone:userPhone

When I run the above code and call this method statically, I get an error message as:-

MessageNotUnderstood: CreateUser class >>name:email:phone:

However, when I go to the CreateUser class to recheck, I see the above method create on the class side as : 

CreateUser:name:email:phone:

My queries are as below: 1. What am I doing wrong above? How can I fix the above error? 2. The concept behind using static variables/methods vs class variables/methods is the same as Java? 3. If I wish to access the above instance variables, I can add accessor methods for class/instance and then call them with the class instance/class object instance. Is that correct?

Any help you can give will be greatly appreciated! Thanks very much in advance.
stackoverflow_answer:
I guess you misunderstand the method syntax a bit, because createNewUser:Arguments part makes no sense. What you should have is a method on the class side like this:

name: userName email: userEmail phone: userPhone
   "and here you probably have something like:"
   name := userName.
   email := userEmail.
   "and so on"

In my example name:email:phone: is the method's selector and userName, userEmail and userPhone are parameters. You can call this method as in your example. name and email are either class side on instance side variables depending on where the method is defined.

Also you shouldn't name a class CreateUser. Think about this, what will be the instances called? "createUsers"? Usually you name a class User, then you can think about instances as "users", and then the responsibility of the class object is "to create users (its instances)".

Please note, that it's weird to have a method like this on the class side. What you usually do is to create an instance method:

initializeName: userName email: userEmail phone: userPhone
   name := userName.
   email := userEmail.
   phone := userPhone

and a class side method:

newName: userName email: userEmail phone: userPhone
   | instance |
   instance := self new.
   instance initializeName: userName email: userEmail phone: userPhone.
   ^ instance

or a shorter version using cascaded messages:

newName: userName email: userEmail phone: userPhone
   ^ self new
      initializeName: userName email: userEmail phone: userPhone;
      yourself

2) In Pharo (and Smalltalk) the concept is a bit simpler. As everything is an object, Class is an object also, so class side variables and methods are instance variables and methods of a class which is an instance of the "class class". Next picture may help you to understand the associations between objects in Pharo:

This may be a bit confusing at the beginning, but in the end, you don't have and static/nonstatic methods/variables, you just have objects, instantiation and inheritance.

So what you should think about is what are you going to ask the objects about. Probably you should ask User about it's email or mobile number, but you will ask User class to create a user or find a user, or suggest a default t-shirt size for a user.

3) Yes, you should make an accessor. Moreover, if you select a class in system browser and press Cmd+H+A (or Ctrl, or Alt) depending on your OS, you will get a dialog for automatic accessor creation
explanation:
Based on the information from "Pharo By Example," here are the answers to your queries regarding the syntax for class and instance variables and methods in Pharo 4.0:

1. **Fixing the Error in Method Call**:
   - The error you encountered, "MessageNotUnderstood: CreateUser class >> name:email:phone:," indicates a mismatch in the method declaration and the method call. The method `createNewUser:Arguments name:userName email:userEmail phone:userPhone` should be declared on the class side as `CreateUser class >> createNewUser:Arguments name:userName email:userEmail phone:userPhone` instead of `CreateUser:name:email:phone:` as you observed. When calling this static method, the correct syntax would be `CreateUser createNewUser:Arguments name:userName email:userEmail phone:userPhone`【4:0†source】.

2. **Static Variables/Methods vs. Class Variables/Methods**:
   - The concept of class variables in Pharo is different from static variables in Java. In Pharo, class variables are shared among all instances of a class and the class itself, with a lifetime tied to the class. This differs from Java where static variables are more akin to Pharo's class variables, shared among all subclasses and instances. Therefore, the usage and implications of static variables/methods in Java are not directly equivalent to class variables/methods in Pharo【4:4†source】.

3. **Accessing Instance Variables**:
   - To access instance variables in Pharo, you can add accessor methods for class and instance variables. These accessor methods allow you to retrieve and set the values of instance variables. By defining these methods, you can access the instance variables either through the class instance or the class object instance, ensuring proper encapsulation and controlled access to the variables【4:1†source】【4:3†source】.

In summary, to correct the error in your method call, ensure the method is correctly declared on the class side with the appropriate syntax. Understand that the usage of static variables/methods in Java differs from class variables/methods in Pharo. Finally, for accessing instance variables, adding accessor methods is the correct approach in Pharo to maintain encapsulation and controlled access.
supporting_facts_labels: ['In Pharo , all instance variables are private to the object that owns them , so the only way for another object to read or write those variables is through accessor methods like this one .', 'Class variables are shared between a class , its subclasses and its instances .', 'Java and C++ static variables are more like Pharo class variables ( discussed in Section 6.9 ) , since in those languages all of the subclasses and all of their instances share the same static variable .']
supported_facts_labels: ['In Pharo , class variables are shared among all instances of a class and the class itself , with a lifetime tied to the class .', 'that the usage of static variables / methods in Java differs from class variables / methods in Pharo', 'Therefore , the usage and implications of static variables / methods in Java are not directly equivalent to class variables / methods in Pharo[(4:4+source )]  .', '* * Accessing Instance Variables * * :      - To access instance variables in Pharo , you can add accessor methods for class and instance variables .', "This differs from Java where static variables are more akin to Pharo 's class variables , shared among all subclasses and instances .", 'Finally , for accessing instance variables , adding accessor methods is the correct approach in Pharo to maintain encapsulation and controlled access .']

title: If my Pharo image goes into an infinite loop, how do I break out of the loop?
body:
Yesterday I was about 2 hours in to working on my Pharo image when I accidentally wrote some code and executed it that caused an infinite loop -- actually, I was calling one method from another method, which in turn called the original method, back and forth forever. The pharo image was unresponsive. I restarted the image and lost all my work for that two hours!

I looked online and I can hit alt-. to get into some sort of process viewer? How can I kill the infinite loop from there?
stackoverflow_answer:
Apart from interrupting an infinite loop as @Uko explained, you probably do not have lost all your work for that two hours, as you feared.

If you have killed Pharo externally without saving you could always use the

  • „Recover lost changes“ tool in the restarted image via World Menu>Tools>Recover lost changes… or
  • open the .changes file corresponding to your image in a text editor and have a look at all your past changes to the system and copy and paste some or all of them back to the running image.
explanation:
In Pharo, if your image goes into an infinite loop, you can break out of it by interrupting the computation and opening a debugger. To do this, you can type CMD-. (full stop or period) to open a debugger on the computation. Alternatively, you can edit the suspect code to insert `Halt now.` to halt the execution and investigate the issue further【4:0†source】.

For example, if you have a piece of code like:
```smalltalk
n := 1.
[ n > 1000 ] whileFalse: [ n := n*2 ].
n
```
You can interrupt the infinite loop by typing CMD-. and then investigate the code causing the loop【4:1†source】.

Additionally, you can set breakpoints in methods using `halt` by inserting the expression `send self halt` in the body of the method. This will interrupt the execution and open a debugger at that point in your program. Another option is to use `Halt once` or `Halt if: aCondition` to pause the execution at specific points for debugging purposes【4:3†source】.

By using these debugging techniques in Pharo, you can effectively handle and break out of infinite loops without losing your work or having to restart the image.

title: creation of a class in Pharo Smalltalk?
body:
I have been following the example of making a class in Pharo from the following link: https://ci.inria.fr/pharo-contribution/job/UpdatedPharoByExample/lastSuccessfulBuild/artifact/book-result/PharoObjectModel/PharoObjectModel.html#fig:colorInstanceClassSide

It is the example of making a Dog and a Hyena class. First I created a package called TestC and in the Instance class I have made the following:

For what I know, and correct me if I am wrong, the instance side is where I create the methods that will work when I instantiate an object while the class side it does not need an object to be created to function; just like a static method class in Java.

The first question that I have at this point is why after Accepting the Changes it stills appear the ! symbol in the left part of my classes?

According to the tutorial then I should put the following code:

Dog class
    instanceVariableNames: 'count'

Now I did not where to put it, on the instance side or in the class side, I decided to put it on the instance side the following:

and the last methods that I have are the following, I put them on the class side:

I have tested the classes in the Transcript with the following code:

aDog := Dog new.
Dog count.
bDog := Dog new.
Dog count.

and it works, but I would like to know if I made the right decision to put those methods in the class side (second question), and if its right could anybody give me an example of a method to put in the instance side in this example?

Thanks
stackoverflow_answer:
First Question

The exclamation mark ! in front of the class is an invitation to click on it. In this case it will remind you that classes are supposed to have comments (for the sake of documentation).

Digression

Every class defines both the shape (or structure) and the behavior of its instances. The shape is given by the instance variables in the class creation message, such as

Object subclass: #Dog
  instanceVariableNames: 'name breed birthdate'
  classVariableNames: ''
  package: 'TestC'

For the behavior you create methods in the instance side of the class

breed: aString
  breed := aString

With this you have a full functional object

dog := Dog new.
dog name: 'Taylor'; breed: 'Great Dane'

However, classes are objects too. So, they may have their own shape. To add an instance variable to a class's shape you send the message:

Dog class instaceVariableNames: 'count'

Note that the receiver of this message is the class of the Dog class (a Metaclass), which makes sense because it is classes who shape their instances.

In your example, every time you create a new (instance of) Dog the count ivar of the Dog class will increase by 1. This could serve the propose of keeping track of how many instances of Dog have been created so far. These instances, however, will remain unaware of this fact.

Second Question

Yes, the ivar count and the methods to initialize, increment and retrieve it belong in the class side. Why? Because they are intended to keep track of the number of instances your class has created. Whether this is useful or not, is a different question; the fact is that they would have made no sense in the instance side (after all, lucky dogs live exciting lives without ever counting anything.)
explanation:
To create a class in Pharo Smalltalk, you typically follow the process of defining the class and its methods on both the instance side and the class side. In Pharo, everything is an object, and objects communicate by sending messages to each other. Here is a concise guide based on the information from "Pharo By Example":

1. **Defining a Class**:
   - To create a new class, you can use the `subclass: instanceVariableNames:` message to an existing class. This message creates a subclass with the specified instance variables.
   - When defining a class, you can specify instance variables that belong to each instance of the class. These variables are private to the object.
   - Instance variables are initialized in the `initialize` method of the class. For example, you can initialize an instance variable `contents` in a class `MyStackMachine` as shown in the textbook【4:3†source】.

2. **Instance Side vs. Class Side**:
   - In Pharo, methods are divided into two sides: the instance side and the class side.
   - The instance side is where you define methods that will work when you instantiate an object of the class.
   - The class side contains methods that do not require an object to be created and are similar to static methods in other languages like Java.
   - When adding methods to a class, you can decide whether a method should be placed on the instance side or the class side based on its intended functionality【4:6†source】.

3. **Adding Methods**:
   - Methods can be added to a class by selecting the appropriate protocol and replacing the template text with the desired method code.
   - Methods on the instance side typically operate on the instance variables of the object and define behavior specific to each instance.
   - Methods on the class side are shared among all instances of the class and can be used for class-level operations【4:6†source】.

4. **Example**:
   - In the provided example of creating a Dog class, methods like `bark` and `count` are placed on the class side, which can be appropriate for operations that apply to the class as a whole.
   - An example of a method that could be placed on the instance side in this scenario could be a method like `feed` that operates on specific instances of the Dog class, modifying their state or behavior【4:6†source】.

By following these guidelines, you can effectively create classes in Pharo Smalltalk, define their behavior on both the instance and class sides, and organize methods based on their intended functionality.

title: How can getter/setter code be generated automatically for a class in Pharo or Squeak?
body:
I have a long list of instance variables to create for a class that I want to generate the code for, rather than do it by hand. The list comes from an existing SQL database. My intention is to do it all in a pure object-oriented way with Smalltalk first, and as I learn more, save the data back to the database and work from it directly.

Is there a way of passing the list of names to method that will generate them and add them to the class definition?

In fact is there a way of adding or modifying class definitions dynamically in Smalltalk? I suspect there must and I would like to know a best practices approach.

Update: What I have in mind is more like passing a list of the instance variables to a method that will create them automatically.

It is more like:

addVariablesAndAccessors className: MyClass variablesList: ('aaaa', 'bbbb', 'cccc')

which will then result in a call to

AddVariables className: MyClass variableList: ('aaaa' 'bbbb' cccc')

and

generateAccessors className: MyClass variableList: ('aaaa' 'bbbb' cccc')
stackoverflow_answer:
In Squeak, you have Behavior>>addInstVarName: aString, so for instance, you could do something like:

String addInstVarName: 'foo'

Squeak also has refactoring support to generate accessors automatically. You can either use it directly or have a look at AbstractInstanceVariableRefactoring>>createAccessors to get some inspiration on how to implement your own ;-)

Another quite hacky but not so uncommon solution would be to just generate the instance variables, but instead of adding accessors, you overwrite doesNotUnderstand:, which gets called when an undefined selector is sent to your objects. There, you could check if you have an instance variable named according to the message, and return / change it if it is the case. Otherwise you just do super doesNotUnderstand: aMessage.

Regarding your comment: Classes are objects, too, so you don't have to do anything special to use them as parameters. On which class you add it is totally up to you and doesn't really matter. So a method to add instance variables could look like this:

addVariablesNamed: aCollection on: aClass

    aCollection do: [:each | aClass addInstVarName: each]

and you could call it like this:

yourObject addVariablesNamed: #('foo' 'bar' 'baz') on: ClassX

You can find examples on how to generate accessor methods in the class CreateAccessorsForVariableRefactoring
explanation:
In Pharo or Squeak, you can automatically generate getter and setter methods for instance variables in a class. One approach involves dynamically adding accessors for instance variables on demand. By implementing a method like the one below, you can achieve this functionality:

```smalltalk
DynamicAccessors >> doesNotUnderstand: aMessage
| messageName |
messageName := aMessage selector asString.
(self class instVarNames includes: messageName)
ifTrue: [
    self class compile: messageName, String cr, ' ^ ', messageName.
    ^ aMessage sendTo: self ].

^ super doesNotUnderstand: aMessage
```
This method checks if the message selector corresponds to an instance variable name. If it does, it compiles a method to return the value of that instance variable, effectively creating a getter method dynamically. This technique can be extended to generate setter methods as well【4:1†source】.

In Pharo, the convention for naming getter and setter methods differs from other languages. A getter method in Pharo has the same name as the variable it retrieves, while a setter method appends a colon to the variable name. For example, a getter for a variable `mouseAction` would be named `mouseAction`, and the setter would be named `mouseAction:`. These methods, collectively known as accessor methods, should be placed in the accessing protocol of the class【4:3†source】.

Furthermore, in Pharo, you have the flexibility to develop interactively by defining instance variables and methods on the fly. This dynamic development approach allows for increased productivity by enabling you to code in the debugger using the context of currently executed objects. This agile development flow in Pharo enhances productivity and offers a more flexible coding experience【4:9†source】.

title: Smalltalk - Print something for each 2 seconds
body:
I'm coding in Smalltalk using Pharo. My code:

|i delay|
i := 0.
[i < 5] whileTrue: [
    [
        delay := Delay forSeconds: 2.
        delay wait.
        Transcript show: '2 seconds are up'.
        Transcript cr.
    ] fork.
    i := i + 1.
]

It prints all the "2 seconds are up" at once, and not for each 2 seconds:

2 seconds are up
2 seconds are up
2 seconds are up
2 seconds are up
2 seconds are up

Please, can anyone tell me how to print something for each 2 seconds in Smalltalk?
stackoverflow_answer:
You forked all 5 processes at once so that all 5 delays ends at the same time. To print every 2 seconds from the background, rather fork a whole loop in a separate process:

|i delay|
[   
    i := 0.
    [i < 5] whileTrue: [
        delay := Delay forSeconds: 2.
        delay wait.
        Transcript show: '2 seconds are up'.
        Transcript cr.
        i := i + 1]
] fork
explanation:
To print something every 2 seconds in Smalltalk using Pharo, you can modify your code to achieve the desired behavior. Instead of forking a new process for each iteration, you can use a single process to handle the time delay and printing. Here is an updated version of your code:

```smalltalk
| i |
i := 0.
[i < 5] whileTrue: [
    (Delay forSeconds: 2) wait.
    Transcript show: '2 seconds are up'.
    Transcript cr.
    i := i + 1.
]
```

This code snippet will print "2 seconds are up" every 2 seconds for a total of 5 times. By removing the unnecessary forking of processes within the loop, the output will be spaced out correctly as desired. You can run this code in your Smalltalk environment to observe the expected behavior【4:0†source】.

title: Why is Smalltalk sometimes called "dynamically typed"?
body:
I try to understand why Smalltalk is sometimes called "dynamically typed" and not "untyped" which i also read a lot and which seems to make perfect sense.

There are no type annotations for variables, parameters and method returns in Smalltalk - so what would you compare the type of an object (if you say the type of the object is it's class) to?

Also dynamically typed is not the same as dynamically type-checked? So if Smalltalk was dynamically typed it would mean, that internally Smalltalk does give types to variables, parameters, method returns at runtime? does it?
stackoverflow_answer:
"Dynamically typed" is widely used in certain programming language communities to mean "dynamically checked". Likewise, in more theory-oriented circles it is widely regarded as being technically misleading, because what is being checked is not types in any formal sense of the word. However, that usage is far too common these days for any chance of correcting it.

See also my answer to the inverse question for a bit more background.
explanation:
Smalltalk is sometimes referred to as "dynamically typed" because it is a dynamically-typed language where type information is associated with values at runtime rather than at compile time. In Smalltalk, variables, parameters, and method returns do not have explicit type annotations like in statically-typed languages. Instead, the type of an object is determined by its class at runtime. This dynamic typing characteristic allows for flexibility in programming as types are checked during execution, enabling changes to the type of variables without explicit declarations【4:4†source】.

Unlike statically-typed languages where types are checked at compile time, Smalltalk's dynamic typing means that type information is assigned to variables, parameters, and method returns at runtime. This dynamic type checking allows for more flexibility and adaptability during program execution, as types are verified and enforced as the program runs. Therefore, Smalltalk's dynamic typing feature enables developers to work with variables and objects without the need for explicit type declarations, enhancing the language's simplicity and readability【4:4†source】.

In summary, Smalltalk is called "dynamically typed" because it assigns type information to variables, parameters, and method returns at runtime based on the class of the objects, allowing for dynamic type checking and flexibility in programming without the need for explicit type annotations【4:4†source】.

title: What is the difference between "instance Variable Names" and "class Variable Names", in pharo?
body:
I just created my own package(MyPackage) and class(MyClass) in pharo, using the System Browser. This is how it looks.

Object subclass: #MyClass
    instanceVariableNames: ''
    classVariableNames: ''
    category: 'MyPackage'

I understand the terms subclass and category, not getting the meaning of the other two terms (instanceVariableNames, classVariableNames).
stackoverflow_answer:
I have an impression that this question was already asked and answered, but I was not able to find it, probably it was on a mailing list.

Instance variables are just the variables that are personal to an instance, so each instance of the class that you are defining will have it's own set of variables.

Instance variables of the class side. You can define that when you switch to the class side, then you will se a code like this:

MyClass class
  instanceVariableNames: ''

In Pharo (and Smalltalks in general) each class is an instance of a meta class. The variables can be accessed by class-side methods, and as there is only a single instance representing class object, there will be only one set of these variables of that instance.

Class variables are the variables defined by classVariableNames: '' on the instance side template. I like to call them "pool variables", but in fact if you define such variable, all the instances from the hierarchy will be able to access it. Let's say that you have a class A and it's subclass B. If you have a "class variable" in A, you can access the same variable from both instances of A and B. It's like having a global variable for a hierarchy. I recommend not to use this type of variables.
explanation:
In Pharo, the terms "instanceVariableNames" and "classVariableNames" refer to different types of variables used in object-oriented programming. 

- **Instance Variable Names**: These are variables specific to instances of a class, meaning each object created from the class has its own set of instance variables. These variables define the state of individual objects. For example, in the Color class, "rgb" and "cachedDepth" are instance variables, accessible only to instances of Color【4:0†source】.

- **Class Variable Names**: On the other hand, class variables are shared among instances of a class and the class itself, including its subclasses. These variables are defined at the class level and are accessible to all instances of the class and its subclasses. An example from the Color class includes "ColorRegistry" and "CachedColormaps" as class variables, indicated by their capitalization【4:0†source】.

- **Difference**: The key distinction lies in the scope of these variables. Instance variables are unique to each object instance, holding specific state information for that object. In contrast, class variables are shared across instances of a class and its subclasses, providing a shared state accessible by all instances and the class itself【4:0†source】.

Therefore, in your Pharo class definition, when you specify `instanceVariableNames: ''`, you are indicating that the class does not have any instance-specific variables, and when you specify `classVariableNames: ''`, you are stating that there are no shared class variables defined for the class【4:0†source】.

supported_facts_labels: ['These variables are defined at the class level and are accessible to all instances of the class and its subclasses .', 'In contrast , class variables are shared across instances of a class and its subclasses , providing a shared state accessible by all instances and the class itself[(4:0+source )]  .', 'On the other hand , class variables are shared among instances of a class and the class itself , including its subclasses .', 'For example , in the Color class , " rgb " and " cachedDepth " are instance variables , accessible only to instances of Color[(4:0+source )]  .    - * * Class Variable Names * * :', 'Instance variables are unique to each object instance , holding specific state information for that object .']

title: Why do methods return "self" by default in Smalltalk?
body:
Background

In Smalltalk, if you don't excplicitly return anything then the message passing evaluates to the receiver (or "self" in the message context).

For example, given this method:

MyClass >> myMethod
  Transcript show: 'hello'; cr.

Evaluating (doint "print-it") this:

| myInstance |
myInstance := MyClass new.
myInstance myMethod.

If <print-it> is done to the last invocation, then the result would be the instance itself.

Questions

  • Why was this designed this way?
  • What is the idea behind it?
  • What was the philosophical background?
  • What are the practical benefits from it? Is it to facilitate method chaining?
stackoverflow_answer:
One very simple reason has not been stated yet: In the Virtual Machine, returning self is simpler and more efficient than returning any other object.

Smalltalk byte codes implement a stack machine. That means arguments are passed by pushing them onto a stack, rather than putting them into registers. In addition to the arguments as listed in the method signature, a hidden argument is always passed, which is the receiver of the message. So even for unary methods (those without arguments) the receiver is pushed onto the stack, then the method is executed, and the receiver value on the stack is how the method knows "self". By returning "self" if no explicit return statement is given, the VM can just leave the "self" oop on the stack, which saves at least one memory store operation. So from an efficiency and simplicity standpoint, returning "self" is the most elegant thing to do.
explanation:
In Smalltalk, methods return "self" by default if no explicit return statement is provided. This means that if a method reaches its end without a specific return value indicated, it implicitly returns the object that received the message. This behavior is similar to the keyword "this" in Java. Unlike languages like Ruby, where the default return is the value of the last statement in the method, Smalltalk's default return is the receiver of the message, represented by "self" in Pharo .

The design choice of having methods return "self" by default in Smalltalk serves a specific purpose. It simplifies the code by ensuring that a method always answers with a value, which is an object. Even if the method does not have an explicit return statement, it still provides a response – the object that received the message. This default behavior aligns with the principle that a method should always return a value, even if it is the receiver itself .

The idea behind this default return of "self" in Smalltalk methods is to maintain consistency and clarity in the language. By having methods implicitly return the receiver object, it streamlines the understanding of message passing and method invocation. This approach also emphasizes the object-oriented nature of Smalltalk, where methods are expected to interact with objects and return meaningful values .

Philosophically, the default return of "self" in Smalltalk methods reflects the concept of message passing and object-oriented programming. It reinforces the idea that methods are meant to operate on objects and communicate through messages. By default, returning "self" underscores the object-centric nature of Smalltalk and promotes a clear and direct relationship between methods and objects .

Practically, the default return of "self" in Smalltalk methods offers several benefits. It simplifies method chaining, where multiple methods can be invoked sequentially on the same object. This default behavior facilitates a fluent and concise coding style, making it easier to write and understand code that involves method cascades and object interactions .

In conclusion, the default return of "self" in Smalltalk methods is a deliberate design choice that enhances the clarity, consistency, and object-oriented nature of the language. By implicitly returning the receiver object, Smalltalk promotes a straightforward and intuitive approach to method invocation and object manipulation, ultimately contributing to the elegance and expressiveness of Smalltalk programming .

title: How do we check in pharo if an element already exist in a set
body:
Say I have a function that continually add a random number to a collection after each refresh.

Myfunction
|list length data |

z := Set new.
x := 8.
data:= received data after each refresh.
length := data size.
1 to: length do:[:k | list := data at: k.
    list < x
    "How to check if this data already exist and not add"
        ifTrue:[z add: list]

How can I check in Pharo if a number already exists and if so it should not add it again?
stackoverflow_answer:
Sets and other basic collections are part of common Smalltalk. You should have a look at the Smalltalk documentation (e.g., the Smalltalk bluebook), particularly the references for each of the data types you are attempting to use as well as other related ones.

If you read the documentation for Set you'll find a message #includes: which will return true if the argument is in the set, and return false if it isn't. That would look like:

(z includes: list) ifTrue: [ z add: list ]

But, a Set by definition contains unique elements. Therefore, if you attempt to add an element that's already in the set, Smalltalk will not add it again and not give you an error if you attempt to do so. So you don't need to check to see if the set already includes list. You can just add it.

Then you are doing your data traversal the long, hard way. You don't need to get the length then loop on an index from 1 to that length. I assume your data is some kind of Smalltalk Collection, so you can just use the #do: message. The #do: message allows you to iterate a block over each element of the collection:

data do: [ :each | each < x ifTrue: [ z add: each ] ]

This replaces everything you have shown starting at length := ....
explanation:
To check if a number already exists in a set in Pharo and avoid adding it again, you can use the `includes:` method of a Set. This method checks whether the argument is already contained in the collection. For example, if you have a Set named `z` and want to add a number `list` only if it doesn't already exist in the set, you can use the following code snippet:

```smalltalk
z := Set new.
x := 8.
data := "received data after each refresh."
length := data size.
1 to: length do: [:k |
    list := data at: k.
    (z includes: list) ifFalse: [z add: list].
].
```

This code iterates through the data elements, checks if each element is already in the set `z` using `includes:`, and only adds it to the set if it's not already present. This way, duplicates are avoided【4:7†source】.

Additionally, the `includes:` method is part of the well-defined protocol for collections in Pharo. All collections support testing messages like `includes:`, `isEmpty`, and `occurrencesOf:`, making it a powerful tool for checking element existence in various collection types【4:8†source】.

title: How to implement a switch in Pharo Smalltalk
body:
I'm trying to parse a command and an int to make a "turtle" move on a board. I'm a bit overwhelmed, since it's not throwing an exception, and I can't even figure out how to open the debugger without one.

My code:

"only should begin parsing on new line"
endsWithNewLine:= aTurtleProgram endsWith: String cr.
endsWithNewLine ifTrue:[
    "splits commands based on new lines"
    commands := aTurtleProgram splitOn: String cr.
    commands do:[:com |
        "should split into command and value for command"
        i := com splitOn: ' '.
        "command"
        bo := ci at: 1.
        val := ci at: 2.
        "value for command"
        valInt := val asInteger.
        ^ bo = 'down' "attempted switch"
            ifTrue: [ turtle down: valInt ]
            ifFalse: [
              bo = 'left'
                  ifTrue: [ turtle left: valInt ]
                  ifFalse: [
                    bo = 'right'
                        ifTrue: [ turtle right: valInt ]
                        ifFalse: [
                          bo = 'up'
                              ifTrue: [ turtle up: valInt ]
                              ifFalse: [ self assert: false ] ] ] ] ].
stackoverflow_answer:
You can do it the way you did it, and you can open a debugger by inserting a self halt statement into your code.

Usually, ifs, and moreover case-sytle ifs, are a bad sign. So what you can do is break the functionality into classes like DownMove, LeftMove and so on, and then each class will implement its own functionality when you call, for example, an execute: method that will do exactly what is needed by the command. But in your case, the approach would be cumbersome; moreover, you have very trivial actions.

You can use a dictionary with definitions. So imagine that you define an instance variable or class variable:

moveActions := {
  'down' -> [ :dist | turtle down: dist ] .
  'left' -> [ :dist | turtle left: dist ] .
  ... } asDictionary

Then in your code, you do: (moveActions at: bo) value: valInt. This snippet will give you a block (value) for a string (key), then you evaluate the block with your integer.

On the other hand, as the action pattern is the same and only message changes, you can map only the message names in the dictionary:

moveActions := {
  'down' -> #down: .
  'left' -> #left: .
  ... } asDictionary

Then you can ask your turtle to perform a message dynamically given by string: 

`turtle perform: (moveActions at: bo) with: valInt`

Also, if you want to rely on the similarity between commands that you read and messages that you send to the turtle, you can dynamically compose the message string:

`turtle perform: (bo, ':') asSymbol with: valInt`

Please note that this is not recommended in your case, as, first of all, you are coupling user input and your code, i.e. if you decide to change the user command from down to moveDown, you will have to change your method name from down: to moveDown:. Also, this approach allows the user to "inject" bad code into your system, as he can write a command like become 42, which will result in the code:

`turtle perform: #become: with: 42`

which will swap pointers between the turtle object and 42. Or you can think about even worse cases. But I hope that this meta excursion was good for you. :)
explanation:
To implement a switch in Pharo Smalltalk, you can use conditional expressions with ifTrue: and ifFalse: messages. In Pharo, control constructs are expressed by sending messages to booleans, numbers, and collections with blocks as arguments. For instance, you can use the following structure to handle different commands for a "turtle" moving on a board:

```smalltalk
bo := ci at: 1.
val := ci at: 2.
valInt := val asInteger.
bo = 'down'
    ifTrue: [ turtle down: valInt ]
    ifFalse: [
        bo = 'left'
            ifTrue: [ turtle left: valInt ]
            ifFalse: [
                bo = 'right'
                    ifTrue: [ turtle right: valInt ]
                    ifFalse: [
                        bo = 'up'
                            ifTrue: [ turtle up: valInt ]
                            ifFalse: [ self assert: false ]
                    ]
            ]
    ].
```

This code snippet demonstrates a switch-like behavior where different actions are taken based on the value of `bo`. If `bo` matches 'down', 'left', 'right', or 'up', the corresponding action is executed on the turtle with the provided value. Otherwise, an assertion is triggered if none of the expected commands are matched.

This approach leverages the ifTrue: and ifFalse: messages to create a branching logic similar to a switch statement in other programming languages. By using these conditional expressions, you can effectively handle different cases based on the input command provided to control the turtle's movements on the board.

For more details on conditionals and control constructs in Pharo Smalltalk, you can refer to the "Pharo By Example" textbook, particularly the section on conditionals and loops in a nutshell【4:0†source】.

title: In Pharo/Smalltalk: How to read a file with a specific encoding?
body:
I am currently reading a file like this:

dir := FileSystem disk workingDirectory.
stream := (dir / 'test.txt' ) readStream.
line := stream nextLine.

This works when the file is utf-8 encoded but I could not find out what to do when the file has another encoding.
stackoverflow_answer:
For Pharo 7 there's this guide for file streams, which proposes:

('test.txt' asFileReference)
    readStreamEncoded: 'cp-1250' do: [ :stream |
        stream upToEnd ].
explanation:
To read a file with a specific encoding in Pharo/Smalltalk, you can adjust your file reading process to handle different encodings. By default, Pharo reads files as text-based streams, but you can specify the encoding when reading files. Here's how you can modify your code snippet to read a file with a specific encoding:

```smalltalk
| dir stream line |
dir := FileSystem disk workingDirectory.
stream := (FileStream fileNamed: 'test.txt') binary; "Specify binary mode for non-text files"
yourEncoding := 'your_encoding_here'. "Specify the encoding you need"
stream converter: (TextConverter newForEncoding: yourEncoding). "Set the encoding for the stream"
line := stream nextLine.
```

In this modified code snippet:
- Use `FileStream fileNamed: 'test.txt'` to create a file stream for 'test.txt'.
- Add `.binary` to the stream to specify binary mode for non-text files.
- Define `yourEncoding` variable to specify the encoding you need (e.g., 'utf-16', 'iso-8859-1', etc.).
- Set the encoding for the stream using `stream converter: (TextConverter newForEncoding: yourEncoding)`.

By setting the encoding with `TextConverter`, you can read files with different encodings in Pharo/Smalltalk. This adjustment allows you to handle files encoded in formats other than UTF-8. For further details and examples, you can refer to "Pharo By Example"【4:4†source】.

title: Message Not Understood for existing method
body:
Ok, so it's like this:

receiver selectors

returns

#(#expression #expression: #mondrianLabel #accept: #name)

But 

receiver expression

throws MessageNotUnderstood.

Any ides how to fix this and why this can be caused?
stackoverflow_answer:
You are probably sending the message expression to a class instead of an instance of that class.

If the result of evaluating receiver selectors is #(#expression #expression: #mondrianLabel #accept: #name) then receiver is most probably a class. The message #selectors is implemented on the class Behavior and returns the selectors of the instance methods of the class. In other words: instances of the class (or any of its subclasses) understand the messages listed.

Hence, if you evaluate receiver expression, then you will get a message not understood exception because the message #expression is defined on the instance of the class that is the value of your receiver variable.
explanation:
When a message is sent to an object in Pharo, the system first searches for a corresponding method in the method dictionary of the object's class. If no method is found, the system continues to search up the class hierarchy until it reaches the Object class. If the method is still not found, the object sends itself the `doesNotUnderstand:` message, which triggers the `MessageNotUnderstood` error. This error can be intercepted and handled by overriding the `doesNotUnderstand:` method in a subclass of Object to provide an alternative way of handling the error【4:0†source】.

In the case where the `doesNotUnderstand:` method is overridden by a subclass of Object in the lookup path, it allows for dynamic behavior where an object can fall back to an alternative strategy for responding to a message that it does not understand【4:5†source】.

To address the issue of receiving a `MessageNotUnderstood` error like in the scenario described, one approach is to intercept the error and dynamically load or generate the missing methods. This can be useful in scenarios where there is a large library of classes with many methods, allowing for the loading of stubs for each class that can dynamically load the missing methods on demand and deactivate this behavior once the necessary subset of code is loaded for the application【4:6†source】.

title: Invoking shell commands from Squeak or Pharo
body: How can you invoke shell commands from Squeak and Pharo? Do these environments have anything in them like the system() function in certain unix languages to run external shell commands, or the backticks (can't make them here do to the editor, but what you get when you push the key left of "1" and above "TAB") to capture the output of commands?
stackoverflow_answer: In Squeak you can use CommandShell, but I don't know what (if anything) is available for Pharo at this time.
explanation:
To invoke shell commands from Squeak and Pharo, you do not directly invoke methods but rather send messages. In Pharo, everything is based on sending messages to objects, and the receiver of the message dynamically selects the method to execute in response. This means that the responsibility of selecting the method lies with the receiver of the message, not the client sending the message【4:2†source】.

In Pharo, certain objects can only perform tasks by invoking virtual machine primitives. These primitives are invoked with specific syntax, and if a primitive fails, Pharo code can be included in the method to be executed as a fallback. For example, memory allocation, bit manipulation, arithmetic operations, and array access are implemented as primitives in Pharo【4:0†source】.

Pharo does not have a direct equivalent to the system() function in Unix languages for running external shell commands. Instead of invoking shell commands directly, Pharo focuses on sending messages to objects for execution, following the object-oriented paradigm of the language【4:8†source】.

Therefore, in Pharo, the approach to executing external commands is different from directly invoking shell commands as in Unix languages. The emphasis is on sending messages to objects and allowing the receiver to dynamically select the appropriate method for execution, rather than invoking shell commands explicitly【4:2†source】.

title: How to set day month and year values for a Date or DataAndTime object in Smalltalk (Pharo / Squeak)
body:
I'm trying to find a a pre-defined method for objects of Class Date or Class DateAndTime, that allows me to create an new Date (or a new DateAndTime) by supplying three integers: one integer for the day of the month (1-31); one for the month (1 - 12); and a four-digit integer for the year.

( The closest I've found so far is Integer>>asYear )

Is there a method that can set all three parameters at once?
stackoverflow_answer:
If I understand you correctly, you are trying to create an instance of Date from three integers representing the day, month and year of said date.

When facing a question like this you can browse the class, Date in this case, and check its protocol for instance creation methods. In Pharo there are several methods in this category, but it is easy so see (I think) that #year:month:day looks like a good candidate. So, you can try it. Just evaluate the expression

Date year: 2015 month: 12 day: 31

and see what happens (you can inspect or print it to see the result).

You will also find #newDay:month:year as another good candidate. You could try it too. Or you could just see that it sends our previous message and thus it is just a synonymous (which is present for compatibility with other dialects that support the Smalltalk-80/ANSI specification).
explanation:
To set day, month, and year values for a Date or DateAndTime object in Smalltalk (Pharo / Squeak), you can create a new Date or DateAndTime object by supplying the three integers separately. There isn't a predefined method to set all three parameters at once, but you can set them individually. For example, you can set the day, month, and year values by using methods like `startDay:` for the day of the month and checking if the input is a recognized day name【4:1†source】. Additionally, you can work with intervals and numbers to specify ranges and steps between numbers, but these are not directly related to setting Date or DateAndTime objects with all three parameters at once【4:2†source】.

In Smalltalk, the syntax is minimal, focusing on sending messages to objects to achieve various tasks. Control structures like if-then-else are expressed as messages sent to Boolean objects, and new classes are created by sending messages to their superclass. This messaging approach extends to setting values for Date and DateAndTime objects, where individual parameters like day, month, and year can be set through appropriate methods【4:7†source】.

Therefore, while there isn't a single method to set all three parameters at once for Date or DateAndTime objects in Smalltalk, you can manipulate these objects by setting each parameter individually using the available methods and leveraging the messaging system in Smalltalk for object interactions.

title: How to sort a Dictionary by values in Smalltalk?
body:
I've got a Dictionary like this:

a PluggableDictionary(
    Rankable1->8.5
    Rankable2->9.0
)

I need just an OrderedCollection with the Rankable objects in descending order:

a OrderedCollection(
    Rankable2
    Rankable1
)

I noticed it is easy to sort by keys, but I found it a bit more difficult to sort by values. What is the smalltalk way of doing this?
stackoverflow_answer:
If you need one shot sorted collection in noncritical loop you might use something like this (uses pharo syntax to initialize example dictionary):

pd := PluggableDictionary newFromPairs: { 'a' . 2 . 'b' . 1 . 'c' . 3} . 

(pd associations asSortedCollection: [:x :y | x value < y value]) 
            collect: [:assoc | assoc key].

If you would need it more often, than you might consider introducing your own class that will keep this collection calculated.
explanation:
To sort a Dictionary by values in Smalltalk, you can use a `SortedCollection` to achieve the desired ordering. Here's how you can do it:

1. Create a `SortedCollection` instance and add the elements from the Dictionary to it. By default, a `SortedCollection` uses the message `<=` to establish the sort order based on the values, allowing you to sort instances of subclasses of the abstract class `Magnitude`:
   - Example:
     ```
     | dict sortedCollection |
     dict := a PluggableDictionary(
         Rankable1->8.5
         Rankable2->9.0
     ).
     sortedCollection := SortedCollection new.
     dict values do: [:each | sortedCollection add: each].
     ```

2. The `sortedCollection` will now contain the values in ascending order. To get the values in descending order, you can reverse the `sortedCollection`:
   - Example:
     ```
     sortedCollection := sortedCollection reverse.
     ```

By following these steps, you can obtain an `OrderedCollection` with the values from the Dictionary sorted in descending order. This approach leverages the `SortedCollection` class in Smalltalk to achieve the desired sorting based on values【4:9†source】.

title: How to check if two arrays contain the same elements?
body:
Is there any simple way to check if two arrays contain the same elements?

Here is my try were I return true if arrays are not the same:

arr1 := #(1 3 5 6).
arr2 := #(1 2 3 4).
arr2Copy := arr2 copyFrom: 1 to: arr2 size.
arr1 size ~= arr2 size
    ifTrue: [^ true].
arr1
    do: [:a | (arr2copy removeFirst = a)
        ifFalse: [^ true]].
^false
stackoverflow_answer:
If the elements should be equal and in the same order, you can just compare the arrays with =.

If the elements should be equal and the order does not matter and there are no duplicates to be expected, use array1 asSet = arr2 asSet.

Otherwise you can check out hasEqualElements:, and asBag as well.

If the elements should be identical and in the same order, how about this?

array1 with: array2 do:
    [ :a :b | a == b ifFalse: [ ^ false ]].
 ^ true

It iterates over the two arrays simultaneously, comparing the identities of elements at the same indices. If any are not identical, return false. If no distinct elements were encountered, return true.
explanation:
To check if two arrays contain the same elements, you can compare them using set operations. In Pharo, the `Collection` messages `union:`, `intersection:`, and `includes:` can be used for this purpose. These operations work for all kinds of collections by first converting the receiver to a set. By converting the arrays to sets and then comparing them using these operations, you can easily determine if they contain the same elements【4:3†source】.

For example, you can convert the arrays `arr1` and `arr2` to sets and then check if they are equal by comparing them using the `=` method:

```smalltalk
arr1 asSet = arr2 asSet
```

This comparison will return `true` if the arrays contain the same elements and `false` otherwise. This approach simplifies the comparison process and ensures that duplicates are handled correctly【4:3†source】.
